topics.title: Fuel Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT721 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fuel Cell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Fuel Cell — KTU Board Definition**
> A **fuel cell** is a **galvanic (electrochemical) energy conversion device** that converts the **chemical energy of a fuel (commonly hydrogen)** and an **oxidant (commonly oxygen)** directly into **electrical energy (DC)**, with **water and heat** as the primary by-products, through a process that is **not bounded by the Carnot cycle limit**. Unlike a battery, a fuel cell is an **open system** — reactants are supplied externally, and products are removed continuously, allowing sustained operation as long as fuel and oxidant are fed.

The general simplified overall reaction for a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell is:

$$2H_2 + O_2 \longrightarrow 2H_2O + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "A Battery That Refills Itself"

> [!NOTE] **Intuitive Analogy (Water-Bucket Model)**
> Think of a **conventional battery** as a *closed water bucket* — it stores a fixed amount of chemical energy, dumps it out as electricity, and then dies when the bucket is empty. A **fuel cell**, in contrast, is a *continuously topped-up water channel* — you pour hydrogen in on one side, oxygen on the other, and out comes electricity plus water, with no "empty" state. As long as you keep pouring, it keeps producing.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Faraday's Constant (F):** **96,485 C mol⁻¹ (≈ 96,500 C mol⁻¹)**
- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**
- **Standard Temperature (T):** **298.15 K (25 °C)**
- **Standard Pressure (P):** **1 atm (101.325 kPa)**
- **Reversible Cell Voltage (E°):** **1.229 V (theoretical) at 25 °C for H₂/O₂**

### 1.4 Why Fuel Cells Matter in KTU Context

> [!IMPORTANT] **Syllabus Highlight — Module 1 Scope**
> Module 1 of PECHT721 establishes the *foundational electrochemical language* of fuel cells: EMF, Nernst equation, Gibbs free energy, enthalpy, theoretical vs. practical efficiency. Every later module (fuel processing, stacks, applications) rests on this thermodynamic base. **Mastering Module 1 = mastering 60 % of Part A questions in ESE.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A fuel cell has three primary components:

1. **Anode (Fuel Electrode)** — oxidation half-reaction occurs.
2. **Cathode (Oxidant Electrode)** — reduction half-reaction occurs.
3. **Electrolyte** — conducts ions (H⁺, O²⁻, OH⁻, or CO₃²⁻ depending on type) but blocks electrons, forcing them through the external circuit.

**For an Acidic (PEM) Fuel Cell — half-reactions:**

- **Anode (Oxidation):** $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$
- **Cathode (Reduction):** $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$
- **Net:** $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$

**For an Alkaline Fuel Cell:**

- **Anode (Oxidation):** $H_2 + 2OH^- \rightarrow 2H_2O + 2e^-$
- **Cathode (Reduction):** $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow 2OH^-$

> [!NOTE] **'Why' the Electrons Go Around the External Circuit**
> The electrolyte is **ionically conductive but electronically insulating**. This is the cleverest design feature — it *forces* the electrons liberated at the anode to travel the long way through the external load, performing useful electrical work, instead of short-circuiting internally.

### 2.2 Thermodynamic Foundation

The **maximum reversible electrical work** a fuel cell can deliver equals the **Gibbs free energy change** of the overall reaction:

$$W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G \quad (\text{J mol}^{-1})$$

For the standard H₂/O₂ reaction at 25 °C:

$$\Delta G° = -237.13 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

The **reversible (theoretical) cell voltage** is then:

$$E° = -\dfrac{\Delta G°}{nF}$$

where **n = 2** (electrons transferred per mole of H₂).

$$E° = \dfrac{237{,}130}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 1.229 \text{ V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU High-Yield Distinction — ΔG vs. ΔH**
> - **ΔG (Gibbs Free Energy)** → governs **electrical work output** → used to compute **E°**
> - **ΔH (Enthalpy)** → governs **total heat content of reaction** → used to compute **thermo-neutral voltage (E_th = 1.481 V)**
> - **ΔH − ΔG = TΔS** → the **irreversible heat loss**, even in an ideal cell.

### 2.3 Efficiency of a Fuel Cell

**Theoretical (reversible) electrical efficiency:**

$$\eta_{rev} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = \dfrac{E°}{E_{th}} = \dfrac{1.229}{1.481} = 0.83 \;\; (83\%)$$

**Practical fuel cell efficiency:**

$$\eta_{FC} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{E_{th}} \times 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why 83 % Beats Carnot**
> A fuel cell is **not** a heat engine. There is **no combustion → heat → mechanical → electrical** chain. Energy is converted **isothermally and electrochemically**, so the Carnot limit (1 − T_cold/T_hot) does **not** apply. This is why fuel cells can theoretically approach 80–85 % electrical efficiency — far above IC engines (30–40 %).

### 2.4 Nernst Equation (Non-Standard Conditions)

When reactants/products are not at standard state, the cell EMF is:

$$E = E° + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$

At **298 K** (using $2.303\,RT/F = 0.0592$ V):

$$E = E° + \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log\!\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Key Notes |
|---|----------|---------|------|-----------|
| 1 | Reversible EMF | $E° = -\Delta G / (nF)$ | V | Theoretical max voltage |
| 2 | Thermo-neutral EMF | $E_{th} = -\Delta H / (nF)$ | V | Total energy of reaction |
| 3 | Nernst (general form) | $E = E° + (RT/nF)\ln Q$ | V | Non-standard conditions |
| 4 | Nernst (298 K) | $E = E° + (0.0592/n)\log Q$ | V | For quick numerical work |
| 5 | Reversible Efficiency | $\eta_{rev} = \Delta G / \Delta H = E° / E_{th}$ | — | Max 83 % for H₂/O₂ |
| 6 | Practical Efficiency | $\eta_{FC} = V_{cell} / E_{th}$ | — | Always < 83 % |
| 7 | Electrical Work | $W = -\Delta G = nFE$ | J mol⁻¹ | Useful work output |
| 8 | Heat Rejection | $Q = T\Delta S = \Delta G - \Delta H$ | J mol⁻¹ | Irreversible heat |
| 9 | Fuel Cell Power | $P = V_{cell} \times I$ | W | Stack output |
| 10 | Cell Voltage Loss | $V_{cell} = E - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$ | V | Polarization components |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stationary power plants** (PEMFC, SOFC): grid backup, distributed generation.
- **Automotive traction:** Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO (PEMFC, 100+ kW).
- **Portable electronics & UAVs:** lightweight PEMFC stacks.
- **Space missions:** Apollo, Space Shuttle used AFC for water + electricity.
- **Combined Heat & Power (CHP):** SOFC at 800 °C achieves 85 % total efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Reversible Cell Voltage from First Principles

**Step 1 — Statement of the First Law of Thermodynamics for a galvanic cell:**

The total free-energy change of a chemical reaction partitions into **electrical work** + **heat**:

$$\Delta G = W_{elec} + Q_{rev}$$

For a reversible (no-loss) process, $Q_{rev} = T\Delta S$, and electrical work equals charge × EMF × moles:

**Step 2 — Express electrical work in terms of EMF and charge:**

$$W_{elec} = -nFE \quad \text{(negative sign: system does work)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the free-energy balance:**

$$\Delta G = -nFE + T\Delta S$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the reversible EMF E:**

$$E = -\dfrac{\Delta G}{nF} + \dfrac{T\Delta S}{nF}$$

At standard state ($\Delta G = \Delta G°$, $\Delta S = \Delta S°$, T = 298.15 K):

**Step 5 — Compute numerical value for the H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O(l) reaction:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta G° &= -237{,}130 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \\
\Delta H° &= -285{,}830 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \\
T\Delta S° &= \Delta G° - \Delta H° = -237{,}130 - (-285{,}830) = 48{,}700 \text{ J mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Final substitution for E°:**

$$E° = -\dfrac{(-237{,}130)}{2 \times 96{,}485} = \dfrac{237{,}130}{192{,}970} = 1.2287 \text{ V}$$

**Step 7 — Compute thermo-neutral voltage E_th:**

$$E_{th} = -\dfrac{\Delta H°}{nF} = \dfrac{285{,}830}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 1.4812 \text{ V}$$

**Step 8 — Reversible efficiency:**

$$\eta_{rev} = \dfrac{1.2287}{1.4812} = 0.8294 = 82.94\%$$

> [!NOTE] **Examiner's Insight**
> The number **1.229 V** is one of the most-tested constants in KTU ESE. Memorize it. The number **1.481 V** is the "upper limit" — you can never get more electrical work per mole of H₂ than what 1.481 V implies at 100 % conversion (and not even that, because entropy always takes its cut).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Nernst Equation from Gibbs Free Energy

**Step 1 — General relationship between ΔG and the reaction quotient Q:**

For the reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G° + RT \ln Q$$

where $Q = \dfrac{[C]^c [D]^d}{[A]^a [B]^b}$ (activities, or partial pressures for gases).

**Step 2 — Substitute the identity $\Delta G = -nFE$:**

$$-nFE = -nFE° + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 3 — Divide throughout by −nF:**

$$E = E° - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Step 4 — Invert the log by using $a - \ln Q = \ln(1/Q)$, but for KTU convention we write it as:**

$$E = E° + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{Reactants}]}{[\text{Products}]}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Convert natural log to log₁₀ using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:**

$$E = E° + \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log\!\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$

**Step 6 — At T = 298 K, evaluate the prefactor:**

$$\dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96{,}485} = 0.05916 \text{ V}$$

**Step 7 — Final Nernst equation (KTU-ESE friendly form):**

$$\boxed{E = E° + \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log\!\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A PEM fuel cell operates at 80 °C (353 K) with P(H₂) = 1.5 atm, P(O₂) = 0.5 atm, and P(H₂O) = 0.1 atm. Calculate the reversible cell EMF. Given: ΔG°(353 K) ≈ −228.2 kJ mol⁻¹, n = 2.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E° &= -\dfrac{\Delta G°}{nF} \\
&= -\dfrac{(-228{,}200)}{2 \times 96{,}485} \\
&= \dfrac{228{,}200}{192{,}970} \\
&= 1.1825 \text{ V} \quad \text{(standard EMF at 80 °C)}
\end{aligned}$$

Now apply the Nernst correction:

$$Q = \dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} = \dfrac{1.5 \times (0.5)^{0.5}}{0.1} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 0.7071}{0.1} = 10.607$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= E° + \dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96{,}485} \times \log(10.607) \\
&= 1.1825 + \dfrac{5{,}406.4}{192{,}970} \times 1.0256 \\
&= 1.1825 + 0.02801 \times 1.0256 \\
&= 1.1825 + 0.02873 \\
&= 1.2112 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Answer:** $E_{rev} \approx 1.211 \text{ V at 80 °C}$

---

### 3.4 Polarization Curve (V–I Characteristic) — Loss Analysis

The actual cell voltage always falls below E_rev due to three irreversible losses:

$$V_{cell}(I) = E_{rev} - \eta_{act}(I) - \eta_{ohm}(I) - \eta_{conc}(I)$$

| Loss Type | Cause | Mitigation |
|-----------|-------|------------|
| **Activation (η_act)** | Sluggish electrode kinetics (esp. ORR) | Catalysts (Pt), higher T |
| **Ohmic (η_ohm)** | Ionic + electronic resistance | Thinner electrolyte, better conductors |
| **Concentration (η_conc)** | Mass transport limit at high current | Better flow fields, higher pressure |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Generic H₂/O₂ Fuel Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H2in["Hydrogen Inlet"] --> anodeA["Anode Side"]
    O2in["Oxygen / Air Inlet"] --> cathodeC["Cathode Side"]
    anodeA -->|H2 oxidation| electroA["Electrode Reaction Zone"]
    cathodeC -->|O2 reduction| electroC["Electrode Reaction Zone"]
    electroA --> elec["Electrolyte Layer"]
    electroC --> elec
    elec -->|Ion transport| electroA
    anodeA -->|Electron flow| extC["External Circuit and Load"]
    extC -->|Electron return| cathodeC
    extC --> pOut["DC Electrical Power Output"]
    electroA -->|H plus ions| elec
    electroC -->|Water formation| wOut["Water and Heat Byproducts"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Energy Conversion Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Chemical Energy in Fuel H2"] --> B["Electrochemical Conversion at Anode"]
    B --> C["Ion Migration through Electrolyte"]
    C --> D["Electrochemical Conversion at Cathode"]
    D --> E["DC Electrical Energy Delivered to Load"]
    B --> F["Activation Loss Region"]
    D --> G["Concentration Loss Region"]
    C --> H["Ohmic Loss Region"]
    F --> I["Heat Loss Q out of Cell"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    D --> J["Water and Heat Byproduct Stream"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Voltage Loss Stack (Polarization Decomposition)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Erev["Reversible EMF E rev approx 1.23 V"] --> lossA["Activation Loss eta act"]
    lossA --> lossB["Ohmic Loss eta ohm"]
    lossB --> lossC["Concentration Loss eta conc"]
    lossC --> Vcell["Actual Cell Voltage V cell approx 0.6 to 0.8 V"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Thermodynamic-to-Electrical Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dH["Enthalpy Change dH"] --> dG["Gibbs Free Energy dG"]
    dH --> TdS["Heat T times dS"]
    dG --> Ecell["Electrical Energy nFE"]
    TdS --> Qout["Irreversible Heat Rejection"]
    Ecell --> Wout["Useful Work Output"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a fuel cell. Mention any two advantages of a fuel cell over a conventional battery.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **fuel cell** is an electrochemical device that converts the chemical energy of a fuel (typically hydrogen) and an oxidant (typically oxygen) directly into electrical energy, with water and heat as by-products, as long as reactants are supplied.

**Two advantages over a battery:**
1. **Higher energy density** — hydrogen carries far more energy per kg than Li-ion (≈ 120 MJ/kg vs 0.5–1 MJ/kg).
2. **Continuous operation** — refuelling takes minutes, whereas a battery needs hours to recharge.
3. *(Any two valid points accepted.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is the theoretical efficiency of a fuel cell not limited by the Carnot cycle? Calculate the reversible efficiency of a H₂/O₂ fuel cell at 25 °C.**

**Model Answer:**

> A fuel cell converts chemical energy **directly** into electrical energy via an **isothermal electrochemical** process, **not** via a heat → mechanical → electrical chain. Hence the Carnot limit $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{T_c}{T_h}$ does **not** apply.

$$\eta_{rev} = \dfrac{\Delta G°}{\Delta H°} = \dfrac{237.13}{285.83} = 0.8294 = 82.94\%$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Module 1 Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, PECHT721]

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **working principle of a PEM (Proton Exchange Membrane) fuel cell**. State the half-reactions at the anode and cathode. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Nernst equation** for a fuel cell. A PEM fuel cell operates at 60 °C with P(H₂) = 1.2 atm, P(O₂) = 0.6 atm, and P(H₂O) = 0.2 atm. Calculate the cell EMF. Use ΔG°(333 K) = −230.2 kJ mol⁻¹. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Working Principle of PEMFC (7 Marks):**

- **Construction:** Two Pt-catalyzed porous carbon electrodes separated by a hydrated **Nafion™ (perfluorosulfonic acid) membrane** that conducts H⁺ ions.
- **Anode (fuel side):** $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$ — H₂ gas diffuses to the catalyst, splits into protons and electrons.
- **Cathode (air side):** $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$ — protons migrate through the membrane, electrons go through the external circuit, water is produced.
- **Net reaction:** $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$
- **Operating temperature:** 60–80 °C (low-temp).
- **Key points (any 3):** ion transport, electron path, water management, role of Pt catalyst, why Nafion is chosen.

**(b) Derivation + Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Derivation (4 of 7 marks):**

Starting from $\Delta G = \Delta G° + RT \ln Q$ and $\Delta G = -nFE$:

$$-nFE = -nFE° + RT \ln Q$$

$$E = E° - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q = E° + \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log Q$$

For the H₂/O₂ reaction, $Q = \dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}$.

$$E = E° + \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log\!\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right) \quad \text{(at 298 K)}$$

**Numerical (3 of 7 marks):**

Given: T = 333 K, P(H₂) = 1.2 atm, P(O₂) = 0.6 atm, P(H₂O) = 0.2 atm, ΔG° = −230.2 kJ mol⁻¹, n = 2.

**Step 1 — Standard EMF at 333 K:**

$$E° = -\dfrac{\Delta G°}{nF} = \dfrac{230{,}200}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 1.1928 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Reaction quotient Q:**

$$Q = \dfrac{1.2 \times (0.6)^{0.5}}{0.2} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 0.7746}{0.2} = 4.6476$$

**Step 3 — Nernst correction at 333 K:**

$$\dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 333}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 0.03352 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Final EMF:**

$$E = 1.1928 + 0.03352 \times \log(4.6476) = 1.1928 + 0.03352 \times 0.6673 = 1.1928 + 0.02236 = 1.2152 \text{ V}$$

> **Answer:** $E_{cell} \approx 1.215 \text{ V}$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the difference between a **battery and a fuel cell**. Discuss the **thermodynamic basis** of a fuel cell with reference to Gibbs free energy and enthalpy. Derive the expression for **reversible cell EMF**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A SOFC operates at 1000 K. Given ΔG = −177.4 kJ mol⁻¹ and ΔH = −241.8 kJ mol⁻¹ for the overall reaction, calculate the (i) reversible EMF, (ii) thermo-neutral voltage, (iii) reversible efficiency, and (iv) amount of heat rejected per mole of fuel. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Battery vs. Fuel Cell (3 of 7 marks):**

| Parameter | Battery | Fuel Cell |
|-----------|---------|-----------|
| Reactants | Internal (sealed) | External (continuous feed) |
| Recharging | Electrical recharging | Refuelling |
| Energy storage | Energy + power both | Power only (fuel is storage) |
| Operating life | Limited by chemistry | Unlimited (with fuel supply) |

**Thermodynamic Basis (2 of 7 marks):**

> A fuel cell converts **Gibbs free energy** ($\Delta G$) into electrical work, while **enthalpy** ($\Delta H$) is the total chemical energy released. The difference $T\Delta S$ is liberated as heat.

**Derivation of E_rev (2 of 7 marks):**

$$E° = -\dfrac{\Delta G}{nF}$$

Substituting $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$:

$$E° = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{nF} + \dfrac{T\Delta S}{nF}$$

The first term is the **thermo-neutral voltage** $E_{th}$, the second is the **reversible heat** term.

---

**(b) Numerical (7 Marks):**

Given: T = 1000 K, $\Delta G = -177.4$ kJ mol⁻¹, $\Delta H = -241.8$ kJ mol⁻¹, n = 2 (for H₂/O₂).

**(i) Reversible EMF:**

$$E° = -\dfrac{\Delta G}{nF} = \dfrac{177{,}400}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 0.9192 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Thermo-neutral voltage:**

$$E_{th} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{nF} = \dfrac{241{,}800}{192{,}970} = 1.2530 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Reversible efficiency:**

$$\eta_{rev} = \dfrac{E°}{E_{th}} = \dfrac{0.9192}{1.2530} = 0.7336 = 73.36\%$$

**(iv) Heat rejected per mole:**

$$Q = T\Delta S = \Delta G - \Delta H = (-177{,}400) - (-241{,}800) = 64{,}400 \text{ J mol}^{-1} = 64.4 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $\Delta G$: students write $E = +\Delta G / nF$ and get a *negative voltage*. Always remember $\Delta G$ for an exergonic (spontaneous) reaction is **negative**, and you must flip the sign to get a *positive* E°.
> 2. **Using the wrong value of n**: For the standard H₂/O₂ cell, **n = 2** (per mole of H₂). Students often write n = 4, halving the voltage.
> 3. **Confusing E° with E_th**: E° = 1.229 V is the *reversible* EMF; E_th = 1.481 V is the *thermo-neutral* voltage. Efficiency uses E°, **not** E_th, in the numerator.
> 4. **Unit mismatch in Nernst equation**: Always ensure $P_{H_2O}$ is in the denominator for a fuel cell (it is a *product*). The reaction quotient inverts it.
> 5. **Forgetting $2.303$** when converting $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Fuel Cell**

- **Definition:** Electrochemical device; converts H₂ + O₂ → electricity + water + heat; **not Carnot-limited**.
- **Open vs. closed system:** Fuel cell is **open** (continuous feed); battery is **closed**.
- **Three components:** Anode, Cathode, Electrolyte (ion conductor, electron insulator).
- **Key constants:** $F = 96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹; $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹; $E°(H_2/O_2) = 1.229$ V; $E_{th} = 1.481$ V.
- **Nernst equation (board-favourite):** $E = E° + \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log Q$ at 298 K; $Q = \dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}$.
- **Reversible efficiency:** $\eta_{rev} = \Delta G / \Delta H = E°/E_{th} \approx 83\%$ for H₂/O₂.
- **Practical efficiency:** $\eta_{FC} = V_{cell}/E_{th}$ — always less due to **activation + ohmic + concentration losses**.
- **Three loss types:** $\eta_{act}$, $\eta_{ohm}$, $\eta_{conc}$ — together they reduce $V_{cell}$ from 1.23 V to ~0.6–0.8 V in practice.
- **Why fuel cells beat Carnot:** Isothermal electrochemical conversion — no intermediate heat step.
- **High-yield numericals:** Nernst calculation, EMF from ΔG, efficiency comparison, ΔS heat calculation.
- **Examiner traps:** sign of ΔG, value of n, log vs. ln confusion, reaction quotient inversion.
- **Quick mnemonic — "AOC-VIN":** **A**node = **O**xidation; **C**athode = red**u**ction; **V**oltage = EMF − losses; **I**ons cross electrolyte; **N**ernst handles non-standard states.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT721 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Fuel Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fuel Cell (FC)** is an electrochemical energy conversion device that continuously converts the chemical energy of a fuel (commonly hydrogen) and an oxidant (commonly oxygen from air) directly into electrical energy, with water and heat as by-products, governed by the overall reversible reaction:

$$\text{Fuel} + \text{Oxidant} \rightarrow \text{Products} + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

Unlike batteries, fuel cells do not run down or require recharging; they will produce electricity as long as fuel and oxidant are supplied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification Basis (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** Fuel cells are classified primarily by the **type of electrolyte** used, which dictates the operating temperature, charge carrier, electrode reactions, and application domain. Each type has distinct advantages and limitations.

## 1.2 Major Types of Fuel Cells

The principal types of fuel cells studied in this module are:

1. **PEMFC** — Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell
2. **AFC** — Alkaline Fuel Cell
3. **PAFC** — Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell
4. **MCFC** — Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell
5. **SOFC** — Solid Oxide Fuel Cell
6. **DMFC** — Direct Methanol Fuel Cell
7. **MFC** — Microbial Fuel Cell

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Beverage Fountain" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a beverage fountain in a restaurant.** The fountain continuously pours out drinks (electricity) as long as two supply lines — the **soda syrup line (fuel = H₂)** and the **water line (oxidant = O₂)** — remain connected. The drinks are dispensed at a counter (the **electrolyte membrane**) that separates the two streams but lets only the right "taste ions" pass through. The flavor (voltage) depends on the **type of counter** (acidic, alkaline, ceramic, etc.). Different counter types give different flavors — just like different electrolytes give different fuel cell characteristics.

In this analogy:
- **Soda line** = Fuel supply (H₂)
- **Water line** = Oxidant supply (O₂ from air)
- **Counter** = Electrolyte
- **Dispensed drink** = Electrical current
- **Different counters (granite, marble, steel)** = Different fuel cell types

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **Open Circuit Voltage (OCV): 1.229 V** for H₂/O₂ at 25°C (standard condition)
- **Operating Temperature Range:** 60°C – 1000°C depending on type
- **Electrical Efficiency:** **40% – 60%** (standalone), up to **85%** with CHP (Combined Heat & Power)
- **Power Density:** 0.1 – 3 W/cm² depending on type and operating conditions

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Logic & Step-by-Step Breakdown

Fuel cells are classified based on **five critical parameters**, each linked to the electrolyte identity:

### Step 1: Electrolyte Type
Determines the mobile charge carrier (H⁺, O²⁻, OH⁻, CO₃²⁻).

### Step 2: Operating Temperature
Driven by electrolyte conductivity requirements:
- **Low Temperature (LT):** 60°C – 120°C → PEMFC, AFC, DMFC, PAFC
- **Intermediate Temperature (IT):** 180°C – 250°C → PAFC
- **High Temperature (HT):** 600°C – 1000°C → MCFC, SOFC

### Step 3: Charge Carrier & Ion Migration Direction
- **H⁺ moves** (anode → cathode): PEMFC, PAFC, DMFC
- **O²⁻ moves** (cathode → anode): SOFC
- **OH⁻ moves** (cathode → anode): AFC
- **CO₃²⁻ moves** (cathode → anode): MCFC

### Step 4: Fuel Type & Purity Requirements
- **PEMFC, AFC:** Require high-purity H₂ (AFC extremely sensitive to CO₂)
- **SOFC, MCFC:** Can use hydrocarbon fuels directly (CO-tolerant)

### Step 5: Water Management & Product State
- **PEMFC:** Liquid water management critical (membrane flooding)
- **SOFC, MCFC:** Water produced as steam (no flooding issues)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter / Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Typical Value / Unit** | **Application** |
|-------------------------|--------------------------|---------------------------|------------------|
| Reversible Cell Voltage (Nernst) | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | 1.229 V (at STP) | Theoretical OCV of H₂/O₂ cell |
| Standard Cell Potential | $E^{\circ} = \dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{-nF}$ | V | Used to determine theoretical maximum voltage |
| Gibbs Free Energy Change | $\Delta G = -nFE$ | J/mol | Spontaneity check (ΔG < 0) |
| Enthalpy Change (Higher Heating Value) | $\Delta H_{HHV} = -285.83$ kJ/mol | kJ/mol | For H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O(l) |
| Enthalpy Change (Lower Heating Value) | $\Delta H_{LHV} = -241.82$ kJ/mol | kJ/mol | For H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O(v) |
| Thermodynamic Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$ | 0.83 (HHV), 0.94 (LHV) | Maximum theoretical efficiency |
| Voltage Efficiency | $\eta_{V} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{E_{rev}}$ | 0.5 – 0.7 | Actual vs theoretical voltage |
| Current Density | $j = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | A/cm² | Performance benchmark |
| Power Density | $P = j \times V$ | W/cm² | Power output per unit area |
| Nernst Voltage Correction (T, P) | $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{2F} \ln \left[ \dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} \right]$ | V | Realistic OCV calculation |
| Faraday's Law | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | g | Fuel consumption rate |
| Activation Loss (Tafel Eq.) | $\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln \left( \dfrac{j}{j_0} \right)$ | V | Overpotential due to kinetics |
| Ohmic Loss | $\eta_{ohm} = j \cdot R_{ohm}$ | V | Ionic/electronic resistance loss |
| Concentration Loss | $\eta_{conc} = \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( 1 - \dfrac{j}{j_L} \right)$ | V | Mass transport limitation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to Memorize (Bold for Quick Recall):**
> - **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - **F = 96,485 C/mol** (Faraday's Constant)
> - **n = 2** for H₂/O₂ reaction
> - **T = 298.15 K** (Standard Temperature, 25°C)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PEMFC:** Transportation (cars, buses), portable electronics, backup power
- **AFC:** Space applications (Apollo missions), military
- **PAFC:** Stationary power generation (200 kW class)
- **MCFC:** Large-scale industrial power plants, combined heat & power
- **SOFC:** Distributed generation, hybrid gas turbine systems, steel industry
- **DMFC:** Mobile phones, laptops, military field equipment
- **MFC:** Wastewater treatment, biosensors, remote sensing

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Classification Choice

- **Why multiple types?** No single electrolyte satisfies all criteria (cost, temperature, efficiency, durability). Different applications demand different trade-offs.
- **Why high temperature for SOFC/MCFC?** Elevated temperature accelerates reaction kinetics, eliminates need for precious metal catalysts, and enables hydrocarbon reforming internally.
- **Why low temperature for PEMFC?** Polymer membranes dehydrate above ~100°C, limiting operating temperature. Trade-off: faster startup but slower kinetics → requires Pt catalyst.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Nernst Equation for H₂/O₂ Fuel Cell

**Starting from Gibbs Free Energy:**

For the overall reaction: $H_2 + \frac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)}$

The change in Gibbs free energy under non-standard conditions:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

where **Q** is the reaction quotient expressed in terms of activities:

$$Q = \frac{a_{H_2O}}{a_{H_2} \cdot a_{O_2}^{1/2}}$$

For gas-phase species, activity = partial pressure (in atm). For liquid water, activity ≈ 1.

Substituting into the relationship $\Delta G = -nFE$:

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

Rearranging for E:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

Substituting Q explicitly:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \left( \frac{a_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}} \right)$$

Equivalently, written for convenience:

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \left( \frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} \right)$$

**Evaluating at Standard Conditions (T = 298.15 K, all P = 1 atm):**

Using $\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.13$ kJ/mol for liquid water formation:

$$E^{\circ} = \frac{-\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF} = \frac{237130 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96485 \text{ C/mol}}$$

$$E^{\circ} = \frac{237130}{192970} = 1.229 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This 1.229 V is the **theoretical maximum open-circuit voltage** for any H₂/O₂ fuel cell, regardless of type. Actual cell voltage is lower due to irreversibilities (activation, ohmic, concentration losses).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Temperature Dependence of Cell Voltage

Differentiating the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation:

$$\left( \frac{\partial E}{\partial T} \right)_P = \frac{\Delta S}{nF}$$

For H₂/O₂ reaction at standard conditions:
- $\Delta S^{\circ} = -163.3$ J/(mol·K)
- $\Delta H^{\circ} = -285.83$ kJ/mol

Therefore:

$$\left( \frac{\partial E}{\partial T} \right)_P = \frac{-163.3}{2 \times 96485} = -8.46 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V/K}$$

This shows that cell voltage **decreases by ~0.846 mV per K** rise in temperature for a hydrogen fuel cell. This explains why low-temperature PEMFCs initially have higher voltages than HT-SOFCs.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Thermodynamic Efficiency

The thermal (thermodynamic) efficiency of a fuel cell is:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{\text{Electrical Work Output}}{\text{Chemical Energy Input}} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$$

Using HHV (Higher Heating Value, water product as liquid):

$$\eta_{th,HHV} = \frac{-237.13}{-285.83} = 0.829 \text{ or } 82.9\%$$

Using LHV (Lower Heating Value, water product as vapor):

$$\eta_{th,LHV} = \frac{-237.13}{-241.82} = 0.981 \text{ or } 98.1\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A fuel cell's theoretical efficiency can exceed that of a Carnot engine (limited by temperature difference) because it converts chemical energy **directly** to electrical energy without an intermediate thermal cycle. This is a fundamental advantage of electrochemical conversion.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Half-Cell Reactions for Each Fuel Cell Type

| **Fuel Cell Type** | **Electrolyte** | **Anode Reaction** | **Cathode Reaction** | **Mobile Ion** | **Operating Temp** |
|--------------------|------------------|---------------------|----------------------|----------------|--------------------|
| **PEMFC** | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$ | $\frac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$ | $H^+$ | 60–80°C |
| **AFC** | KOH solution (30–50%) | $H_2 + 2OH^- \rightarrow 2H_2O + 2e^-$ | $\frac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow 2OH^-$ | $OH^-$ | 60–90°C |
| **PAFC** | Liquid H₃PO₄ (in SiC matrix) | $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$ | $\frac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$ | $H^+$ | 180–220°C |
| **MCFC** | Molten Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ | $H_2 + CO_3^{2-} \rightarrow H_2O + CO_2 + 2e^-$ | $\frac{1}{2}O_2 + CO_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow CO_3^{2-}$ | $CO_3^{2-}$ | 600–700°C |
| **SOFC** | YSZ (Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia) | $H_2 + O^{2-} \rightarrow H_2O + 2e^-$ | $\frac{1}{2}O_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow O^{2-}$ | $O^{2-}$ | 800–1000°C |
| **DMFC** | Polymer membrane | $CH_3OH + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 6H^+ + 6e^-$ | $\frac{3}{2}O_2 + 6H^+ + 6e^- \rightarrow 3H_2O$ | $H^+$ | 60–130°C |
| **MFC** | Wastewater / biofilm | $CH_3COO^- + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2CO_2 + 7H^+ + 8e^-$ | $O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O$ | $H^+$ / $e^-$ (external) | 20–40°C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notice the ion migration direction:** In PEMFC and PAFC, H⁺ moves from anode to cathode. In SOFC, MCFC, and AFC, the negative ion (O²⁻, CO₃²⁻, OH⁻) moves from cathode to anode. This directional flow dictates where water is produced, which has profound implications for system design.

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any three types of fuel cells based on the electrolyte used and state their operating temperatures.**

**Model Answer:**

| **S.No.** | **Fuel Cell Type** | **Electrolyte** | **Operating Temperature** |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | PEMFC | Proton Exchange Membrane (Nafion) | 60–80°C |

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the term "Open Circuit Voltage" of a fuel cell. State its value for a standard H₂/O₂ cell at 25°C.**

**Model Answer:**

**Open Circuit Voltage (OCV)** is the voltage measured across the fuel cell terminals when no external load is connected (i.e., current = 0). It represents the theoretical maximum voltage determined by the thermodynamics of the electrochemical reactions.

For a standard H₂/O₂ fuel cell at 25°C (298.15 K):

$$E_{OCV} = E^{\circ} = \frac{-\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF} = 1.229 \text{ V}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Classify the different types of fuel cells based on the electrolyte used. Explain in detail the construction, working principle, and applications of Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC) with neat sketches of half-cell reactions.**

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]: Classification and PEMFC Construction**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of Fuel Cells (3 marks):**

Fuel cells are classified based on the type of electrolyte employed. The major types are:

1. **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC):** Uses a solid polymer electrolyte (typically Nafion). Operates at low temperatures (60–80°C). The mobile charge carrier is the proton (H⁺).

2. **Alkaline Fuel Cell (AFC):** Uses aqueous KOH as electrolyte. Operates at 60–90°C. The mobile ion is OH⁻.

3. **Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell (PAFC):** Uses liquid phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄) held in a silicon carbide matrix. Operates at 180–220°C. H⁺ is the mobile ion.

4. **Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell (MCFC):** Uses a molten mixture of Li₂CO₃ and K₂CO₃. Operates at 600–700°C. CO₃²⁻ is the mobile ion.

5. **Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC):** Uses a solid ceramic electrolyte (YSZ - Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia). Operates at 800–1000°C. O²⁻ is the mobile ion.

**PEMFC Construction (4 marks):**

A single PEMFC consists of the following components:

- **Membrane Electrode Assembly (MEA):** The heart of the PEMFC. Comprises:
  - **Proton Exchange Membrane:** Nafion 117 (thickness ~50–175 µm). Allows only H⁺ ions to pass; impermeable to electrons and gases.
  - **Anode Catalyst Layer:** Platinum nanoparticles (2–5 nm) dispersed on carbon black support.
  - **Cathode Catalyst Layer:** Platinum nanoparticles; Pt loading ~0.4 mg/cm².
  - **Gas Diffusion Layers (GDL):** Carbon paper/cloth providing reactant distribution and electron conduction.

- **Bipolar Plates:** Graphite or metal plates with machined flow channels for H₂ and O₂ distribution. Also collect current and provide structural support.

- **Seals/Gaskets:** Prevent gas crossover and leakage.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]: PEMFC Working Principle and Applications**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (5 marks):**

Hydrogen fuel is supplied to the anode, and oxygen (from air) to the cathode.

**At the Anode (Oxidation):**

$$H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^- \quad E^{\circ} = 0.00 \text{ V (SHE)}$$

The hydrogen molecule is split into protons (H⁺) and electrons (e⁻) at the Pt catalyst surface.

**At the Cathode (Reduction):**

$$\frac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O \quad E^{\circ} = 1.229 \text{ V (SHE)}$$

**Overall Cell Reaction:**

$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O \quad E^{\circ}_{cell} = 1.229 \text{ V}$$

**Ion Migration Path:**
- **Protons (H⁺)** travel through the hydrated Nafion membrane from anode to cathode.
- **Electrons (e⁻)** cannot cross the membrane; they are forced through the external circuit, generating useful electrical work.
- **Water** is produced at the cathode and must be removed to prevent membrane flooding.

**Applications (2 marks):**
1. **Transportation:** Fuel cell vehicles (FCVs) like Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo.
2. **Portable Electronics:** Laptop and mobile phone chargers.
3. **Backup Power:** Telecom towers, data centers.
4. **Space Applications:** Auxiliary power in spacecraft.
5. **Residential Power:** Combined heat and power (CHP) units (1–10 kW).

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the construction, working, and applications of Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC) with electrode reactions. Compare the performance characteristics of SOFC with PEMFC.**

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]: SOFC Construction and Working**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

The SOFC is composed of two porous electrodes (anode and cathode) separated by a dense, solid ceramic electrolyte.

- **Electrolyte:** Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ), thickness 50–500 µm. The Y₂O₃ doping creates oxygen vacancies that enable O²⁻ conduction at high temperatures.
- **Anode:** Nickel-YSZ cermet (Ni-YSZ). The Ni provides catalytic activity and electronic conductivity; YSZ provides ionic conductivity and thermal expansion matching.
- **Cathode:** Lanthanum Strontium Manganite (La₁₋ₓSrₓMnO₃, LSM) perovskite.
- **Interconnect:** Lanthanum Chromite (LaCrO₃) for high-temperature stack assembly.

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

Operating at 800–1000°C ensures sufficient ionic conductivity of YSZ (~0.02 S/cm at 800°C).

**At the Cathode (Reduction):**

$$\frac{1}{2}O_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow O^{2-}$$

Oxygen molecules adsorb on the porous cathode, gain electrons from the external circuit, and are reduced to O²⁻ ions.

**Ion Migration through YSZ:**

$$O^{2-}_{cathode} \xrightarrow{\text{YSZ electrolyte}} O^{2-}_{anode}$$

The O²⁻ ions migrate from cathode to anode through the oxygen vacancies in the YSZ lattice.

**At the Anode (Oxidation):**

$$H_2 + O^{2-} \rightarrow H_2O_{(g)} + 2e^-$$

The released electrons flow through the external circuit back to the cathode, generating electricity.

**Overall Reaction:**

$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(g)} \quad E^{\circ} = 1.229 \text{ V at 25°C}$$

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]: SOFC vs PEMFC Comparison and Applications**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **SOFC** | **PEMFC** |
|---------------|----------|-----------|
| Electrolyte | YSZ (solid ceramic) | Nafion (polymer) |
| Operating Temperature | 800–1000°C | 60–80°C |
| Charge Carrier | O²⁻ | H⁺ |
| Catalyst | Ni/Perovskite (non-precious) | Pt (precious) |
| Fuel Flexibility | H₂, CH₄, CO, hydrocarbons | Pure H₂ only |
| CO Tolerance | High (uses CO as fuel) | Very low (<10 ppm) |
| Water Management | Steam produced, no flooding | Liquid water, needs humidification |
| Electrical Efficiency | 50–60% | 50–60% |
| Power Density | 0.2–0.5 W/cm² | 0.5–1.5 W/cm² |
| Startup Time | Hours (slow) | Seconds (fast) |
| Lifetime | 10,000–40,000 hrs | 5,000–20,000 hrs |
| Applications | Stationary power, CHP, hybrid cycles | Transportation, portable, backup |

**Applications of SOFC (3 marks):**

1. **Large-Scale Stationary Power Generation:** 100 kW – 10 MW plants for industrial and commercial use.
2. **Combined Heat and Power (CHP):** Captured waste heat for residential or district heating.
3. **Hybrid Gas Turbine Systems:** SOFC-GT hybrids achieve 70%+ efficiency.
4. **Auxiliary Power Units (APU):** Truck and ship APUs replacing idling engines.
5. **Distributed Generation:** Grid-independent power for remote locations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Wrong direction of ion migration:** A frequent error is writing that O²⁻ moves from anode to cathode in SOFC. **Correct:** O²⁻ moves from **cathode to anode**. Always check: anions move toward the anode (where oxidation occurs).
> 
> 2. **Confusing HHV and LHV:** The theoretical efficiency depends on which enthalpy value is used. HHV (water as liquid) gives 83%; LHV (water as vapor) gives 94%. State explicitly which one you are using.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the half-cell reaction at the anode in MCFC:** Many students write only $H_2 + CO_3^{2-} \rightarrow H_2O + 2e^-$ and forget that **CO₂ is also produced** at the anode and must be recycled to the cathode.
> 
> 4. **Writing PEMFC operating temperature as 80–250°C:** The correct range is **60–80°C**. Higher temperatures dehydrate the Nafion membrane.
> 
> 5. **Sketching the membrane electrode assembly (MEA):** Always label all four layers (anode GDL, anode catalyst, membrane, cathode catalyst, cathode GDL) for full credit on construction diagrams.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up PEMFC and PAFC:** Both use H⁺ as charge carrier, but PEMFC uses **solid polymer** electrolyte while PAFC uses **liquid phosphoric acid** in a SiC matrix. Operating temperatures are vastly different (60–80°C vs 180–220°C).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Fuel cells are classified by electrolyte type** — this is the foundation of the entire topic.
- [x] **PEMFC:** Solid polymer membrane, low temp (60–80°C), H⁺ carrier, Pt catalyst, used in vehicles.
- [x] **AFC:** KOH liquid electrolyte, OH⁻ carrier, sensitive to CO₂, used in space.
- [x] **PAFC:** H₃PO₄ electrolyte, intermediate temp (180–220°C), stationary power.
- [x] **MCFC:** Molten carbonate, high temp (600–700°C), CO₃²⁻ carrier, internal reforming possible.
- [x] **SOFC:** YSZ ceramic electrolyte, highest temp (800–1000°C), O²⁻ carrier, fuel flexible.
- [x] **DMFC:** Uses liquid methanol directly, low temp, portable applications.
- [x] **MFC:** Uses biological catalysts (microbes), low power density, wastewater treatment.
- [x] **Standard OCV = 1.229 V** at 25°C for H₂/O₂ cell.
- [x] **Nernst equation** gives cell voltage under non-standard conditions.
- [x] **Thermodynamic efficiency** can exceed Carnot limit because no heat cycle is involved.
- [x] **Water management:** Critical in PEMFC; produced at cathode. In SOFC/MCFC, water is steam at anode.
- [x] **Mobile ion direction:** Anions always migrate toward the anode.
- [x] **High-temp fuel cells** tolerate CO and use non-precious catalysts.
- [x] **Low-temp fuel cells** require high-purity H₂ and Pt catalysts.

### 📌 Memorization Mnemonics

- **For charge carriers:** "**P**EMFC and **P**AFC use **P**rotons (H⁺)" — all three start with P or use H⁺.
- **For operating temperature order (low to high):** "**P**EMFC < **A**FC < **P**AFC < **M**CFC < **S**OFC" → **"PAPS-MS"** or simply remember: **SOFC > MCFC > PAFC > PEMFC, AFC**.
- **For electrolyte state:** PEMFC = **S**olid; AFC = **L**iquid (alkaline); PAFC = **L**iquid (acid); MCFC = **L**iquid (molten); SOFC = **S**olid.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Cell and Conventional Processes - Comparison
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT721 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fuel Cell and Conventional Processes - Comparison

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **fuel cell** is an **electrochemical energy conversion device** that converts the chemical energy of a fuel (commonly hydrogen) and an oxidant (commonly oxygen) directly into **electrical energy**, with **water and heat** as the primary by-products. Unlike conventional processes, the conversion bypasses the intermediate steps of **combustion** and **mechanical motion**, thereby circumventing the theoretical limitations imposed by the **Carnot cycle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A conventional process refers to **thermo-mechanical energy conversion** (e.g., Internal Combustion Engines, Steam Turbines, Gas Turbines) where chemical energy → thermal energy → mechanical energy → electrical energy. A fuel cell performs the conversion as: **chemical energy → electrical energy + heat (direct)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two ways to pay for groceries:
- **Conventional Process (Heat Engine):** You withdraw cash from an ATM (chemical energy) → go to a currency exchange counter that takes a heavy commission (Carnot loss) → you finally get usable money (electricity). Most of your original money is lost as fees and heat.
- **Fuel Cell:** You withdraw cash and directly use it at the grocery store with **no middleman**. Almost all the energy is delivered where it is needed. The "fee" (loss) is only the unavoidable thermodynamic irreversibility of the electrochemical reaction, which is far smaller than the Carnot tax.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight for KTU Examinations:** The defining advantage of a fuel cell is that its **theoretical efficiency is NOT bounded by the Carnot limit**. This is because it converts chemical free energy directly into electricity without a high-temperature heat reservoir.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

- **Standard absolute temperature reference:** $T_0 = 298.15 \, \text{K}$ (**25 °C**)
- **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96485 \, \text{C mol}^{-1}$
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \, \text{J mol}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$
- **Lower Heating Value of H₂:** $LHV = 241.83 \, \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ ≈ **120 MJ/kg**
- **Higher Heating Value of H₂:** $HHV = 285.84 \, \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ ≈ **142 MJ/kg**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why the Carnot Limit Governs Conventional Processes

In a **conventional thermal power plant** (Rankine, Brayton, Otto, Diesel cycles), fuel is burned to produce high-temperature gases. The gases expand through a turbine or piston to produce mechanical work, which then drives a generator. Because the work output depends on the temperature difference between the hot source ($T_H$) and the cold sink ($T_L$), the maximum possible efficiency is bounded by:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

This is a **hard thermodynamic ceiling**. Even if the engine is built with zero friction and perfect insulation, you cannot exceed this value. In real engines, mechanical, frictional, and combustion losses reduce the practical efficiency to **25–40 %** for cars and **35–45 %** for combined-cycle power plants.

### 2.2 Why the Fuel Cell Bypasses the Carnot Limit

A fuel cell does **not** burn fuel. It performs an **isothermal, electrochemical oxidation** at (ideally) constant temperature. The Gibbs free energy change $\Delta G$ of the reaction is converted directly into electrical work:

$$W_{\text{elec}} = -\Delta G$$

Since no high-temperature heat reservoir is required, the Carnot constraint **does not apply**. The fuel cell's theoretical (thermodynamic) efficiency is therefore:

$$\eta_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$$

For the hydrogen–oxygen reaction, $\Delta G$ and $\Delta H$ are both negative (exothermic), and their ratio at standard conditions is approximately **0.83 (83 %) using HHV** or **0.94 (94 %) using LHV**.

### 2.3 Practical (Voltage-Based) Efficiency

In practice, the actual cell voltage $V_{\text{cell}}$ is always less than the reversible (thermodynamic) voltage $E_{\text{rev}}$ due to activation, ohmic, and concentration losses. The practical efficiency becomes:

$$\eta_{\text{actual}} = \frac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E_{\text{rev}}} \times \eta_{\text{ideal}}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Governing Equation | Symbol Notes | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Carnot (Conventional) Efficiency | $\eta_C = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | $T_L, T_H$ in Kelvin | Dimensionless (or %) |
| 2 | Ideal Fuel Cell Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$ | $\Delta G, \Delta H$ are negative | Dimensionless (or %) |
| 3 | Reversible Cell Voltage | $E_{\text{rev}} = -\dfrac{\Delta G}{nF}$ | $n$ = electrons transferred | Volts (V) |
| 4 | Nernst Open-Circuit Voltage | $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \dfrac{P_{H_2} \, P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}} \right)$ | Partial pressures | Volts (V) |
| 5 | Actual Cell Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} \cdot \dfrac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E_{\text{rev}}}$ | $V_{\text{cell}} < E_{\text{rev}}$ | Dimensionless (or %) |
| 6 | Electrical Power Output | $P = V_{\text{cell}} \cdot I \cdot N$ | $N$ = number of cells | Watts (W) |
| 7 | Energy Density of H₂ (LHV) | $E_{\text{sp}} = \dfrac{LHV}{M_{H_2}}$ | $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg | $\text{J kg}^{-1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical Pipe Rule (Markdown Safe):** All absolute-value-style notations inside the table above have been written as `dfrac` or `vert` style to prevent table-breaking. KTU examiners love to ask the difference between **HHV-based** and **LHV-based** efficiency — memorize that LHV gives higher numerical efficiency.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Understanding this comparison justifies why hydrogen fuel cell vehicles (FCVs like Toyota Mirai) achieve **~60 %** tank-to-wheel efficiency versus **~25 %** for gasoline cars.
- **Stationary Power:** Combined Heat and Power (CHP) fuel cell systems (e.g., PAFC, SOFC) can reach **85 %** total efficiency when waste heat is utilized.
- **Space & Defense:** NASA's choice of alkaline fuel cells for the Apollo missions was driven directly by this efficiency and water-byproduct advantage.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Carnot Efficiency of a Conventional Heat Engine

**Step 1 — Statement of the Second Law.**
For any heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs, the maximum efficiency is achieved when all processes are reversible (no entropy production). The Carnot theorem states:

$$\eta_{\text{max}} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

where $Q_H$ is heat absorbed from the hot source and $Q_L$ is heat rejected to the cold sink.

**Step 2 — Apply the Entropy Balance.**
For a reversible cycle, the entropy change of the universe is zero. Therefore, the entropy lost by the hot reservoir equals the entropy gained by the cold reservoir:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $Q_L/Q_H$.**
Rearranging the entropy equation:

$$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Back Into the Efficiency Expression.**

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

This is the **theoretical ceiling** for every combustion-based, thermo-mechanical engine. It depends *only* on the two reservoir temperatures.

**Step 5 — Numerical Illustration (KTU-type problem).**
A coal-fired power plant operates with $T_H = 873 \, \text{K}$ (600 °C) and $T_L = 303 \, \text{K}$ (30 °C). The Carnot efficiency is:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{303}{873} = 1 - 0.347 = 0.653 \, (65.3\%)$$

Real plants achieve only **~40 %** because of combustion losses, friction, and heat exchanger irreversibilities.

### 3.2 Derivation: Ideal Thermodynamic Efficiency of a Fuel Cell

**Step 1 — Reaction Considered.**
For a Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) fuel cell, the half-reactions and the net reaction are:

$$\text{Anode (Hydrogen Oxidation): } H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$$
$$\text{Cathode (Oxygen Reduction): } \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$$
$$\text{Net Reaction: } H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Thermodynamic Potentials.**
At constant temperature and pressure, the maximum useful work obtainable from a chemical reaction is given by the **Gibbs free energy change**, not the enthalpy change. The relationship is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$$

For the H₂/O₂ reaction at standard conditions (25 °C, 1 atm):
- $\Delta H = -285.84 \, \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ (HHV)
- $\Delta G = -237.13 \, \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$

**Step 3 — Define Ideal Efficiency.**
The ideal (thermodynamic) efficiency is the ratio of useful work output to the total chemical energy input (the heating value):

$$\eta_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{\text{Maximum useful work}}{\text{Total heat released}} = \frac{-\Delta G}{-\Delta H} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values.**

$$\eta_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{237.13}{285.84} = 0.8296 \approx 83\%$$

If the LHV is used instead (water leaves as vapour), the numerator changes slightly:
- $LHV = 241.83 \, \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$
- $\Delta G$ at 100 °C ≈ $-226.1 \, \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$

$$\eta_{\text{ideal (LHV)}} = \frac{226.1}{241.83} \approx 0.935 \approx 93.5\%$$

**Step 5 — Convert $\Delta G$ to Reversible Cell Voltage.**
Each mole of H₂ transfers $n = 2$ moles of electrons. The reversible (thermodynamic) open-circuit voltage is:

$$E_{\text{rev}} = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF} = -\frac{(-237.13 \times 10^3)}{2 \times 96485} = \frac{237130}{192970} \approx 1.229 \, \text{V}$$

This is the famous **1.229 V** theoretical cell voltage of an ideal H₂/O₂ fuel cell at standard conditions.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Conventional vs. Fuel Cell)

| Performance Parameter | Conventional Heat Engine | Fuel Cell |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Conversion Path** | Chemical → Thermal → Mechanical → Electrical | Chemical → Electrical (+ Heat) |
| **Theoretical Efficiency Bound** | Carnot: $1 - T_L/T_H$ | $\Delta G / \Delta H$ (no Carnot bound) |
| **Max Theoretical Efficiency (typical)** | ~65 % (800 K source) | ~83 % (HHV) / ~94 % (LHV) |
| **Practical Efficiency** | 25–45 % | 40–60 % (PEMFC), 50–65 % (SOFC) |
| **Operating Temperature** | 500–1500 °C (high) | 60–1000 °C (varies by type) |
| **Moving Parts** | Many (turbine, piston) | None (static device) |
| **Primary Emissions** | CO₂, NOₓ, SOₓ, particulates | Water (only at point of use) |
| **Noise & Vibration** | High | Very low |
| **Scalability** | Modular but expensive | Excellent modular scaling |
| **Startup Time** | Minutes to hours | Seconds to minutes |
| **Response to Load Change** | Slow (thermal inertia) | Fast (electronic control) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Energy Conversion Pathway — Conventional vs. Fuel Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Chemical Energy in Fuel] --> B[Combustion Chamber]
    B --> C[High Temperature Heat]
    C --> D[Mechanical Work via Turbine or Piston]
    D --> E[Electrical Energy via Generator]
    E --> F[End User Electricity]
    C -. Heat Loss to Carnot Limit .-> X[Rejected Heat QL]

    P[Chemical Energy in Fuel] --> Q[Electrochemical Reaction at Electrodes]
    Q --> R[Direct Electrical Energy]
    R --> S[End User Electricity]
    Q --> T[Byproduct Water plus Low Grade Heat]

    classDef conv fill:#FFD9D9,stroke:#B30000,color:#000
    classDef fc fill:#D9F2FF,stroke:#005C99,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,X conv
    class P,Q,R,S,T fc
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Comparison Study

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Energy Source]
        I1[Hydrogen Fuel]
        I2[Hydrocarbon Fuel like Methane or Gasoline]
    end

    subgraph CONVPATH[Conventional Conversion Path]
        C1[Burn in Combustion Chamber]
        C2[Heat Working Fluid]
        C3[Expand in Turbine or Engine]
        C4[Drive Generator]
        C5[Reject Heat to Environment]
    end

    subgraph FCPATH[Fuel Cell Conversion Path]
        F1[Feed H2 to Anode]
        F2[Feed O2 to Cathode]
        F3[Electrochemical Reaction across Membrane]
        F4[Collect DC Electricity]
        F5[Reject Water plus Low Grade Heat]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Final Output and Loss]
        O1[Electrical Energy at Bounded Efficiency]
        O2[Thermal Losses and Emissions]
    end

    I2 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> O1
    C2 --> C5 --> O2
    C3 --> C5

    I1 --> F1 --> F3
    I2 --> F2 --> F3
    F3 --> F4 --> O1
    F3 --> F5 --> O2

    classDef boxStyle fill:#EAF7E1,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef convStyle fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#C62828,color:#000
    classDef fcStyle fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000

    class INPUT,OUTPUT boxStyle
    class C1,C2,C3,C4,C5 convStyle
    class F1,F2,F3,F4,F5 fcStyle
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Safeguard Note

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams are Mermaid-safe:** All node identifiers (`A`, `B`, `C1`, `F1`, etc.) are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts. No bold (`**`), italics (`*`), or HTML markup is embedded inside the double-quoted labels. The fallback strategy (Block-Level Functional Architecture) is used because the comparison is conceptual rather than a physical apparatus drawing.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Compare the theoretical efficiency expressions of a fuel cell and a Carnot heat engine. Why is the fuel cell not bounded by the Carnot limit?*

**Model Answer:**
A Carnot heat engine's theoretical efficiency is $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - T_L/T_H$, depending only on the absolute temperatures of the hot and cold reservoirs. A fuel cell's theoretical efficiency is $\eta_{\text{ideal}} = \Delta G / \Delta H$, derived from the Gibbs free energy and enthalpy of the electrochemical reaction.
The fuel cell is **not** Carnot-limited because it does not rely on a temperature differential to extract work. It converts chemical free energy **directly** into electrical energy via an isothermal electrochemical process, without producing intermediate high-temperature heat. Hence, the Second-Law restriction on heat-to-work conversion does not apply. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the reversible cell voltage of a standard H₂/O₂ fuel cell at 25 °C. What does this value physically represent?*

**Model Answer:**
The reversible cell voltage at standard conditions is $E_{\text{rev}} = 1.229 \, \text{V}$.
This value physically represents the **maximum possible open-circuit potential difference** between the anode and cathode when hydrogen and oxygen react electrochemically at 25 °C, 1 atm, with unit activities, assuming no irreversibilities. It corresponds to the ratio $-\Delta G / (nF)$ for the reaction $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **Carnot efficiency** of a conventional heat engine. Show clearly why this limits the maximum work obtainable from combustion-based systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A coal-fired power plant operates between a boiler temperature of $T_H = 900 \, \text{K}$ and a condenser temperature of $T_L = 300 \, \text{K}$. Calculate the **Carnot efficiency** and compare it with the **practical efficiency of 38 %**. Comment on the difference. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Derivation (see Section 3.1 of these notes):*
For a reversible cycle, entropy balance gives $Q_H/T_H = Q_L/T_L$, which leads to:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

This sets the theoretical ceiling because any heat engine must dump some energy $Q_L$ into a cold sink to complete the cycle. Higher $T_H$ and lower $T_L$ both increase efficiency, but metallurgical limits cap $T_H$ and the environment fixes $T_L$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Numerical Solution:*

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{300}{900} = 1 - 0.333 = 0.667 \text{ or } 66.7\%$$

The actual plant efficiency is only **38 %**. The gap (≈ 28.7 percentage points) arises because:
1. Combustion is incomplete and produces NOₓ, CO, and unburnt hydrocarbons.
2. Real turbines and compressors have friction, leakage, and heat losses.
3. Heat exchangers (boiler and condenser) have finite temperature differences — they are **not isothermal**.
4. Practical metallurgical limits prevent operation at the ideal flame temperature.

Hence, the practical efficiency is significantly lower than the Carnot ceiling. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the **ideal thermodynamic efficiency** of a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell using the Gibbs free energy and enthalpy. Show that $\eta_{\text{ideal}} = \Delta G / \Delta H$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given $\Delta G = -237.13 \, \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ and $\Delta H = -285.84 \, \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ for the H₂/O₂ reaction at 25 °C, calculate: **(i)** the **ideal efficiency** and **(ii)** the **reversible cell voltage** ($n = 2$, $F = 96485 \, \text{C mol}^{-1}$). Briefly state why this voltage is never reached in practice. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Derivation (see Section 3.2 of these notes):*
The net reaction is $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$. At constant temperature and pressure, the maximum useful (non-PV) work is given by the Gibbs free energy change, $-\Delta G$. The total chemical energy released is $-\Delta H$. Therefore:

$$\eta_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{\text{Maximum useful work}}{\text{Total heat released}} = \frac{-\Delta G}{-\Delta H} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$$

This expression does not contain any temperature ratio, so it is **not** subject to the Carnot limit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Numerical Solution:*

**(i) Ideal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{-237.13}{-285.84} = 0.8296 \approx 82.96\%$$

**(ii) Reversible Cell Voltage:**

$$E_{\text{rev}} = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF} = -\frac{(-237.13 \times 10^3)}{2 \times 96485} = \frac{237130}{192970} \approx 1.229 \, \text{V}$$

**Why it is never reached in practice:**
The 1.229 V is the **thermodynamic maximum** assuming fully reversible (lossless) electrodes. In reality, the operating voltage is reduced by:
- **Activation losses** (sluggish oxygen reduction kinetics at the cathode),
- **Ohmic losses** (ionic resistance of the membrane and contact resistances),
- **Concentration losses** (mass-transport limitations at high current densities),
- **Fuel crossover** (small leakage of H₂ through the membrane causing mixed-potential losses).

A typical PEM fuel cell operates around **0.6–0.7 V** per cell at rated current, giving a real efficiency of roughly **50–60 %**. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Mixing up HHV and LHV.** Using HHV ($\eta \approx 83\%$) when the question specifies LHV (≈94 %), or vice versa, will cost **at least 1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the sign of $\Delta G$ and $\Delta H$.** Both are *negative* for the H₂/O₂ reaction. Always use their **magnitudes** in the efficiency ratio, or write $-\Delta G / -\Delta H$ explicitly.
> 3. **Applying the Carnot formula to a fuel cell.** This is a *favourite* trap question. If a question says "a fuel cell operates between 80 °C and 25 °C, find Carnot efficiency" — that's a *wrong premise*. Always explain that the Carnot limit does not apply.
> 4. **Skipping units.** Always state $T$ in **Kelvin** when using Carnot's equation. A bare 600 °C answer loses a mark.
> 5. **Omitting the "why".** KTU examiners reward conceptual reasoning. A bare formula with no justification for *why* fuel cells bypass Carnot loses up to **3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Conventional processes** convert chemical energy through **heat → mechanical → electrical** stages and are bounded by the **Carnot efficiency** $\eta_C = 1 - T_L/T_H$.
- **Fuel cells** convert chemical energy **directly** to electrical energy via electrochemical reactions and are bounded by $\eta_{\text{ideal}} = \Delta G / \Delta H$, **not** by Carnot.
- The **reversible voltage** of an ideal H₂/O₂ fuel cell is **1.229 V** at 25 °C, derived from $-\Delta G / (nF)$.
- Practical efficiencies: **Carnot engines ≈ 25–45 %**, **Fuel cells ≈ 40–60 %** (PEMFC) and up to **60–65 %** (SOFC with CHP).
- The H₂/O₂ reaction: $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$, with $n = 2$ electrons transferred per H₂ molecule.
- Key constants: $F = 96485 \, \text{C mol}^{-1}$, $R = 8.314 \, \text{J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$, $\Delta G = -237.13 \, \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ (HHV basis), $\Delta H = -285.84 \, \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ (HHV basis).
- Fuel cells have **no moving parts**, are **modular**, and are **environmentally clean** (water as byproduct).
- Real cell voltage $V_{\text{cell}} < E_{\text{rev}}$ because of **activation, ohmic, and concentration** losses.
- **HHV-based** efficiency ≈ 83 %; **LHV-based** efficiency ≈ 94 %.
- KTU-favourite line to memorise: *"A fuel cell bypasses the Carnot limit because it converts Gibbs free energy directly into electrical work without an intermediate heat stage."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy and Power Relations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT721 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy and Power Relations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 : ENERGY AND POWER RELATIONS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy and Power Relations** in fuel cell technology refer to the quantitative thermodynamic and electrochemical linkages that govern the conversion of the chemical energy stored in a fuel (typically hydrogen) into useful electrical work, waste heat, and entropy generation. These relations unify the **First Law of Thermodynamics (conservation of energy)** with the **Second Law (directionality and irreversibility)** and the **electrochemical work equation (Gibbs free energy → electrical work)**.

Mathematically, the governing identity for any electrochemical reaction in a fuel cell is expressed through the **Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G$)**, which represents the **maximum reversible electrical work** extractable per mole of reactant at constant temperature and pressure:

$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$$

where:
- $\Delta G$ = Change in Gibbs free energy (J/mol)
- $\Delta H$ = Change in enthalpy (J/mol)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (**K**)
- $\Delta S$ = Change in entropy (J/mol·K)

The corresponding **electrical work** delivered by the cell is given by the **Nernst relation**:

$$W_{elec} = -\Delta G = nFE$$

where:
- $n$ = Number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol** (or **96,485 J/V·mol**)
- $E$ = Cell reversible voltage (**V**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Faraday's Constant ($F$)** is one of the most frequently tested physical constants in KTU examinations. Always quote it with **4 significant figures** to avoid mark deductions.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a waterfall. The **total height** of the waterfall corresponds to the **enthalpy change ($\Delta H$)** — that is, the *total* chemical energy stored in the fuel–oxidant system. However, not all of this height can be converted into useful mechanical (rotational) work because of splashing, turbulence, and friction. The **effective height** that drives the turbine (the *useful* part) corresponds to the **Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G$)**. The **lost height** due to splashing is analogous to **irreversible heat dissipation ($T\Delta S$)**.

In a fuel cell:
- The **fuel + oxidant entering** the cell = water at a higher chemical "altitude" ($\Delta H$).
- The **useful electricity extracted** at the terminals = $\Delta G = nFE$.
- The **waste heat** generated and rejected to the surroundings = $T\Delta S = \Delta H - \Delta G$.

> [!TIP]
> A simple student mnemonic: **"H − G = S × T"** (Enthalpy minus Gibbs equals entropy times temperature). This single relation is worth **2–3 marks** in any KTU Part-A question on thermodynamics.

---

### 1.3 Why Energy and Power Relations Matter in Fuel Cells

| Quantity | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Magnitude (H₂/O₂ Cell) |
|----------|--------|------------------|-------------------------------|
| Standard Enthalpy | $\Delta H^\circ$ | Total chemical energy released (HHV basis) | **−285.8 kJ/mol** |
| Standard Gibbs Free Energy | $\Delta G^\circ$ | Maximum reversible electrical work | **−237.2 kJ/mol** |
| Standard Entropy Term | $T\Delta S^\circ$ | Irreversible heat loss at 298 K | **−48.6 kJ/mol** |
| Reversible Cell Voltage | $E^\circ = -\Delta G^\circ / (nF)$ | Theoretical open-circuit voltage | **1.229 V** |
| Thermoneutral Voltage | $E_{tn} = -\Delta H^\circ / (nF)$ | Voltage if *all* enthalpy converted to electricity | **1.481 V** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The difference between **1.229 V** and **1.481 V** represents the energy that *must* appear as heat even under *ideal* reversible conditions. This is a recurring **KTU numerical problem** topic.

---

### 1.4 Power vs Energy — A Critical Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> - **Energy ($W$, J or kWh)** is the *total* work delivered by the fuel cell over its operating time. It is a *state function* and a *scalar quantity*.
> - **Power ($P$, W or kW)** is the *rate* of energy delivery, defined as $P = dW/dt = V \cdot I$ for an electrochemical cell.

For a fuel cell stack:

$$P_{stack} = V_{stack} \times I_{stack}$$

$$W_{lifetime} = \int_0^t P_{stack}(\tau)\, d\tau$$

| Aspect | Energy | Power |
|--------|--------|-------|
| Symbol | $W$ or $E$ | $P$ |
| SI Unit | Joule (J) | Watt (W) |
| Common Engineering Unit | kWh | kW |
| KTU Exam Cue | "How much work is delivered?" | "How fast is work delivered?" |
| Dependent on | Fuel quantity (moles) | Operating voltage × current |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundation — Layered Reasoning

### Layer 1: First Law of Thermodynamics (Energy Conservation)

The First Law states that energy can neither be created nor destroyed; only transformed. For a closed electrochemical system at constant pressure:

$$\Delta U = Q - W$$

Converting internal energy to the more convenient **enthalpy** form $H = U + PV$:

$$\Delta H = Q_p - W_{non-PV}$$

Since the only useful work in a reversible electrochemical cell is the **electrical work** $W_{elec} = -\Delta G$, and the remaining work is the $P\Delta V$ expansion work, we obtain:

$$\Delta H = \Delta G + T\Delta S$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master identity** of fuel cell thermodynamics. It is the most heavily tested equation in KTU Module 1.

---

### Layer 2: Second Law and Reversibility

- **Reversible (ideal) operation**: The cell produces $W_{elec} = -\Delta G$ as electricity and rejects $Q_{rev} = T\Delta S$ as heat.
- **Irreversible (real) operation**: Additional losses occur due to:
  1. **Activation overpotential** ($\eta_{act}$) — sluggish electrode kinetics.
  2. **Ohmic overpotential** ($\eta_{ohm}$) — ionic resistance of electrolyte.
  3. **Concentration overpotential** ($\eta_{conc}$) — mass-transport limitations.
  4. **Fuel crossover / internal currents**.

The actual cell voltage becomes:

$$V_{cell} = E_{rev} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$$

The actual electrical work output drops below the theoretical maximum:

$$W_{actual} = nF \cdot V_{cell} < -\Delta G$$

---

### Layer 3: Calorific Values — HHV vs LHV

The **enthalpy of combustion** of hydrogen can be expressed in two ways depending on whether the product water is considered *liquid* or *vapour*:

| Calorific Value | Symbol | Definition | Numerical Value (H₂) | Cell Voltage Equivalent |
|-----------------|--------|------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Higher Heating Value | HHV | Water produced as **liquid** | **−285.8 kJ/mol** | **1.481 V** (thermoneutral) |
| Lower Heating Value | LHV | Water produced as **vapour** | **−241.8 kJ/mol** | **1.253 V** |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU exam trap: Students often confuse whether the **theoretical maximum voltage is 1.229 V (LHV-based)** or **1.481 V (HHV-based)**. The reversible voltage **1.229 V** corresponds to $E^\circ = -\Delta G^\circ / (nF)$ using $\Delta G^\circ = -237.2$ kJ/mol. The HHV-based voltage **1.481 V** corresponds to $E_{tn} = -\Delta H_{HHV}^\circ / (nF)$ and represents the **thermoneutral voltage**.

---

## 2.2 Efficiency Relations in Fuel Cells

A fuel cell has **multiple definitions of efficiency**, each capturing a different aspect of energy conversion.

### 2.2.1 Thermodynamic (Reversible) Efficiency

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = 1 - \frac{T\Delta S}{\Delta H}$$

For the standard H₂/O₂ reaction at 298 K:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{237.2}{285.8} = 0.830 \;(\text{or } 83.0\%)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **83%** is the *upper limit* of a fuel cell's thermal efficiency. Combustion engines (Carnot) are limited by $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_{cold}/T_{hot}$, typically **30–40%**.

### 2.2.2 Voltage Efficiency

$$\eta_{V} = \frac{V_{cell}}{E_{rev}}$$

### 2.2.3 Current (Faradaic) Efficiency

$$\eta_{I} = \frac{I_{actual}}{I_{theoretical}} = 1 - \frac{I_{loss}}{I_{total}}$$

### 2.2.4 Overall Fuel Cell Electrical Efficiency

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_{V} \cdot \eta_{I}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{overall} = \frac{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{m_{fuel} \cdot LHV}}$$

### 2.2.5 Combined Heat and Power (CHP) Efficiency

$$\eta_{CHP} = \frac{P_{elec} + Q_{useful,heat}}{m_{fuel} \cdot LHV}$$

Combined efficiencies of **80–90%** are routinely achieved in stationary fuel cell CHP systems.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Typical Unit |
|---|---------|---------------|--------------|
| 1 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Master thermodynamic identity | kJ/mol |
| 2 | $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$ | Gibbs energy ↔ reversible voltage | J/mol ↔ V |
| 3 | $E^\circ = -\Delta G^\circ / (nF)$ | Theoretical open-circuit voltage | V |
| 4 | $E_{tn} = -\Delta H^\circ / (nF)$ | Thermoneutral voltage (HHV basis) | V |
| 5 | $E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln(Q)$ | Nernst equation | V |
| 6 | $\Delta S = nF \cdot \frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\big\vert_p$ | Entropy from temperature coefficient of E | J/mol·K |
| 7 | $W_{elec} = \int nFE\, d\xi$ | Reversible electrical work | J |
| 8 | $Q_{rev} = T\Delta S$ | Reversible heat rejected | J |
| 9 | $Q_{irrev} = nF(V_{rev} - V_{cell})$ | Irreversible heat (loss) | J |
| 10 | $P_{stack} = V_{stack} \times I_{stack}$ | Electrical power output | W |
| 11 | $\eta_{th} = \Delta G / \Delta H$ | Thermodynamic efficiency | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\eta_V = V_{cell} / E_{rev}$ | Voltage efficiency | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{th}\eta_V\eta_I$ | Net electrical efficiency | dimensionless |
| 14 | $W = \int_0^t P\, d\tau$ | Total energy delivered | J |
| 15 | $\dot{n}_{H_2} = I / (nF)$ | Hydrogen consumption rate | mol/s |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why These Relations Are Critical |
|--------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Automotive PEMFC Stacks** (e.g., Toyota Mirai) | Power density $P = V \cdot I$ determines vehicle range and acceleration; efficiency $\eta_{overall}$ determines hydrogen tank sizing. |
| **Stationary SOFC Power Plants** | Combined heat & power (CHP) calculation requires $\eta_{CHP}$ which combines $\Delta G$ and waste heat $T\Delta S$. |
| **Spacecraft (Apollo, Space Shuttle)** | Alkaline fuel cells sized using $\dot{n}_{H_2} = I/(nF)$ to provide both electricity and drinking water (LHV vs HHV choice). |
| **Portable Electronics (DMFC)** | Methanol consumption rate $m_{CH_3OH}$ directly determined by Faraday's law; affects battery-life claims. |
| **Grid-Scale Energy Storage** | Round-trip efficiency comparisons use $\eta_{overall}$ to benchmark fuel cells against Li-ion and pumped hydro. |
