# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Gibbs Free Energy from First Principles

We begin with the **First Law** for a closed system undergoing a reaction at constant pressure:

$$\Delta U = Q_p - W_{total}$$

At constant pressure, the system can do $P\Delta V$ expansion work *plus* non-expansion work (here, electrical work $W_{elec}$). Substituting:

$$\Delta U = Q_p - (P\Delta V + W_{elec})$$

Rearranging and recognising $Q_p = \Delta H$ (definition of enthalpy at constant pressure):

$$\Delta U + P\Delta V = \Delta H - W_{elec}$$

Using the definition $H = U + PV$:

$$\Delta H = \Delta H - W_{elec} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad W_{elec} = 0$$

This trivial result occurs because we have not yet invoked the Second Law. We now apply the **Second Law** in the form of the **Clausius inequality**:

$$\Delta S \geq \frac{Q_{rev}}{T}$$

For a **reversible** process at temperature $T$:

$$Q_{rev} = T\Delta S$$

Combining the energy balance:

$$\Delta H = Q_p = Q_{rev} + W_{elec,rev} \quad \text{(at constant T, P)}$$

Therefore:

$$W_{elec,rev} = \Delta H - Q_{rev} = \Delta H - T\Delta S$$

Defining $W_{elec,rev} = -\Delta G$ (since work *done by* the system is negative in sign convention), we obtain the canonical identity:

$$\boxed{\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **guaranteed to appear** in some form in KTU Part-A or Part-B questions. Practise writing each step with the corresponding thermodynamic law referenced.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reversible Cell Voltage from Gibbs Energy

We equate the reversible electrical work to the negative of the Gibbs free energy change:

$$W_{elec,rev} = -\Delta G$$

For an electrochemical reaction transferring $n$ moles of electrons per mole of reaction, the total charge passed is $nF$ coulombs. The work done by a cell at voltage $E$ passing this charge is:

$$W_{elec} = (nF) \cdot E$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$-\Delta G = nFE$$

Solving for $E$:

$$\boxed{E = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF}}$$

For the standard hydrogen–oxygen reaction:

$$\text{H}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$$

with $n = 2$, $\Delta G^\circ = -237.2$ kJ/mol, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol:

$$E^\circ = \frac{-(-237{,}200\,\text{J/mol})}{(2)(96{,}485\,\text{C/mol})}$$

$$E^\circ = \frac{237{,}200}{192{,}970}$$

$$\boxed{E^\circ = 1.229\;\text{V}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Nernst Equation from Gibbs Energy

The Gibbs free energy change under non-standard conditions is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln(Q)$$

where $Q$ is the reaction quotient. Substituting $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$:

$$-nFE = -nFE^\circ + RT \ln(Q)$$

Dividing both sides by $-nF$:

$$\boxed{E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln(Q)}$$

At standard temperature $T = 298.15$ K and converting $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$:

$$\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{(8.314)(298.15)}{96{,}485} = 0.02569\;\text{V}$$

Hence the common working form of the Nernst equation is:

$$E = E^\circ + \frac{0.05916}{n}\log_{10}(Q)\quad \text{(at 25 °C)}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Energy and Power for a PEMFC Stack

**Problem Statement (typical KTU numerical):**
A 50-cell PEMFC stack operates at an average cell voltage of **0.70 V** and delivers a current of **200 A**. The stack uses **hydrogen** fuel. Calculate:
1. Total stack power output (W and kW).
2. Hydrogen consumption rate (mol/s and g/s).
3. Heat generation rate (W), given $E_{rev} = 1.229$ V per cell.
4. Overall electrical efficiency ($\eta_{overall}$), using LHV = 241.8 kJ/mol.

**Step 1: Stack Voltage and Power**

$$V_{stack} = 50 \times 0.70\;\text{V} = 35.0\;\text{V}$$

$$P_{stack} = V_{stack} \times I = 35.0 \times 200 = 7000\;\text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{stack} = 7.00\;\text{kW}}$$

**Step 2: Hydrogen Consumption Rate**

From Faraday's law (with $n = 2$ electrons per H₂):

$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \frac{I}{nF} = \frac{200}{(2)(96{,}485)} = 1.0364 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{mol/s}$$

Mass flow rate (M(H₂) = 2.016 g/mol):

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = (1.0364 \times 10^{-3})(2.016) = 2.089 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{g/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{H_2} \approx 2.09\;\text{g/s} \approx 7.52\;\text{kg/h}}$$

**Step 3: Heat Generation Rate**

The electrical work per mole of H₂ actually delivered:

$$W_{elec,actual} = nF V_{cell} = (2)(96{,}485)(0.70) = 135{,}079\;\text{J/mol}$$

Total enthalpy supplied (LHV basis, water as vapour):

$$\Delta H_{LHV} = 241{,}800\;\text{J/mol}$$

Heat generated per mole of H₂:

$$Q = \Delta H - W_{elec} = 241{,}800 - 135{,}079 = 106{,}721\;\text{J/mol}$$

Heat generation rate:

$$\dot{Q} = Q \cdot \dot{n}_{H_2} = (106{,}721)(1.0364 \times 10^{-3}) = 110.6\;\text{W}$$

Wait — this is for **one cell**. For the entire 50-cell stack:

$$\dot{Q}_{stack} = 50 \times 110.6 = 5{,}530\;\text{W} = 5.53\;\text{kW}$$

We can also verify via the energy balance:

$$\text{Fuel energy in} = \dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot \Delta H = (1.0364 \times 10^{-3})(241{,}800) = 250.6\;\text{W per cell}$$

$$P_{cell} = V_{cell} \cdot I = 0.70 \times 200 = 140\;\text{W per cell}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{cell} = 250.6 - 140 = 110.6\;\text{W per cell}\;\checkmark$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{stack} = 5.53\;\text{kW}}$$

**Step 4: Overall Efficiency**

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{stack}}{\dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot \Delta H_{LHV}}$$

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{7000}{(1.0364 \times 10^{-3})(241{,}800)} = \frac{7000}{250.6} = 0.6994$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{overall} \approx 70.0\%}$$

> [!TIP]
> Always cross-check: $\eta_{overall}$ should lie between the thermodynamic limit ($\eta_{th} \approx 83\%$) and zero. A result of **70%** is realistic for a well-designed PEMFC stack.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Temperature Effect on Reversible Voltage

**Problem Statement:**
Using the temperature coefficient $\left(\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_p = -0.000845$ V/K for the H₂/O₂ reaction, calculate $E$ at **80 °C** (353 K). Given $E^\circ = 1.229$ V at 25 °C.

**Step 1: Temperature Difference**

$$\Delta T = 353 - 298 = 55\;\text{K}$$

**Step 2: Voltage Correction**

$$\Delta E = \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_p \cdot \Delta T = (-0.000845)(55) = -0.04648\;\text{V}$$

**Step 3: New Voltage**

$$E(80°C) = 1.229 - 0.04648 = 1.1825\;\text{V}$$

$$\boxed{E(80°C) \approx 1.183\;\text{V}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examination observation: reversible cell voltage **decreases** with increasing temperature. This is because $\Delta S < 0$ for the H₂/O₂ reaction (two gas molecules → one liquid), so $T\Delta S$ becomes more negative with $T$, reducing $-\Delta G$.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Energy Accounting for the H₂/O₂ Reaction

| Energy Form | Symbol | Per Mole of H₂ (Standard) | % of $\Delta H_{HHV}$ |
|-------------|--------|----------------------------|----------------------|
| Total chemical (HHV) | $\Delta H$ | 285.8 kJ | 100% |
| Reversible electrical | $\Delta G$ | 237.2 kJ | 83.0% |
| Reversible heat | $T\Delta S$ | 48.6 kJ | 17.0% |
| Actual electrical (70% efficiency) | $W_{actual}$ | 200.1 kJ | 70.0% |
| Irreversible loss (overpotentials) | $W_{loss}$ | 37.1 kJ | 13.0% |
| Total heat rejected | $Q_{total}$ | 85.7 kJ | 30.0% |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Flow Diagram in a Generic Fuel Cell

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates how the chemical energy of the fuel–oxidant couple is partitioned into useful electricity, reversible heat, and irreversible losses as the reactants pass through the cell.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Fuel Inlet H2"] --> M1["Anode Electrode"]
    B["Oxidant Inlet O2"] --> M2["Cathode Electrode"]
    M1 --> R["Electrochemical Reaction Zone"]
    M2 --> R
    R --> E1["ΔG = nFE Reversible Electrical Work"]
    R --> E2["TΔS Reversible Heat"]
    R --> E3["ηact ηohm ηconc Irreversible Loss"]
    E1 --> P["DC Power Output V x I"]
    E2 --> Q1["Waste Heat at Tcell"]
    E3 --> Q2["Additional Waste Heat"]
    P --> L["External Load Motor or Grid"]
    Q1 --> C["Coolant Loop"]
    Q2 --> C
    C --> H["Combined Heat and Power CHP"]
    R --> W["Water Out and Depleted Fuel Out"]
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style B fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#663300
    style P fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600
    style H fill:#ffd1dc,stroke:#660033
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **two inlet streams** ($H_2$ and $O_2$) enter the cell carrying the total chemical potential $\Delta H$.
- At the **reaction zone**, this enthalpy is partitioned into **three** sub-streams: reversible electrical work ($\Delta G$), reversible heat ($T\Delta S$), and irreversible loss terms.
- The **electrical work** stream exits via the external circuit as $P = VI$ to the load.
- Both the **reversible** and **irreversible** heat streams are captured by the **coolant loop** and may be exploited for CHP applications.
- The **product stream** (water + depleted gases) leaves the cell, completing the mass balance.

---

## 4.2 Thermodynamic Cascade Diagram — Energy Transformation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1["Energy Input Stage"]
        I1["Chemical Energy of Fuel ΔH HHV"]
    end
    subgraph S2["Conversion Stage Inside Fuel Cell"]
        C1["Maximum Reversible Work ΔG"]
        C2["Reversible Heat TΔS Mandatory"]
        C3["Irreversible Losses ηact ηohm ηconc"]
    end
    subgraph S3["Output Stage"]
        O1["Useful Electrical Power P = V x I"]
        O2["Recoverable Heat Qdot Qr Qirr"]
        O3["Exhaust Products H2O CO2"]
    end
    I1 --> C1
    I1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C3 --> O1
    C2 --> O2
    C3 --> O2
    I1 --> O3
    style I1 fill:#ffe680,stroke:#665500
    style O1 fill:#80ff80,stroke:#006600
    style O2 fill:#ffaa80,stroke:#aa3300
```

**Engineering interpretation:**
- The cascade visually enforces the **First Law**: what enters as $\Delta H$ must leave as $P + Q + \text{products}$.
- Note that the **reversible heat** ($T\Delta S$) is *unavoidable* even for an *ideal* fuel cell — students frequently forget this in KTU derivations.

---

## 4.3 Power–Voltage–Current Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph stack["Fuel Cell Stack Architecture"]
        V1["Vcell 1"] --> V2["Vcell 2"] --> V3["..."] --> Vn["Vcell N"]
    end
    stack --> Pcalc["P = N x Vcell x I"]
    Igen["Load Current I = nF x dnH2/dt"] --> stack
    Pcalc --> Pout["Pstack kW Output"]
    Vcell["Actual Vcell"] --> E1["Erev 1.229 V"]
    Vcell --> E2["Activation Loss"]
    Vcell --> E3["Ohmic Loss"]
    Vcell --> E4["Concentration Loss"]
    E2 --> E5["Polarisation Curve V vs I"]
    E3 --> E5
    E4 --> E5
    style Pout fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600
    style E5 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#803300
```

**Reading guide for KTU students:**
- The **polarisation curve** ($V$ vs $I$) is the *experimental fingerprint* of every fuel cell.
- Three regions of loss (activation, ohmic, concentration) define the departure from $E_{rev}$.
- The **maximum power point** of the stack is found at the *knee* between ohmic and concentration regions.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define Gibbs free energy and explain its significance in the context of fuel cell operation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Gibbs free energy ($G$) is a thermodynamic state function defined as $G = H - TS$, where $H$ is enthalpy, $T$ is absolute temperature, and $S$ is entropy. For a chemical reaction, the change in Gibbs free energy is given by $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$.

**Significance for fuel cells (2 marks):**
1. $\Delta G$ represents the **maximum reversible electrical work** obtainable from an electrochemical reaction: $W_{elec,rev} = -\Delta G = nFE$.
2. It determines the **theoretical reversible cell voltage**: $E_{rev} = -\Delta G / (nF)$. For the standard H₂/O₂ cell, $E^\circ = 1.229$ V.
3. The difference between $\Delta G$ and $\Delta H$ quantifies the **irreversible heat loss** ($T\Delta S$) that is unavoidable even in an ideal cell.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse the **sign convention** of $\Delta G$. A *spontaneous* reaction has **negative** $\Delta G$, and the cell does *positive* electrical work. Always write: "$W_{elec} = -\Delta G$".

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Distinguish between Higher Heating Value (HHV) and Lower Heating Value (LHV) of a fuel. How does this distinction affect the theoretical efficiency of a hydrogen fuel cell?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | HHV | LHV |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Definition | Heat released when combustion products include **liquid water** | Heat released when combustion products include **water vapour** |
| Value for H₂ | **285.8 kJ/mol** | **241.8 kJ/mol** |
| Cell voltage equivalent | $E_{tn} = 1.481$ V | $E^\circ = 1.253$ V |

**Effect on theoretical efficiency (1 mark):**
- Using **HHV**: $\eta_{th,HHV} = \Delta G / \Delta H_{HHV} = 237.2 / 285.8 = 0.830 = 83.0\%$
- Using **LHV**: $\eta_{th,LHV} = \Delta G / \Delta H_{LHV} = 237.2 / 241.8 = 0.981 = 98.1\%$
- **The choice of HHV vs LHV therefore drastically changes the reported efficiency** of a fuel cell. Automotive applications typically use **LHV** because the exhaust water is in vapour form.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Reporting fuel cell efficiency >100% by mixing LHV-based efficiency with HHV-based energy input. This is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics, derive the relation $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ and show that the maximum reversible electrical work obtainable from a fuel cell reaction is $W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G = nFE$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A proton-exchange-membrane fuel cell (PEMFC) stack has **120 cells** in series. At an operating point, the stack delivers a current of **250 A** at an average cell voltage of **0.68 V**. Using the lower heating value of hydrogen (**LHV = 241.8 kJ/mol**) and given $E_{rev} = 1.229$ V per cell:
- (i) Calculate the stack power output and overall electrical efficiency.
- (ii) Compute the rate of hydrogen consumption (kg/h) and the rate of heat rejection (kW).
- (iii) If 25% of the rejected heat is recovered for cogeneration, find the **CHP efficiency**.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 [1 mark] — First Law for closed system:**
At constant pressure, with heat $Q_p$ supplied and work $W$ done by the system:

$$\Delta U = Q_p - W$$

**Step 2 [1 mark] — Enthalpy substitution:**
Recognising $Q_p = \Delta H$ at constant pressure and writing $W = P\Delta V + W_{elec}$:

$$\Delta U = \Delta H - P\Delta V - W_{elec}$$

Since $\Delta H = \Delta U + P\Delta V$, the first two terms cancel, leaving:

$$0 = 0 - W_{elec} \quad \text{(trivial without 2nd Law)}$$

**Step 3 [2 marks] — Second Law (Clausius):**
For a reversible process: $\Delta S = Q_{rev}/T$, hence $Q_{rev} = T\Delta S$.

**Step 4 [2 marks] — Combine laws:**
At constant $T$, $P$, the heat absorbed equals the reversible heat:

$$\Delta H = Q_{rev} + W_{elec,rev} = T\Delta S + W_{elec,rev}$$

Therefore:

$$W_{elec,rev} = \Delta H - T\Delta S$$

**Step 5 [1 mark] — Definition of $\Delta G$:**
Defining $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ and noting that work done *by* the system is negative in thermodynamic convention:

$$\boxed{W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G = nFE}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 [1 mark] — Stack voltage and power:**

$$V_{stack} = N_{cells} \cdot V_{cell} = 120 \times 0.68 = 81.6\;\text{V}$$

$$P_{stack} = V_{stack} \cdot I = 81.6 \times 250 = 20{,}400\;\text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{stack} = 20.4\;\text{kW}}$$

**Step 2 [1 mark] — Hydrogen molar flow rate (Faraday's Law):**

$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \frac{I}{nF} = \frac{250}{(2)(96{,}485)} = 1.2955 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{mol/s}$$

$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = 1.2955 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 4.664\;\text{mol/h}$$

Mass flow (M(H₂) = 2.016 g/mol):

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 4.664 \times 2.016 = 9.402\;\text{g/h}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{H_2} \approx 9.40\;\text{g/h} \approx 0.0094\;\text{kg/h}}$$

**Step 3 [2 marks] — Heat rejection rate:**

Energy input rate (LHV basis):

$$\dot{E}_{in} = \dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot \Delta H_{LHV} = (1.2955 \times 10^{-3})(241{,}800) = 313.27\;\text{W per cell}$$

For 120 cells:

$$\dot{E}_{in,stack} = 313.27 \times 120 = 37{,}592\;\text{W} = 37.59\;\text{kW}$$

By energy balance:

$$\dot{Q}_{reject} = \dot{E}_{in,stack} - P_{stack} = 37{,}592 - 20{,}400 = 17{,}192\;\text{W}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{reject} \approx 17.19\;\text{kW}}$$

**Step 4 [2 marks] — Overall and CHP efficiencies:**

Overall electrical efficiency:

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{stack}}{\dot{E}_{in,stack}} = \frac{20{,}400}{37{,}592} = 0.5426$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{overall} = 54.3\%}$$

Recovered heat:

$$\dot{Q}_{recovered} = 0.25 \times 17{,}192 = 4{,}298\;\text{W}$$

CHP efficiency:

$$\eta_{CHP} = \frac{P_{stack} + \dot{Q}_{recovered}}{\dot{E}_{in,stack}} = \frac{20{,}400 + 4{,}298}{37{,}592} = 0.6570$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{CHP} \approx 65.7\%}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Nernst equation** $E = E^\circ - (RT/nF)\ln Q$ for a fuel cell, starting from the relation between Gibbs free energy and reaction quotient. Using this equation, calculate the cell voltage at 80 °C for an H₂/O₂ fuel cell supplied with hydrogen at **3 atm** and oxygen at **1 atm**, producing water vapour at **1 atm**. Given $E^\circ = 1.229$ V at 25 °C.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A solid-oxide fuel cell (SOFC) operates at **1000 K**. The reversible cell voltage at standard conditions (1 atm all species) is **$E^\circ = 1.0$ V**. The standard entropy change is $\Delta S^\circ = -50$ J/mol·K. Calculate:
- (i) The standard Gibbs free energy change $\Delta G^\circ$ and enthalpy change $\Delta H^\circ$.
- (ii) The thermoneutral voltage $E_{tn}$ at 1000 K.
- (iii) The reversible heat released per mole of reaction.
- (iv) The voltage efficiency if the actual operating voltage is **0.75 V**.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Nernst Derivation & Calculation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 [1 mark] — Free energy dependence on composition:**

For a reaction with reaction quotient $Q$:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln(Q)$$

**Step 2 [2 marks] — Substitute electrochemistry relations:**
Using $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$:

$$-nFE = -nFE^\circ + RT \ln(Q)$$

**Step 3 [1 mark] — Rearrange:**

$$\boxed{E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln(Q)}$$

**Step 4 [2 marks] — Set up reaction quotient for H₂/O₂ (water vapour):**

$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(g)}$$

$$Q = \frac{P_{H_2O}}{(P_{H_2})(P_{O_2})^{1/2}}$$

With $P_{H_2} = 3$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 1$ atm, $P_{H_2O} = 1$ atm:

$$Q = \frac{1}{(3)(1)^{1/2}} = \frac{1}{3} = 0.333$$

**Step 5 [1 mark] — Evaluate voltage at 80 °C (353 K):**

$$E = 1.229 - \frac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96{,}485)}\ln\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)$$

$$\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{2936.8}{192{,}970} = 0.01522\;\text{V}$$

$$\ln(1/3) = -1.0986$$

$$E = 1.229 - (0.01522)(-1.0986) = 1.229 + 0.01672 = 1.2457\;\text{V}$$

$$\boxed{E(80°C) \approx 1.246\;\text{V}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students incorrectly substitute $P_{O_2}$ without raising it to the power $\frac{1}{2}$ corresponding to the **stoichiometric coefficient** in the balanced equation. This is a guaranteed **1-mark cut** in KTU Part-B valuation.

---

#### Part (b) — SOFC Numerical [7 Marks]

**Step 1 [1 mark] — Standard Gibbs free energy:**

$$\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ = -(2)(96{,}485)(1.0) = -192{,}970\;\text{J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G^\circ = -192.97\;\text{kJ/mol}}$$

**Step 2 [2 marks] — Enthalpy from Gibbs–Helmholtz relation:**

From $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$:

$$\Delta H^\circ = \Delta G^\circ + T\Delta S^\circ = -192{,}970 + (1000)(-50)$$

$$\Delta H^\circ = -192{,}970 - 50{,}000 = -242{,}970\;\text{J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H^\circ = -242.97\;\text{kJ/mol}}$$

**Step 3 [1 mark] — Thermoneutral voltage:**

$$E_{tn} = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{nF} = \frac{242{,}970}{(2)(96{,}485)} = 1.259\;\text{V}$$

$$\boxed{E_{tn} \approx 1.259\;\text{V}}$$

**Step 4 [1.5 marks] — Reversible heat per mole:**

$$Q_{rev} = T\Delta S^\circ = (1000)(-50) = -50{,}000\;\text{J/mol}$$

The negative sign indicates heat is **released** by the cell at 1000 K:

$$\boxed{Q_{rev} = 50.0\;\text{kJ/mol (released)}}$$

**Step 5 [1.5 marks] — Voltage efficiency:**

$$\eta_V = \frac{V_{cell}}{E_{rev}} = \frac{0.75}{1.0} = 0.75$$

$$\boxed{\eta_V = 75.0\%}$$

---

## 5.3 Examiner's General Valuation Warnings for Energy and Power Relations

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark-Loss Hotspots Identified from KTU Past Papers:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention (lose up to 1 mark):** Always explicitly state that spontaneous reactions have **negative $\Delta G$** and that the cell does **positive** electrical work.
> 2. **HHV vs LHV (lose up to 2 marks):** State which value is being used and justify the choice (e.g., "since the cell operates above 100 °C, water leaves as vapour; hence we adopt LHV").
> 3. **Units (lose 0.5 mark each):** Always carry units through numerical calculations: J/mol, kW, kg/h, V, A.
> 4. **Faraday's Law (lose 1 mark):** Use $\dot{n}_{H_2} = I/(nF)$, not $I/F$. The factor $n = 2$ is critical.
> 5. **Energy Balance Closure (lose 1–2 marks):** Always finish numerical problems by verifying $\dot{E}_{in} = P + \dot{Q}$.
> 6. **Stoichiometric Coefficients in Nernst $Q$ (lose 1 mark):** Remember to raise partial pressures to the powers of their stoichiometric coefficients.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Energy and Power Relations:**

- ☐ **Master Identity:** $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ — the single most important equation of Module 1.
- ☐ **Electrical Work Link:** $W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G = nFE_{rev}$ — derivable in 3 lines.
- ☐ **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol — memorise to **4 significant figures**.
- ☐ **Standard Hydrogen–Oxygen Cell:** $E^\circ = 1.229$ V (LHV-equivalent of $\Delta G^\circ = -237.2$ kJ/mol).
- ☐ **Thermoneutral Voltage:** $E_{tn} = 1.481$ V (HHV basis, $\Delta H = -285.8$ kJ/mol).
- ☐ **Nernst Equation:** $E = E^\circ - (RT/nF)\ln Q$, or $E = E^\circ + (0.05916/n)\log_{10} Q$ at 25 °C.
- ☐ **Temperature Coefficient:** $\left(\partial E/\partial T\right)_p = \Delta S / (nF) < 0$ for H₂/O₂ — reversible voltage *decreases* with $T$.
- ☐ **Hydrogen Consumption:** $\dot{n}_{H_2} = I/(nF)$ and $\dot{m}_{H_2} = M_{H_2} \cdot I \cdot t / (nF)$.
- ☐ **Stack Power:** $P_{stack} = N_{cells} \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I$.
- ☐ **Energy Efficiency Definitions:**
  - Thermodynamic: $\eta_{th} = \Delta G / \Delta H$
  - Voltage: $\eta_V = V_{cell} / E_{rev}$
  - Overall: $\eta_{overall} = P_{out} / (\dot{n}_{fuel} \cdot \text{LHV})$
  - CHP: $\eta_{CHP} = (P_{elec} + Q_{recovered}) / (\dot{n}_{fuel} \cdot \text{LHV})$
- ☐ **Reversible Heat (unavoidable):** $Q_{rev} = T\Delta S = \Delta H - \Delta G$.
- ☐ **Irreversible Heat (losses):** $Q_{irr} = nF(E_{rev} - V_{cell})$ per mole.
- ☐ **Energy Balance:** $\dot{E}_{fuel} = P_{elec} + \dot{Q}_{reject} + \dot{E}_{exhaust}$ (always verify).
- ☐ **HHV vs LHV Trap:** Choose LHV when exhaust water is vapour (T > 100 °C); HHV for low-T applications.
- ☐ **Units Hygiene:** kJ/mol for energies, V for voltages, A for currents, kW for power, kg/h for mass flow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Units
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT721 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Units

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Units in Fuel Cell Technology

In the context of **Fuel Cell Technology**, *units* refer to the standardized, internationally accepted physical quantities (primarily derived from the **Système International d'Unités — SI Units**) that quantify the electrical, electrochemical, thermal, fluidic, and energetic performance of a fuel cell system. KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 mandates that every student must be fluent in expressing fuel cell performance in correct SI units, derived units, and conventional engineering shorthand.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Units* are the fundamental and derived physical quantities that form the quantitative language of fuel cell engineering — covering voltage (V), current (A), current density (A/cm²), power density (W/cm²), energy density (Wh/kg), specific consumption (g/kWh), and efficiency (%).

The principal system of units used is the **SI (International System of Units)**, supplemented by practical engineering units such as **ASR (Ω·cm²)**, **LHV (Lower Heating Value)**, and **HHV (Higher Heating Value)**.

## 1.2 Fundamental vs Derived Units Relevant to Fuel Cells

| Category | Quantity | SI Unit | Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fundamental | Length | metre | m |
| Fundamental | Mass | kilogram | kg |
| Fundamental | Time | second | s |
| Fundamental | Electric Current | ampere | A |
| Fundamental | Temperature | kelvin | K |
| Fundamental | Amount of substance | mole | mol |
| Derived | Voltage / Potential | volt | V |
| Derived | Power | watt | W |
| Derived | Energy | joule | J |
| Derived | Charge | coulomb | C |
| Derived | Resistance | ohm | Ω |
| Derived | Pressure | pascal | Pa |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy (Water-Pipe Model):**
> Imagine electricity as **water flowing through a pipe**:
> - **Voltage (V)** = water *pressure* pushing electrons
> - **Current (A)** = water *flow rate*
> - **Power (W)** = total *useful work* done by water (Pressure × Flow)
> - **Energy (J or Wh)** = total *volume of work* over time
> - **Resistance (Ω)** = pipe *narrowness*
> - **Current Density (A/cm²)** = flow *concentrated* per unit pipe cross-section
> - **Power Density (W/cm²)** = power delivered per *active area* of the cell
>
> Just as a thin pipe (high resistance) reduces flow, a poor membrane or slow catalyst reduces current in a fuel cell. This is why **current density** and **power density** are the *true* benchmarks of cell performance — they normalize away the size of the cell, allowing fair comparison.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Conditions

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Conditions used in Fuel Cell Data Sheets:**
> - **STP (Standard Temperature & Pressure):** 0 °C (273.15 K), 1 atm (101.325 kPa)
> - **SATP (Standard Ambient Temperature & Pressure):** 25 °C (298.15 K), 1 atm
> - **NTP (Normal Temperature & Pressure):** 20 °C (293.15 K), 1 atm

The physical constants that appear most frequently in fuel cell unit conversions are:

- **Faraday's constant, F = 96,485 C/mol** (or A·s/mol)
- **Universal gas constant, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Avogadro's number, Nₐ = 6.022 × 10²³ /mol**

## 1.5 Why Units Matter in Fuel Cells

A single fuel cell generates only about **0.5 – 1.0 V** under load. To produce usable power, cells are **stacked** — but to compare different stacks fairly, we must express output *per unit area* or *per unit mass* of fuel consumed. This is why units like **A/cm²**, **W/cm²**, **g H₂/kWh**, and **kg H₂/MW·h** are the *currency* of fuel cell engineering.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Quantities Explained

### 2.1.1 Voltage (V) — The Driving Force
- **Symbol:** V or E
- **Unit:** Volt = J/C
- **KTU Concept:** Open-circuit voltage (OCV) is the maximum theoretical voltage when no current flows. Under load, voltage drops due to **activation, ohmic, and concentration losses**.
- Theoretical max for H₂/O₂ cell: **E⁰ = 1.229 V (LHV basis) at 25 °C, 1 atm**.
- For a stack with *n* cells in series: V_stack = n × V_cell.

### 2.1.2 Current (I) and Current Density (j)
- **Current, I** = rate of electron flow, measured in **amperes (A)**.
- **Current density, j** = I / A_active, where A_active is the active electrode area in **cm²** or **m²**.
- **Unit:** A/cm² (most common in PEMFC literature) or A/m².
- **Why normalize?** A bigger electrode naturally gives more current. Dividing by area isolates the *quality* of the catalyst/membrane.

### 2.1.3 Power (P) and Power Density (P_density)
- **Power, P** = V × I, unit **watt (W)**.
- **Power density, p** = P / A_active, unit **W/cm²** (or kW/m² for stacks).
- **Energy, E** = ∫ P dt, unit **joule (J)** or **watt-hour (Wh)**.
  - Conversion: **1 Wh = 3,600 J**.

### 2.1.4 Resistance, Area-Specific Resistance (ASR)
- **Resistance, R** = V / I, unit **ohm (Ω)**.
- **ASR** = R × A_active, unit **Ω·cm²**. This is the *single most useful* metric for membrane evaluation.
- Lower ASR → better membrane/electrode performance.

### 2.1.5 Efficiency (η)
- **Electrical efficiency, ηₑ** = (Useful electrical energy out) / (Energy content of fuel in).
- **Unit:** dimensionless, expressed in **%**.
- **ηₑ = (V_cell / E_thermo) × 100 %**, where E_thermo is the thermoneutral voltage (**1.481 V HHV** or **1.229 V LHV**).

### 2.1.6 Fuel Consumption Rate
- **Specific hydrogen consumption:** g H₂ / kWh.
- **Volumetric consumption:** kg H₂ / MW·h.
- Derived from **Faraday's Law**: ṁ_H₂ = (M_H₂ × I) / (n × F), where M_H₂ = 2.016 g/mol, n = 2 electrons per H₂.

### 2.1.7 Energy Density vs Specific Energy
- **Specific energy** = Energy / Mass, unit **Wh/kg**.
- **Energy density** = Energy / Volume, unit **Wh/L**.
- H₂ (gaseous, 200 bar) ≈ **600 Wh/kg** but only **~400 Wh/L**.
- H₂ (liquid) ≈ **2,400 Wh/kg** mass basis.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — High-Yield Unit-Based Formulas for KTU Fuel Cell Calculations**

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power | P = V × I | W (watt) | V in volts, I in amps |
| 2 | Energy | E = P × t | J or Wh | 1 Wh = 3,600 J |
| 3 | Current density | j = I / A | A/m² or A/cm² | A = active area |
| 4 | Power density | p = V × j | W/m² or W/cm² | per unit active area |
| 5 | Stack voltage | V_stack = n × V_cell | V | n = no. of cells |
| 6 | ASR | ASR = R × A | Ω·m² or Ω·cm² | membrane metric |
| 7 | Efficiency (electrical) | η = (V_cell / E_thermo) × 100 | % | E_thermo = 1.229 V (LHV) |
| 8 | H₂ consumption (mass) | ṁ = (M·I) / (n·F) | kg/s | n = 2 for H₂ |
| 9 | H₂ consumption (specific) | m_sp = ṁ / P | g/kWh | divide by power |
| 10 | Charge | Q = I × t | C (coulomb) | = A·s |
| 11 | Moles reacted | n_mol = Q / (n_e·F) | mol | Faraday's law |
| 12 | Energy density (vol) | E_v = E / V | Wh/L or J/m³ | volume-based |
| 13 | Specific energy | E_m = E / m | Wh/kg or J/kg | mass-based |
| 14 | Open-circuit voltage | E₀ = (ΔG / nF) | V | ΔG = Gibbs free energy |
| 15 | Thermoneutral V | E_th = (ΔH / nF) | V | ΔH = enthalpy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Cheat Codes:**
> - 1 A/cm² = 10,000 A/m²
> - 1 W/cm² = 10,000 W/m² = 10 kW/m²
> - 1 Ω·cm² = 0.0001 Ω·m² = 10⁻⁴ Ω·m²
> - 1 Wh = 3.6 kJ = 3,600 J
> - 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar
> - 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These units are *not academic abstractions*. They appear on every fuel cell datasheet, in every auto-manufacturer spec (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO), and in standards like **ISO 14687** (hydrogen fuel quality) and **SAE J2719**. When an OEM says "the stack delivers 128 kW at 650 V," the engineer must immediately compute: current = 128,000 / 650 ≈ **197 A**; if the active area is 250 cm², current density ≈ **0.79 A/cm²** — well within safe PEMFC operating range (0.5–1.5 A/cm² typical). This is the day-to-day engineering calculus of fuel cells.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrogen Mass Consumption Rate from Current

**Given:** A PEM fuel cell delivers a steady current I amperes.

**Find:** Mass flow rate of H₂ consumed (kg/s) and specific consumption (g/kWh).

### Step-by-Step (Exhaustive)

**Step 1 — Recall the half-reaction at the anode:**
$$
\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-
$$

**Step 2 — Identify electrons per H₂ molecule:** n_e = 2.

**Step 3 — Total charge per mole of H₂ (Faraday's Law):**
$$
Q_{mol} = n_e \cdot F
$$
$$
Q_{mol} = 2 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970 \text{ C/mol}
$$

**Step 4 — Moles of H₂ reacted per second:**
$$
\dot{n} = \frac{I}{Q_{mol}} = \frac{I}{n_e \cdot F}
$$

**Step 5 — Mass flow rate of H₂ (using M_H₂ = 2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol):**
$$
\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dot{n} \cdot M_{H_2} = \frac{I \cdot M_{H_2}}{n_e \cdot F}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute numerical values to get kg/s:**
$$
\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{I \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 96{,}485}
$$
$$
\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{I \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{192{,}970}
$$
$$
\dot{m}_{H_2} \approx I \times 1.045 \times 10^{-8} \text{ kg/s per ampere}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to specific consumption (g/kWh):**
$$
m_{sp} = \frac{\dot{m}_{H_2} \text{ (g/s)}}{P \text{ (kW)}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{H_2} \times 3600}{P} \text{ g/kWh}
$$

For a real cell at V_cell = 0.7 V (so P = 0.7 × I W):
$$
m_{sp} = \frac{I \times 1.045 \times 10^{-5} \times 3600}{0.7 \times I / 1000}
$$
$$
m_{sp} = \frac{0.03762}{0.0007} = 53.7 \text{ g/kWh (at 0.7 V)}
$$

**Step 8 — The ideal (reversible) value at V_cell = 1.229 V:**
$$
m_{sp,ideal} = \frac{0.03762}{0.001229} \approx 30.6 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The ratio (30.6 / 53.7) = 57 % gives the electrical efficiency at 0.7 V, which matches η = 0.7 / 1.229 = 56.96 %. Always use **LHV = 1.229 V** for transport applications since exhaust water is vapor.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Density from Current Density and Polarization Curve

**Given:** Polarization equation: V_cell = E₀ − a · ln(j) − R · j

**Find:** Maximum power density p_max.

**Step 1 — Expression for power density:**
$$
p(j) = V_{cell}(j) \times j
$$
$$
p(j) = (E_0 - a \ln j - R \cdot j) \cdot j
$$
$$
p(j) = E_0 \cdot j - a \cdot j \cdot \ln j - R \cdot j^2
$$

**Step 2 — To find maximum, differentiate w.r.t. j and set to zero:**
$$
\frac{dp}{dj} = E_0 - a(\ln j + 1) - 2Rj = 0
$$

**Step 3 — This transcendental equation has no closed form, but the unit of p is W/cm² and j is A/cm²** — confirming the derived unit:
$$
\frac{\text{V} \times \text{A}}{\text{cm}^2} = \frac{\text{W}}{\text{cm}^2}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Conversion Between W/cm² and kW/m²

**Step 1 — Use SI prefixes:**
$$
1 \frac{\text{W}}{\text{cm}^2} = 1 \frac{\text{W}}{(10^{-2} \text{m})^2} = 1 \frac{\text{W}}{10^{-4} \text{m}^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Invert:**
$$
= 10^4 \frac{\text{W}}{\text{m}^2} = 10 \frac{\text{kW}}{\text{m}^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Hence:**
$$
\boxed{1 \text{ W/cm}^2 = 10 \text{ kW/m}^2}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Unit Conversion Reference

| From | To | Multiply By |
|---|---|---|
| 1 A/cm² | A/m² | 10,000 |
| 1 W/cm² | W/m² | 10,000 |
| 1 mA/cm² | A/cm² | 0.001 |
| 1 Ω·cm² | Ω·m² | 1 × 10⁻⁴ |
| 1 kW | W | 1,000 |
| 1 kWh | MJ | 3.6 |
| 1 atm | kPa | 101.325 |
| 1 bar | kPa | 100 |
| 1 Wh/kg | J/kg | 3,600 |
| 1 g/kWh (H₂) | kg/MWh | 1.0 (same numerically) |
| 1 ppm | mg/kg | 1 (same numerically) |
| 25 °C | K | 298.15 (add 273.15) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Unit Conversion Flow in Fuel Cell Performance

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Fuel Cell System Input] --> B[Fuel H2 Mass Flow kg per s]
    A --> C[Oxidant Air Mass Flow kg per s]
    A --> D[Current I in Amperes]
    D --> E[Current Density j A per cm2]
    D --> F[Power P in Watts]
    F --> G[Power Density p W per cm2]
    F --> H[Energy E in Wh or J]
    H --> I[Specific Energy Wh per kg]
    H --> J[Energy Density Wh per L]
    D --> K[Hydrogen Consumption g per kWh]
    D --> L[Efficiency percent]
    B --> K
    F --> L
    E --> G
    F --> E
    E --> M[ASR Ohm cm2]
    M --> N[Membrane Quality Benchmark]
    L --> O[KTU Performance Score]
    G --> O
    K --> O
    I --> O
    J --> O
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Hierarchy of SI Units Used in Fuel Cells

```mermaid
graph LR
    F1[Length m] --> D1[Area m2]
    F2[Mass kg] --> D2[Energy J]
    F3[Time s] --> D2
    F4[Current A] --> D3[Charge C]
    F3 --> D3
    F5[Temperature K] --> D4[Thermal Energy J]
    D3 --> D5[Faradays Law mol]
    F1 --> D6[Volume m3]
    D1 --> D6
    D2 --> D7[Power W]
    F4 --> D7
    D5 --> D8[Hydrogen Mass Rate kg per s]
    D7 --> D9[Power Density W per m2]
    D1 --> D9
    D2 --> D10[Specific Energy Wh per kg]
    F2 --> D10
    D2 --> D11[Energy Density Wh per m3]
    D6 --> D11
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Energy Conversion Path with Units

```mermaid
graph TD
    H2[Hydrogen H2] -->|Lower Heating Value MJ per kg| CHEM[Chemical Energy]
    CHEM -->|n F = 192970 C per mol| ELEC[Electrical Energy]
    ELEC -->|V times I| P[Power W]
    P -->|integrate over time| E[TOTAL ENERGY Wh]
    E -->|divide by mass of system| SE[SPECIFIC ENERGY Wh per kg]
    E -->|divide by volume of system| ED[ENERGY DENSITY Wh per L]
    P -->|divide by area A| PD[POWER DENSITY W per cm2]
    CHEM -->|eta times CHEM| USEFUL[USEFUL ELECTRICAL OUT]
    USEFUL -->|divide by CHEM| EFF[EFFICIENCY percent]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the following terms with their SI units: (i) Current density, (ii) Power density, (iii) Area-specific resistance.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Current density (j):** The electric current flowing per unit active electrode area.
- **Definition:** j = I / A, where I = current in amperes (A) and A = active electrode area.
- **SI Unit:** **A/m²** (commonly also expressed as **A/cm²**, where 1 A/cm² = 10,000 A/m²).

**(ii) Power density (p):** The electrical power output per unit active electrode area.
- **Definition:** p = P / A = (V × j) / 1 = V × j.
- **SI Unit:** **W/m²** (commonly also expressed as **W/cm²**, where 1 W/cm² = 10 kW/m²).

**(iii) Area-Specific Resistance (ASR):** The resistance of a component normalized by its cross-sectional area; a key metric for membrane performance.
- **Definition:** ASR = R × A.
- **SI Unit:** **Ω·m²** (commonly **Ω·cm²**, where 1 Ω·cm² = 10⁻⁴ Ω·m²).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**State Faraday's constant and universal gas constant with their values and SI units. Why are they important in fuel cell calculations?**

**Model Answer:**

**Faraday's constant (F):** The magnitude of electric charge per mole of electrons.
- **Value:** F = **96,485 C/mol** (or A·s/mol)
- **SI Unit of C/mol:** A·s/mol
- **Importance:** Used to convert between charge passed and moles of fuel (H₂) consumed; the foundation of electrochemical stoichiometry.

**Universal gas constant (R):** The constant of proportionality in the ideal gas equation PV = nRT.
- **Value:** R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **SI Unit:** J/(mol·K)
- **Importance:** Used in Nernst equation to compute the temperature- and pressure-dependent cell potential.

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection Formula:** E = E⁰ + (RT/nF) · ln(P_H₂ · P_O₂^{0.5}) — both constants appear together in the Nernst equation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for **hydrogen mass consumption rate (kg/s)** in terms of stack current I, and hence obtain the **specific hydrogen consumption in g/kWh** when a cell operates at 0.65 V. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PEMFC stack has **50 cells** in series. Each cell has an active area of **200 cm²** and operates at **0.70 V** and **0.6 A/cm²**. Calculate: (i) total stack power, (ii) total stack current, (iii) power density per cell, (iv) hydrogen consumption rate in kg/h. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of H₂ Mass Consumption Rate**

**Step 1 — Half-reaction at anode:**
$$
\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-
$$
Each H₂ molecule releases n_e = 2 electrons.

**Step 2 — Charge per mole of H₂ (Faraday's Law):**
$$
Q_{mol} = n_e \cdot F = 2 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970 \text{ C/mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Moles of H₂ per second:**
$$
\dot{n} = \frac{I}{Q_{mol}} = \frac{I}{192{,}970}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass rate (M_H₂ = 2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol):**
$$
\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{I \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{192{,}970} = 1.045 \times 10^{-8} \cdot I \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Power at 0.65 V:**
$$
P = V \cdot I = 0.65 \cdot I \text{ W} = \frac{0.65 \cdot I}{1000} \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Specific consumption (g/kWh):**
$$
m_{sp} = \frac{\dot{m}_{H_2} \times 3600 \times 1000 \text{ (g)}}{P \text{ (kW)}}
$$
$$
m_{sp} = \frac{1.045 \times 10^{-8} \cdot I \times 3.6 \times 10^6}{0.65 \cdot I / 1000} = \frac{0.0376}{0.00065}
$$
$$
\boxed{m_{sp} \approx 57.9 \text{ g/kWh}}
$$

**Assumptions:** Pure H₂, 100 % Faradaic efficiency, 1 atm pressure, all reacted H₂ produces current.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation**

**Given:** n = 50 cells, A = 200 cm², V_cell = 0.70 V, j = 0.6 A/cm²

**(i) Total stack power:**
$$
P_{stack} = n \cdot V_{cell} \cdot (j \cdot A) = 50 \times 0.70 \times (0.6 \times 200)
$$
$$
P_{stack} = 50 \times 0.70 \times 120 = 50 \times 84 = 4{,}200 \text{ W}
$$
$$
\boxed{P_{stack} = 4.2 \text{ kW}}
$$

**(ii) Total stack current:**
$$
I_{stack} = j \cdot A = 0.6 \times 200 = 120 \text{ A}
$$
$$
\boxed{I_{stack} = 120 \text{ A}}
$$

**(iii) Power density per cell:**
$$
p_{cell} = V_{cell} \cdot j = 0.70 \times 0.6 = 0.42 \text{ W/cm}^2
$$
$$
\boxed{p_{cell} = 0.42 \text{ W/cm}^2 = 4.2 \text{ kW/m}^2}
$$

**(iv) H₂ consumption rate:**
$$
\dot{m}_{H_2} = 1.045 \times 10^{-8} \times 120 = 1.254 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/s}
$$
Convert to kg/h:
$$
\dot{m}_{H_2} = 1.254 \times 10^{-6} \times 3600 = 4.51 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/h}
$$
$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{H_2} \approx 4.51 \text{ g/h}}
$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between **specific energy (Wh/kg)** and **energy density (Wh/L)**. Why does compressed hydrogen gas have high specific energy but low energy density? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fuel cell vehicle carries **5 kg of compressed H₂** at 700 bar. The LHV of H₂ is **120 MJ/kg**. The fuel cell system (including BoP) has an average efficiency of **55 %** during a drive cycle. If the vehicle consumes energy at an average rate of **20 kW**, calculate: (i) total chemical energy stored, (ii) usable electrical energy, (iii) driving range in hours, (iv) specific energy of the entire hydrogen tank in Wh/kg. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Specific Energy vs Energy Density**

| Aspect | Specific Energy | Energy Density |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Energy per unit **mass** | Energy per unit **volume** |
| Formula | E_s = E / m | E_v = E / V |
| SI Unit | J/kg (or Wh/kg) | J/m³ (or Wh/L) |
| Importance | Range per kg of fuel | Tank size needed |

**Why compressed H₂ has high specific energy but low energy density:**

- **Mass basis:** Even at 700 bar, the H₂ molecules themselves have a *very low molecular weight* (M = 2.016 g/mol). The energy content per kg is unchanged by compression — it is an intrinsic property of the H–H bond.
- **Volume basis:** At 1 atm, 1 kg of H₂ occupies ~11 m³ (balloon). Compressing to 700 bar reduces this to ~16 L, but this is still huge compared to gasoline (~1.3 L for equivalent energy).
- **The heavy tank adds mass** (carbon-fiber composite ≈ 100 kg for 5 kg H₂), reducing the *system* specific energy.
- **Conclusion:** H₂ is excellent gravimetrically (good for aerospace) but poor volumetrically (challenge for cars).

---

**(b) Numerical Computation**

**Given:** m_H₂ = 5 kg, LHV = 120 MJ/kg, η = 0.55, P = 20 kW

**(i) Total chemical energy stored:**
$$
E_{chem} = m \cdot LHV = 5 \times 120 = 600 \text{ MJ}
$$
$$
\boxed{E_{chem} = 600 \text{ MJ} = 600 \times 10^6 \text{ J}}
$$

**(ii) Usable electrical energy:**
$$
E_{elec} = \eta \cdot E_{chem} = 0.55 \times 600 = 330 \text{ MJ}
$$
Convert to kWh:
$$
E_{elec} = \frac{330 \times 10^6}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 91.67 \text{ kWh}
$$
$$
\boxed{E_{elec} = 330 \text{ MJ} = 91.67 \text{ kWh}}
$$

**(iii) Driving range in hours:**
$$
t = \frac{E_{elec}}{P} = \frac{91.67 \text{ kWh}}{20 \text{ kW}} = 4.58 \text{ h}
$$
$$
\boxed{t \approx 4.58 \text{ hours} \ (\approx 275 \text{ min})}
$$

**(iv) Specific energy of entire H₂ tank in Wh/kg:**

Assuming tank mass = 100 kg (carbon-fiber composite for 5 kg H₂), total mass = 105 kg:
$$
E_s = \frac{E_{chem}}{m_{total}} = \frac{600 \text{ MJ}}{105 \text{ kg}} = 5.71 \text{ MJ/kg}
$$
Convert to Wh/kg:
$$
E_s = \frac{5.71 \times 10^6}{3600} = 1{,}587 \text{ Wh/kg}
$$
$$
\boxed{E_s \approx 1{,}587 \text{ Wh/kg}}
$$

(If we consider only the hydrogen mass: 600 MJ / 5 kg = 120 MJ/kg = 33,333 Wh/kg — a textbook ideal.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting n = 2 electrons** for H₂ — most students write 1, which doubles the consumption error. This is the #1 mistake.
> 2. **Mixing LHV (1.229 V) and HHV (1.481 V)** in efficiency calculations. Use LHV for vehicle applications; HHV for stationary CHP systems.
> 3. **Unit mismatch between cm² and m²** — always convert j × A into SI units before computing power in W.
> 4. **Not multiplying by the number of cells in series** for stack voltage. For a 50-cell stack, even at 0.7 V, V_stack = 35 V, not 0.7 V.
> 5. **Dividing kg/s by kW** without unit conversion — kg/s / kW is NOT g/kWh. You must multiply by 3,600,000 to convert.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert grams to kilograms** in specific energy. 1 MJ/kg = 277.8 Wh/kg, not 1000.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Units in Fuel Cells**

- ⚡ **Voltage (V)** is in *volts*; OCV of H₂/O₂ cell = **1.229 V (LHV)** at 25 °C, 1 atm.
- ⚡ **Current (A)** is in *amperes*; **Current density j** = I/A in **A/cm²** or **A/m²** (1 A/cm² = 10⁴ A/m²).
- ⚡ **Power (W)** = V × I; **Power density** = V × j in **W/cm²** (1 W/cm² = 10 kW/m²).
- ⚡ **Energy (J or Wh)**; **1 Wh = 3,600 J**; **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ**.
- ⚡ **ASR (Ω·cm²)** is THE benchmark for membranes; lower is better.
- ⚡ **Efficiency η = V_cell / 1.229** (LHV basis, transport); use **1.481 V** for HHV.
- ⚡ **Faraday's constant F = 96,485 C/mol** — always carry this in your head.
- ⚡ **H₂ consumption** ṁ = (M·I)/(n·F) = 1.045 × 10⁻⁸ · I kg/s; specific ≈ **30.6 g/kWh ideal** at 1.229 V; **~55 g/kWh realistic** at 0.6–0.7 V.
- ⚡ **Universal gas constant R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** appears in Nernst equation.
- ⚡ **Specific energy** = Wh/kg; **Energy density** = Wh/L — H₂ is high in the first, low in the second.
- ⚡ **Temperature**: K = °C + 273.15; **Pressure**: 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar.
- ⚡ **Conversions** to memorize: W/cm² ↔ kW/m², A/cm² ↔ A/m², Ω·cm² ↔ Ω·m², Wh ↔ J.
- ⚡ **Polarization curve** is plotted as V (V) vs j (A/cm²); the power density curve is V × j.
- ⚡ For a *stack*: V_stack = n × V_cell; I_stack = I_cell (cells in series carry same current).
- ⚡ Always state *which basis* (LHV or HHV) you are using in efficiency — KTU examiners check this.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line Takeaway:** *In fuel cell technology, "units" are not just symbols — they are the language that translates chemistry into engineering, and the difference between a confused student and a topper is the habit of writing the unit beside every number.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Scenarios
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT721 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Application Scenarios

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Application Scenarios** in fuel cell technology refer to the categorized domains and operational environments in which fuel cell systems are deployed as primary, auxiliary, or backup energy conversion devices. According to the KTU 2024 PECHT721 syllabus, these scenarios are broadly classified into four pillars:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 1 - Application Scenarios):**
> "The study of operational deployment of fuel cells across **Stationary Power Generation**, **Transportation (Motive)**, **Portable Power**, and **Specialty/Space Applications**, including their system integration requirements, power ranges, and engineering constraints."

The classification is based on three engineering parameters:
- **Power Output Range:** $\mathbf{W}$ (watts) to $\mathbf{MW}$ (megawatts)
- **Operating Duration:** Intermittent, continuous, or cyclic duty
- **Form Factor & Weight Density:** Specific Power ($\mathbf{W/kg}$) and Power Density ($\mathbf{W/cm^2}$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Unlike batteries that store energy, fuel cells are *energy conversion devices* that continuously produce electricity as long as fuel (typically $\mathbf{H_2}$) and oxidant ($\mathbf{O_2}$) are supplied. This makes their application profile fundamentally different from electrochemical storage.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Water Purifier" Intuition)

Think of a fuel cell as a **continuous, refillable water purifier** for electricity, while a battery is a **stored water tank**.

| Device | Analogy | Energy Source | Refill Pattern |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Battery** | A water tank with stored water | Chemical energy stored internally | Recharge after depletion |
| **Fuel Cell** | A purifier that makes water on demand | External fuel (hydrogen) tank | Quick refuel in minutes |
| **Engine (ICE)** | A campfire burning wood | Fossil fuel combustion | Refuel in minutes (but inefficient) |

Just as a water purifier is chosen over a tank when you need *continuous, clean, scalable output*, fuel cells dominate scenarios requiring **long endurance, fast refueling, and high energy density per unit mass**.

## 1.3 The Four Pillars of Application

```
APPLICATION DOMAINS (Power Range Mapping)
├── 1. STATIONARY     : 1 kW  ──────────► 10 MW    (Homes → Power Plants)
├── 2. TRANSPORTATION : 1 kW  ──────────► 250 kW   (Scooters → Heavy Trucks)
├── 3. PORTABLE       : 1 W   ──────────► 500 W    (Phones → Military Radios)
└── 4. SPECIALTY      : 50 W  ──────────► 100 kW   (Spacecraft → Submarines)
```

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **Electrical Efficiency of Fuel Cell:** $\mathbf{40\% - 60\%}$ (vs. $30\%-35\%$ for ICE)
- **Combined Heat & Power (CHP) Efficiency:** Up to $\mathbf{85\%-90\%}$
- **Hydrogen Energy Density (LHV):** $\mathbf{120 \, MJ/kg}$ (vs. gasoline at $44 \, MJ/kg$)
- **Typical PEMFC Stack Power Density:** $\mathbf{0.5 - 1.0 \, W/cm^2}$
- **Operating Temperature Spread:** $\mathbf{-40^{\circ}C}$ (PEMFC) to $\mathbf{1000^{\circ}C}$ (SOFC)
- **Cold Start Time (PEMFC):** $\mathbf{< 30 \, s}$ (modern membrane technology)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Selection Logic — Why Different Scenarios Need Different Fuel Cell Types

Every application scenario imposes a unique set of constraints. The fuel cell type (PEMFC, SOFC, PAFC, AFC, MCFC, DMFC) is chosen by **matching its characteristic profile** to the **operational demand profile** of the application.

### 2.1.1 Application-Scenario Matching Logic

**Step 1 — Identify the Power Demand Profile:**
- *Steady base load* → SOFC / MCFC (high temperature, slow dynamics)
- *Variable traction load* → PEMFC (fast startup, high power density)
- *Intermittent micro-load* → DMFC / PEMFC (compact, ambient operation)

**Step 2 — Identify the Weight & Volume Constraint:**
- *Aviation/Space* → PEMFC with cryogenic $\mathbf{H_2}$ (mass-optimized)
- *Underwater/Submarine* → PEMFC with stored $\mathbf{O_2}$ (closed-loop)
- *Building-integrated* → SOFC (footprint-flexible, thermal integration)

**Step 3 — Identify the Ambient Operating Conditions:**
- *Cold climates* → Low-temp PEMFC with membrane humidification
- *High-temperature industrial sites* → SOFC (waste-heat utilization)
- *Pure-water-sensitive applications* → PEMFC vs. AFC trade-off

**Step 4 — Identify the Economic & Refueling Constraint:**
- *Hydrogen-available regions* → Pure $\mathbf{H_2}$ PEMFC
- *Hydrocarbon-available regions* → Reformed hydrocarbon → SOFC/PAFC

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the units of *Specific Energy* ($\mathbf{Wh/kg}$), *Energy Density* ($\mathbf{Wh/L}$), and *Efficiency* ($\mathbf{\%}$). These appear every year.

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Typical Units | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{el} = \frac{V_{cell}}{1.482} \times 100\%$ | Electrical efficiency from cell voltage | $\mathbf{\%}$ | All scenarios |
| 2 | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{el} \times \eta_{BoP}$ | System efficiency (incl. BOP) | $\mathbf{\%}$ | Vehicle/Stationary |
| 3 | $E_{stored} = m_{H_2} \times 120 \, MJ/kg$ | Chemical energy of stored $\mathbf{H_2}$ | $\mathbf{MJ}$ | Range calculation |
| 4 | $E_{usable} = E_{stored} \times \eta_{FC} \times \eta_{drv}$ | Usable electric energy | $\mathbf{MJ}$ | EV range |
| 5 | $Range = \frac{E_{usable}}{P_{aux} + F_{drag} \cdot v}$ | Vehicle range model | $\mathbf{km}$ | Transportation |
| 6 | $P_{stack} = n \cdot A \cdot i \cdot V_{cell}$ | Stack power output | $\mathbf{W}$ | Stack sizing |
| 7 | $\text{Specific Power} = \frac{P_{stack}}{m_{stack}}$ | Power-to-weight ratio | $\mathbf{W/kg}$ | Aviation, vehicles |
| 8 | $\text{Power Density} = \frac{P_{stack}}{V_{stack}}$ | Power-to-volume ratio | $\mathbf{W/L}$ | Portable, automotive |
| 9 | $\eta_{CHP} = \eta_{el} + \eta_{th}$ | Combined heat & power efficiency | $\mathbf{\%}$ | Stationary CHP |
| 10 | $Q_{th} = P_{el} \cdot \frac{(1-\eta_{el})}{\eta_{el}}$ | Recoverable thermal energy | $\mathbf{W}$ | Cogeneration |
| 11 | $\lambda_{air} = \frac{\text{Air supplied}}{\text{Stoichiometric air}}$ | Air stoichiometric ratio | dimensionless | BOP design |
| 12 | $\text{LHV}_{H_2} = 120 \, MJ/kg$ | Lower Heating Value of $\mathbf{H_2}$ | $\mathbf{MJ/kg}$ | All scenarios |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notations:** $n$ = number of cells, $A$ = active cell area, $i$ = current density, $V_{cell}$ = operating voltage. **Never confuse the vertical pipe in `|x|` syntax with table separators** — in tables, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Scenario | Engineering Utility | Why Fuel Cell Wins |
|---|---|---|
| **Heavy Trucks (long-haul)** | High torque, zero local emissions | Higher energy density than Li-ion for >500 km range |
| **Space Missions** | Closed-loop water byproduct | Drinking water for astronauts + electricity |
| **Data Centers** | Grid-independent backup | 48-hour runtime vs. 15-min UPS batteries |
| **Underwater Drones (UUVs)** | Silent operation, no exhaust bubbles | Stealth + extended mission duration |
| **Hospitals** | Silent, vibration-free power | Critical-load reliability |
| **Cold Storage / Refrigeration** | Waste heat for absorption cooling | $\eta_{CHP}$ up to 90% combined utility |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Do **not** confuse *Specific Energy* (per unit mass, $\mathbf{Wh/kg}$) with *Energy Density* (per unit volume, $\mathbf{Wh/L}$). PEMFC systems have *moderate* specific energy but *low* energy density due to bulky hydrogen tanks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Vehicle Range Using a Fuel Cell Power Train

> **Problem Context:** A fuel cell electric vehicle (FCEV) carries $\mathbf{5 \, kg}$ of compressed hydrogen at $\mathbf{700 \, bar}$. The drivetrain consumes an average of $\mathbf{P_{avg} = 12 \, kW}$ during steady highway driving. The fuel cell operates at $\mathbf{\eta_{FC} = 50\%}$ and the electric drive train at $\mathbf{\eta_{drv} = 90\%}$. Calculate the theoretical driving range, ignoring auxiliary loads.

### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the total chemical energy stored in the hydrogen tank.**

We use the Lower Heating Value (LHV) of hydrogen:
$$\text{LHV}_{H_2} = 120 \, MJ/kg$$

The total chemical energy available is:
$$E_{chem} = m_{H_2} \times \text{LHV}_{H_2}$$

Substituting $m_{H_2} = 5 \, kg$:
$$E_{chem} = 5 \, kg \times 120 \, MJ/kg$$

$$E_{chem} = 600 \, MJ$$

**Step 2 — Apply the fuel cell electrical efficiency to extract usable electric energy.**

The fuel cell converts chemical energy to electrical energy with efficiency:
$$\eta_{FC} = \frac{E_{el}}{E_{chem}} = 50\% = 0.50$$

Therefore:
$$E_{el} = \eta_{FC} \times E_{chem}$$

$$E_{el} = 0.50 \times 600 \, MJ$$

$$E_{el} = 300 \, MJ$$

**Step 3 — Apply the drivetrain efficiency to obtain energy at the wheels.**

$$\eta_{drv} = \frac{E_{wheels}}{E_{el}} = 90\% = 0.90$$

$$E_{wheels} = \eta_{drv} \times E_{el}$$

$$E_{wheels} = 0.90 \times 300 \, MJ$$

$$E_{wheels} = 270 \, MJ$$

**Step 4 — Convert energy to driving time using average power demand.**

The relationship between energy, power, and time is:
$$E = P \times t \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{E}{P}$$

Substituting:
$$t = \frac{270 \, MJ}{12 \, kW} = \frac{270 \times 10^6 \, J}{12 \times 10^3 \, W}$$

$$t = 22{,}500 \, s$$

**Step 5 — Convert time to distance using the vehicle's average speed.**

Assuming an average highway speed of $v = 90 \, km/h$:
$$\text{Range} = v \times t = 90 \, \frac{km}{h} \times \frac{22{,}500 \, s}{3600 \, s/h}$$

$$\text{Range} = 90 \times 6.25 = 562.5 \, km$$

### Final Consolidated Solution

$$\boxed{\text{Range} \approx 562.5 \, km}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Scenario-Cell Type-Fuel Mapping

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is **frequently asked in KTU Part A (3 marks)** as a direct table-filling or matching question.

| Application Scenario | Dominant FC Type | Operating $T$ (°C) | Fuel Form | Power Range | Key Advantage |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Spacecraft (NASA)** | AFC (legacy) / PEMFC | $\mathbf{60-80}$ | Cryogenic $\mathbf{H_2}$ + Liquid $\mathbf{O_2}$ | $1-100 \, kW$ | Pure water byproduct |
| **Passenger Cars (Toyota Mirai)** | PEMFC | $\mathbf{60-80}$ | $700 \, bar$ compressed $\mathbf{H_2}$ | $100-130 \, kW$ | 5-min refuel, 600 km range |
| **Heavy-Duty Trucks** | PEMFC | $\mathbf{60-80}$ | Liquid $\mathbf{H_2}$ | $200-300 \, kW$ | High payload, long range |
| **Buses (city transit)** | PEMFC | $\mathbf{60-80}$ | $350 \, bar$ compressed $\mathbf{H_2}$ | $150-200 \, kW$ | Zero tailpipe emissions |
| **Residential CHP** | PEMFC / SOFC | $\mathbf{60-1000}$ | Natural gas reformed | $1-10 \, kW$ | Heating + electricity |
| **Industrial CHP** | MCFC / PAFC | $\mathbf{600-800}$ | Natural gas / biogas | $\mathbf{100 \, kW - 10 \, MW}$ | High efficiency, fuel flexibility |
| **Data Center Backup** | PEMFC | $\mathbf{60-80}$ | $\mathbf{H_2}$ cylinder bank | $50 \, kW - 1 \, MW$ | 48-hour runtime, silent |
| **Forklifts (warehouses)** | PEMFC | $\mathbf{60-80}$ | Metal hydride / $350 \, bar$ | $5-20 \, kW$ | Indoor-zero emissions |
| **Portable Military** | PEMFC / DMFC | $\mathbf{25-80}$ | Methanol / chemical $\mathbf{H_2}$ | $50-500 \, W$ | Stealth, long endurance |
| **Submarines (U212 class)** | PEMFC | $\mathbf{60-80}$ | Stored $\mathbf{H_2}$ + Liquid $\mathbf{O_2}$ | $100-300 \, kW$ | Silent, weeks-long submersion |
| **Aircraft (DLR Antares/HY4)** | PEMFC | $\mathbf{60-80}$ | $350-700 \, bar$ compressed $\mathbf{H_2}$ | $20-100 \, kW$ | Zero-altitude emissions |
| **Smartphones/Laptops** | DMFC (R&D) | $\mathbf{25-60}$ | Liquid methanol | $1-25 \, W$ | Multi-day runtime |

## 3.3 Comparative Analysis: Fuel Cell vs. Battery vs. ICE in Transportation

| Parameter | PEMFC Vehicle | Li-ion EV | Gasoline ICE |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Refuel Time** | $\mathbf{3-5}$ min | $\mathbf{30-60}$ min (DC fast) | $\mathbf{2-3}$ min |
| **Range (typical)** | $\mathbf{500-700}$ km | $\mathbf{300-500}$ km | $\mathbf{600-1000}$ km |
| **Tank-to-Wheel Efficiency** | $\mathbf{45-55\%}$ | $\mathbf{70-85\%}$ | $\mathbf{20-30\%}$ |
| **Cold Start ($-20^{\circ}C$)** | Challenging (PEMFC water freeze) | Excellent | Good |
| **Operating Cost (\$/100 km)** | Medium-High | Low (electricity) | High (fuel) |
| **Infrastructure Maturity** | Low (2024 status) | High | Very High |
| **Best-Use Case** | Long-haul, heavy-duty | Urban, short-range | Long-range, rural |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** Fuel cells dominate **range-sensitive + payload-heavy + fast-refuel-required** scenarios. Batteries dominate **urban + short-range + high-efficiency** scenarios. The two are **complementary**, not competing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fuel Cell System in a Vehicle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H2Tank["Compressed H2 Tank 700 bar"]
    AirIn["Ambient Air Intake"]
    FCStack["PEMFC Stack 80 to 130 kW"]
    DCBoost["DC DC Boost Converter"]
    BatPack["Li ion Buffer Battery"]
    Inverter["Three Phase Inverter"]
    Motor["PMSM Traction Motor"]
    Wheels["Vehicle Wheels"]
    Rad["Cooling Radiator"]
    Ctrl["Vehicle Control Unit VCU"]
    StackMan["Thermal Management System"]
    H2Tank --> FCStack
    AirIn --> FCStack
    FCStack --> DCBoost
    BatPack --> DCBoost
    DCBoost --> Inverter
    Inverter --> Motor
    Motor --> Wheels
    Ctrl --> DCBoost
    Ctrl --> Inverter
    FCStack --> StackMan
    StackMan --> Rad
    FCStack -. heat recovery .-> Rad
```

> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The fuel cell stack provides primary power; the Li-ion buffer handles transient load peaks (regenerative braking, acceleration). The VCU orchestrates power-split between the two sources.
