When the cell discharges, current flows and electrical work is produced. Simultaneously, the entropy of the universe must increase. Since the electrochemical reaction produces fewer moles of gas (and more ordered liquid water) in many fuel cell reactions, ΔS is **negative**. This means the system *releases* entropy to the surroundings as heat Q = T|ΔS|.

In a practical PEM fuel cell, this TΔS heat is removed by:
- Cooling plates
- Air or liquid coolant circulation
- Radiator systems in automotive applications

The total heat rejection rate at the cell level is:

$$Q_{rejected} = T\Delta S \times \dot{n}_{reaction}$$

For the H₂/O₂ cell, this is 48.6 kJ per mole of H₂ — a substantial thermal load that the cooling system must handle. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution for DMFC:**

**(i) Reversible cell voltage:**

$$E° = -\frac{\Delta G°}{nF} = -\frac{(-702 \times 10^3)}{(6)(96485)}$$

$$E° = \frac{702000}{578910} = 1.213 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Maximum electrical work:**

$$W_{elec,\,max} = nFE° = (6)(96485)(1.213)$$

Alternatively, directly:

$$W_{elec,\,max} = -\Delta G° = 702 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**(iii) Heat rejected to surroundings:**

Using the identity ΔH = ΔG + TΔS, therefore TΔS = ΔH − ΔG:

$$Q_{rejected} = T\Delta S = \Delta H - \Delta G$$

$$Q_{rejected} = -726 - (-702) = -24 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The negative sign indicates **heat is released by the system** (exothermic). Magnitude:

$$\boxed{Q_{rejected} = 24 \text{ kJ per mole of methanol}}$$

**Thermodynamic efficiency check:**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{702}{726} = 0.9669 = 96.7\%$$

This shows the DMFC has an exceptionally high theoretical efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in ΔG:** Students often write ΔG = +237.2 kJ/mol for the H₂/O₂ reaction. **Always** remember the products are more stable than reactants → ΔG is **negative** for spontaneous reactions.
>
> 2. **HHV vs LHV Confusion:** PEMFC question → use ΔH = 285.8 kJ/mol. SOFC question → use ΔH = 241.8 kJ/mol. Examiners deduct **2–3 marks** if this is wrong.
>
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** When substituting into E = −ΔG/(nF), ΔG **must** be in **Joules**, not kJ. Forgetting the ×1000 conversion is the **#1 source of error** in this module.
>
> 4. **Number of Electrons (n):** H₂/O₂ → n = 2. Methanol oxidation → n = 6. Using the wrong n gives wrong voltage by a factor of 3.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to Convert:** F = 96,485 C/mol, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K). Do not mix F = 26.8 Ah/mol with voltages in volts without converting.
>
> 6. **Confusing W and −ΔG:** The maximum work the system can **deliver** is −ΔG (positive number for spontaneous reactions). Saying W = ΔG loses the sign and is marked wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Gibbs Free Energy & Work Potential of Fuel**

- **Definition:** G = H − TS; ΔG = ΔH − TΔS. Negative ΔG → spontaneous reaction → electricity production.

- **Core Equation (Memorize!):** $W_{elec,\,max} = -\Delta G = nFE_{cell}$

- **Reversible Cell Voltage:** $E° = -\dfrac{\Delta G°}{nF}$. For H₂/O₂ (liquid water): E° = **1.229 V**.

- **Faraday's Constant:** F = **96,485 C/mol** = 26.8 Ah/mol. Used in all fuel cell electrical calculations.

- **H₂/O₂ Reference Values (Memorize All):**
  - Liquid water: ΔG° = −237.2 kJ/mol, ΔH° = −285.8 kJ/mol, E° = 1.229 V, η = 83.0%
  - Steam: ΔG° = −228.6 kJ/mol, ΔH° = −241.8 kJ/mol, E° = 1.185 V, η = 94.5%

- **Heating Values:**
  - **HHV** = Higher Heating Value → liquid water product → low-temp fuel cells (PEMFC, AFC, PAFC)
  - **LHV** = Lower Heating Value → steam product → high-temp fuel cells (SOFC, MCFC)

- **Thermodynamic Efficiency:** $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$. This is the **theoretical maximum** efficiency of a fuel cell — actual efficiency is lower due to activation, ohmic, and concentration losses.

- **Temperature Effect:** E **decreases** with T for H₂/O₂ (ΔS < 0). Coefficient ≈ **−0.85 mV/K** per cell.

- **Nernst Equation (Non-Standard Conditions):** $E = E° - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$. Used when pressure, concentration, or temperature deviate from standard.

- **Equilibrium Relation:** $\Delta G° = -RT \ln K$. A large negative ΔG° → large K → reaction goes to completion.

- **Heat Rejection by Cell:** $Q_{cell} = T\Delta S = \Delta H - \Delta G$. This is the thermal load the cooling system must handle.

- **Carnot vs Fuel Cell:** Fuel cells **bypass** the Carnot limit because they convert chemical → electrical energy **directly**, without an intermediate heat stage. This is why fuel cells are theoretically more efficient than any heat engine.

- **Exam Strategy:**
  1. Write the balanced reaction and identify **n** (electrons transferred per mole).
  2. Look up or calculate **ΔG°** and **ΔH°**.
  3. Apply E° = −ΔG°/(nF) — remember to convert kJ → J.
  4. Compute η = ΔG/ΔH if required.
  5. State assumptions clearly (reversible, constant T and P).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reversible Voltage - Nernst Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT721 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Reversible Voltage - Nernst Equation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reversible Cell Voltage (E):** The maximum theoretical voltage that a fuel cell can deliver when operating under **thermodynamically reversible (quasi-equilibrium) conditions**, with no current flowing (or infinitesimally small current). It represents the open-circuit thermodynamic limit of the electrochemical energy conversion. The **Nernst Equation** is the fundamental thermodynamic relationship that expresses this reversible cell voltage as a function of: - The standard-state reversible voltage (E°) - The operating temperature (T) - The partial pressures (or activities) of the reactants and products > [!NOTE] > For the canonical Hydrogen-Oxygen Fuel Cell reaction: > H₂(g) + ½O₂(g) → H₂O(l), the standard reversible voltage **E° = 1.229 V at 298.15 K and 1 atm**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Imagine a **water reservoir on a hill**: - The **height of the hill** represents the **maximum reversible voltage (E)** — the theoretical energy available. - The **water level (amount of water)** represents the **partial pressures** of reactants — more fuel/oxidizer means a "higher hill" and more energy. - The **temperature** represents **how eager the water is to roll down** — higher T changes the equilibrium energy landscape. - **Actual operating voltage** is the height the water *actually* reaches after friction (irreversible losses), which is always less than the reversible hilltop. The Nernst Equation is essentially the "**altimeter for the energy hill**" — it tells you exactly how high the reversible voltage hill is, given the chemical weather (T and P) of the system. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants (Memorize) | Symbol | Quantity | Value | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **R** | Universal Gas Constant | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | | **F** | Faraday's Constant | **96,485 C/mol** | | **T_std** | Standard Temperature | **298.15 K (25°C)** | | **n** | Electrons transferred per mole fuel | **2** (for H₂) | | **E° (H₂/O₂, liq. H₂O)** | Standard Reversible Voltage | **1.229 V** | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step The reversible voltage of a fuel cell is governed by the **change in Gibbs Free Energy (ΔG)** of the overall electrochemical reaction: 1. **Step 1 — Thermodynamic Foundation:** The maximum non-expansion (electrical) work obtainable from a reaction at constant T and P is equal to the negative change in Gibbs free energy. $$W_{max} = -\Delta G$$ 2. **Step 2 — Electrical Work Link:** Electrical work per mole of reaction = (charge transferred) × (voltage) = nFE. $$-\Delta G = nFE$$ 3. **Step 3 — Non-Standard Conditions:** Under non-standard pressures, the actual Gibbs free energy change is given by the **van't Hoff relation**: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$ where **Q** is the reaction quotient (ratio of product activities to reactant activities raised to stoichiometric powers). 4. **Step 4 — Substitution into Voltage:** Replacing ΔG with −nFE and ΔG° with −nFE°: $$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$ 5. **Step 5 — Rearrange for E (Nernst Equation):** $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ 6. **Step 6 — Sign Convention (Reactants over Products):** For the **H₂/O₂ fuel cell** (n = 2), Q = P_H₂O / (P_H₂ · P_O₂^(1/2)): $$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$ ### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Master these six equations — they constitute 80% of examination marks on this topic. | # | Equation | Domain / Use | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $E^{\circ} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF}$ | Standard reversible voltage from Gibbs energy | V | | 2 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | **General Nernst Equation** | V | | 3 | $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$ | H₂/O₂ fuel cell (n = 2) | V | | 4 | $E^{\circ}(T) = 1.229 - 0.00085(T - 298.15)$ | Liquid water product (PEMFC, AFC) | V | | 5 | $E^{\circ}(T) = 1.288 - 0.000845(T - 298.15)$ | Vapor water product (SOFC, PAFC) | V | | 6 | $\dfrac{RT}{F} = \dfrac{T}{11,605}$ at T in K | Numerical shortcut | V | | 7 | $\dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln 10 = 0.02958\,T \cdot \log_{10}(\cdot)$ | Base-10 logarithm form | V/decade | > **Note on Boundary Conditions:** All pressures must be expressed in **atm (absolute)**, not gauge. If using bar: divide bar value by 1.01325 to convert to atm. ### 2.3 Real-World Utility - **Fuel Cell Stack Sizing:** Engineers use E(Nernst) as the **theoretical maximum** to predict the open-circuit voltage of a stack and quantify activation/ohmic/concentration losses. - **Operating Point Optimization:** Knowing how E depends on T and P allows operators to tune compressor power, humidification, and stack temperature for maximum efficiency. - **Diagnostic Benchmark:** Measured OCV is compared with Nernst voltage — the difference (called the **Nernst loss** or **open-circuit voltage loss**) reveals internal crossover currents and parasitic reactions. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the General Nernst Equation **Starting Point:** Consider a generic electrochemical half-cell reaction: $$aA + ne^{-} \rightleftharpoons cC$$ The Gibbs free energy change at any state is: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$ where: $$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ} \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta G = -nFE$$ Substituting both expressions: $$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$ Divide throughout by **−nF** (note the sign flip on the log term): $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ This is the **General Nernst Equation** — boxed as a board examination staple. --- ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation for the Hydrogen Fuel Cell **Step 1 — Reaction:** $$\text{H}_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$ **Step 2 — Identify n:** Two electrons are transferred per molecule of H₂ oxidized, hence **n = 2**. **Step 3 — Construct the Reaction Quotient Q:** For pure solids and liquids, activity = 1. For gases, activity = partial pressure (in atm). $$Q = \frac{a_{H_2O}}{a_{H_2} \cdot a_{O_2}^{1/2}} = \frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into General Nernst:** $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Invert the log argument (reactants over products convention):** $$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$ This is the **fuel cell Nernst equation** at the standard 25°C: $E^{\circ} = 1.229$ V. --- ### 3.3 Temperature Dependence — Full Analytical Form The standard-state Gibbs energy itself depends on temperature. From fundamental thermodynamics: $$\Delta G^{\circ}(T) = \Delta H^{\circ}(T) - T\,\Delta S^{\circ}(T)$$ For the H₂/O₂ reaction: - $\Delta H^{\circ}_{298} = -285.83$ kJ/mol (formation of liquid H₂O) - $\Delta S^{\circ}_{298} = -163.34$ J/(mol·K) Assuming ΔH° and ΔS° are approximately constant near room temperature: $$\Delta G^{\circ}(T) \approx -285{,}830 - T(-163.34) = -285{,}830 + 163.34\,T \;\;\text{(J/mol)}$$ Dividing by **−2F** to obtain the standard reversible voltage: $$E^{\circ}(T) = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}(T)}{2F} = \frac{285{,}830 - 163.34\,T}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$ Simplifying: $$E^{\circ}(T) = 1.4812 - 8.46 \times 10^{-4}\,T \quad \text{[V, T in K]}$$ **Verification at 298 K:** $$E^{\circ}(298) = 1.4812 - (8.46 \times 10^{-4})(298) = 1.4812 - 0.2521 = 1.2291 \text{ V} \;\; \checkmark$$ This matches the widely cited empirical correlation: $$E^{\circ}(T) \approx 1.229 - 0.00085\,(T - 298.15) \;\;\text{V}$$ The coefficient 0.00085 V/K comes from the full thermochemical integration accounting for the **temperature variation of ΔCp** (heat capacity change) of the reaction species. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example > **Problem:** A PEM fuel cell operates at **75°C (348.15 K)** with P_H₂ = 1.0 atm, P_O₂ = 0.21 atm, P_H₂O = 0.0316 atm (saturated water vapor). Calculate the Nernst reversible voltage. **Solution:** **Step A — Compute E°(T)** using the empirical liquid-water correlation: $$E^{\circ}(348.15) = 1.229 - 0.00085 \times (348.15 - 298.15) = 1.229 - 0.00085 \times 50$$ $$E^{\circ}(348.15) = 1.229 - 0.0425 = 1.1865 \text{ V}$$ **Step B — Compute the Nernst term:** $$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{(8.314)(348.15)}{2 \times 96{,}485} = \frac{2894.5}{192{,}970} = 0.01500 \text{ V}$$ **Step C — Compute the log argument:** $$\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} = \frac{(1.0)(0.21)^{1/2}}{0.0316} = \frac{1.0 \times 0.4583}{0.0316} = 14.50$$ **Step D — Take natural log:** $$\ln(14.50) = 2.674$$ **Step E — Combine:** $$E = 1.1865 + (0.01500)(2.674) = 1.1865 + 0.04011 = 1.2266 \text{ V}$$ **Result:** E ≈ **1.227 V** — note this is close to standard conditions because the reactant partial pressures approximate typical air-fuel operation. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture This diagram maps the **thermodynamic flow** from chemical species to the final reversible voltage output, illustrating the Nernst equation's role as the integration point. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Reactant Inputs
P-H2 and P-O2] --> B[Reaction Quotient Q
P-H2O over P-H2 x P-O2 to the 1 by 2] C[Temperature T in K] --> D[Thermal Voltage RT over nF] B --> E[Nernst Equation Core
E equals E0 plus RT over 2F ln Q] D --> E F[Standard Voltage E0 at T
1.229 minus 0.00085 times T minus 298] --> E E --> G[Reversible Voltage E in Volts
Theoretical Maximum] G --> H[Loss Subtractions
Activation, Ohmic, Concentration] H --> I[Actual Operating Voltage Vcell] style A fill:#e1f5ff style C fill:#fff4e1 style F fill:#f0e1ff style E fill:#ffe1e1 style G fill:#d4f4dd style I fill:#ffcccc ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Effect of Operating Parameters on E ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Increase in T] --> R1[E0 decreases by 0.00085 V per K] R1 --> O1[Reversible Voltage Decreases] P2[Increase in P-H2] --> R2[Nernst log term becomes more positive] R2 --> O2[Reversible Voltage Increases] P3[Increase in P-O2] --> R3[Nernst log term becomes more positive scaled by half] R3 --> O3[Reversible Voltage Increases logarithmically] P4[Increase in P-H2O] --> R4[Nernst log term becomes less positive] R4 --> O4[Reversible Voltage Decreases
Product Dilution Effect] style O1 fill:#ffe1e1 style O2 fill:#d4f4dd style O3 fill:#d4f4dd style O4 fill:#ffe1e1 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Process Map — From Standard State to Operating Conditions ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph THERMO[Thermodynamic Foundation] S1[Gibbs Free Energy
Delta G equals Delta H minus T Delta S] --> S2[Standard Voltage
E0 equals minus Delta G0 over nF] end subgraph NERNST[Nernst Correction] S2 --> N1[Add RT over nF ln Q] N1 --> N2[Reversible Voltage E at any T and P] end subgraph REALITY[Real Fuel Cell] N2 --> L1[Subtract Activation Loss] L1 --> L2[Subtract Ohmic Loss] L2 --> L3[Subtract Concentration Loss] L3 --> OUT[Operating Cell Voltage Vcell] end style THERMO fill:#e8f4ff style NERNST fill:#fff4e8 style REALITY fill:#ffe8e8 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define reversible cell voltage. What is its value for a standard H₂/O₂ fuel cell at 25°C?** **Model Answer:** The reversible cell voltage (E) is the maximum theoretical voltage a fuel cell can deliver under thermodynamically reversible (open-circuit, equilibrium) conditions. It is determined solely by the Gibbs free energy change of the cell reaction. $$E^{\circ} = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF} = -\frac{-237{,}200 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}}$$ For the standard H₂/O₂ fuel cell producing liquid water at 25°C and 1 atm: **E° = 1.229 V**. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **State the Nernst equation for a fuel cell and explain the significance of each term.** **Model Answer:** For a general electrochemical cell: $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ Significance of terms: - **E**: Reversible cell voltage under operating conditions (V). - **E°**: Standard reversible voltage at 25°C, 1 atm (V). - **R**: Universal gas constant, 8.314 J/(mol·K). - **T**: Absolute operating temperature (K). - **n**: Number of electrons transferred per mole of fuel. - **F**: Faraday's constant, 96,485 C/mol. - **Q**: Reaction quotient (ratio of product activities to reactant activities). The equation captures how temperature and species activities shift the equilibrium potential away from its standard value. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** **Derive the Nernst equation from fundamental thermodynamic principles, starting from the Gibbs free energy change.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Define the maximum electrical work from Gibbs free energy:** At constant temperature and pressure, the maximum non-expansion work obtainable is: $$W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G$$ **Step 2 — Express electrical work in terms of charge and voltage:** For n moles of electrons per mole of reaction: $$W_{elec} = nFE$$ Therefore: $$-\Delta G = nFE \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta G = -nFE$$ **Step 3 — Apply the van't Hoff isotherm for non-standard states:** $$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the standard and non-standard ΔG expressions:** $$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$ **Step 5 — Divide throughout by −nF (flipping sign on log term):** $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ This is the **General Nernst Equation** — derived from first principles. --- **(b)** **For a hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell operating at 80°C with P_H₂ = 1.2 atm, P_O₂ = 0.5 atm, and P_H₂O = 0.1 atm, calculate the reversible cell voltage using the Nernst equation.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Temperature in Kelvin:** $$T = 80 + 273.15 = 353.15 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2 — Standard reversible voltage at 80°C (liquid water):** $$E^{\circ}(353.15) = 1.229 - 0.00085 \times (353.15 - 298.15) = 1.229 - 0.0468 = 1.1822 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3 — Thermal voltage term:** $$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{(8.314)(353.15)}{2 \times 96{,}485} = \frac{2936.6}{192{,}970} = 0.01522 \text{ V}$$ **Step 4 — Reaction quotient argument:** $$\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} = \frac{1.2 \times (0.5)^{1/2}}{0.1} = \frac{1.2 \times 0.7071}{0.1} = \frac{0.8485}{0.1} = 8.485$$ **Step 5 — Natural logarithm:** $$\ln(8.485) = 2.139$$ **Step 6 — Apply Nernst equation:** $$E = 1.1822 + (0.01522)(2.139) = 1.1822 + 0.03256 = 1.2148 \text{ V}$$ **Final Answer: E ≈ 1.215 V** --- #### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** **Derive the Nernst equation specifically for the hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell reaction. Discuss how the reversible voltage depends on temperature and partial pressures.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Cell reaction and electron count:** $$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}, \quad n = 2$$ **Step 2 — Write the reaction quotient:** $$Q = \frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into General Nernst:** $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}\right) = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Temperature dependence:** $E^{\circ}$ itself decreases linearly with T at approximately **−0.00085 V/K** (liquid water case) due to the negative entropy change of the reaction. Higher T → lower reversible voltage. **Step 5 — Pressure dependence:** - Higher **P_H₂** → larger log term → higher E. - Higher **P_O₂** → higher E, but only as the **square root** (weaker dependence). - Higher **P_H₂O** → smaller log term → lower E (product dilution). The combined log term is positive when reactant partial pressures exceed that of product water — typically the case under normal operation. --- **(b)** **A solid oxide fuel cell (SOFC) operates at 1000 K. The standard Gibbs free energy of formation of water vapor at 1000 K is −192.6 kJ/mol. Calculate the standard reversible voltage at this temperature and the Nernst voltage when P_H₂ = 0.9 atm, P_O₂ = 0.21 atm, P_H₂O = 0.3 atm.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Standard reversible voltage at 1000 K:** $$E^{\circ}(1000) = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF} = -\frac{-192{,}600}{2 \times 96{,}485} = \frac{192{,}600}{192{,}970} = 0.9981 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2 — Thermal voltage at 1000 K:** $$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{(8.314)(1000)}{2 \times 96{,}485} = \frac{8314}{192{,}970} = 0.04308 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3 — Log argument:** $$\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} = \frac{0.9 \times (0.21)^{1/2}}{0.3} = \frac{0.9 \times 0.4583}{0.3} = \frac{0.4125}{0.3} = 1.375$$ **Step 4 — Natural logarithm:** $$\ln(1.375) = 0.3185$$ **Step 5 — Nernst voltage:** $$E = 0.9981 + (0.04308)(0.3185) = 0.9981 + 0.01372 = 1.0118 \text{ V}$$ **Final Answer: E ≈ 1.012 V** **Commentary:** SOFCs operate at lower reversible voltages than PEMFCs due to high-temperature entropy losses, but the slower kinetics are mitigated by fast electrode reactions. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign Error on Log Argument:** Reversing the Q convention. **Always remember**: Nernst equation for fuel cells uses **REACTANTS in the NUMERATOR** of ln — so the equation has a **PLUS** sign, not minus. > 2. **Forgetting the ½ exponent on P_O₂:** This is the single most frequent error in derivation marks. > 3. **Temperature in Celsius, not Kelvin:** Always convert °C to K before substituting into RT/F. > 4. **Using E° = 1.229 V at all temperatures:** This is a 25°C value. For high-temperature SOFCs at 800–1000°C, you must use the temperature-corrected E° via the Gibbs energy or empirical correlation. > 5. **Pressure Units:** Using bar or psia directly causes a unit mismatch. KTU expects **atm**. > 6. **Confusing n = 2 with n = 4:** Some students double-count. For H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻, **n = 2 per mole of H₂**, not per mole of H₂O. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Nernst Equation is the bridge** between thermochemistry (ΔG°) and electrochemistry (E). - **Standard Reversible Voltage (E°)** for H₂/O₂ → H₂O(l) = **1.229 V at 25°C**. - For H₂/O₂ → H₂O(g) = **1.188 V** (entropy difference between liquid and vapor). - The Nernst form for fuel cell: $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$. - **E° decreases with temperature** at ~−0.85 mV/K (liquid) or ~−0.85 mV/K (vapor, slightly different coefficient). - **E increases** with partial pressure of H₂ (linearly in log) and O₂ (as √). - **E decreases** with partial pressure of product water. - The reversible voltage is the **upper bound** — real cells always operate below this due to irreversibilities. - Memorize: **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), F = 96,485 C/mol, RT/F at 298 K ≈ 0.02569 V**. - At 25°C, **RT/2F ≈ 0.01285 V** — useful for quick mental checks. - Temperature-corrected E°(T) empirical formulas are exam gold: - Liquid water: $E^{\circ}(T) = 1.229 - 0.00085(T - 298.15)$ - Vapor water: $E^{\circ}(T) = 1.288 - 0.000845(T - 298.15)$

topics.title: Voltage Dependence on Pressure, Temperature, and Concentration
Efficiency: Thermodynamic, Voltage, and Fuel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT721 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Efficiency: Thermodynamic, Voltage, and Fuel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Fuel Cell Technology**, the term **"Efficiency"** refers to the ability of an electrochemical energy conversion device to extract useful electrical work from the chemical energy stored in a fuel. Unlike heat engines, which are fundamentally constrained by the **Carnot limit**, a fuel cell's efficiency is governed by the thermodynamics of its **electrochemical reactions** and the **irreversibilities (losses)** that occur during real operation.

KTU 2024 Scheme classifies fuel cell efficiency into three principal dimensions:

1. **Thermodynamic (or Ideal) Efficiency** — derived from the ratio of Gibbs free energy change to the enthalpy change of the overall reaction.
2. **Voltage (or Electrochemical) Efficiency** — derived from the ratio of the actual operating cell voltage to the theoretical reversible cell voltage.
3. **Fuel (or Faradaic) Efficiency** — derived from the ratio of the fuel actually consumed in the electrochemical reaction to the total fuel supplied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Focus:** The product of these three individual efficiencies yields the **Overall Stack Efficiency**, which is the parameter actually used to compare a fuel cell system against conventional power generation technologies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are buying mangoes at ₹100 per kg (the **total chemical energy** or enthalpy, ΔH). However, only a portion of the mango is edible flesh that gives nourishment (**useful work**, ΔG), while the rest is peel and seed that cannot be eaten.

- **Thermodynamic Efficiency** = (edible portion) / (whole mango) = ΔG / ΔH → this is fixed by the chemistry of the mango and cannot be changed.
- **Voltage Efficiency** = Imagine you have to peel the mango with a blunt knife. You lose some flesh in the process. The "sharpness" of the electrochemical process (activation, ohmic, concentration losses) determines how much useful voltage you actually obtain.
- **Fuel Efficiency** = Imagine some mangoes fall off the table and roll away unpeeled. The fuel utilization factor tells you how much of the supplied fuel actually reacted.

Together, these give the **Overall Efficiency** — what fraction of the original mango's value you truly enjoyed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Constant:** The **Faraday Constant** $F = 96{,}485 \ \text{C/mol}$ is the cornerstone for converting chemical energy (kJ/mol) into electrical potential (Volts) in every fuel cell efficiency equation.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Faraday Constant ($F$)**: $\mathbf{96{,}485 \ C \cdot mol^{-1}}$
- **Universal Gas Constant ($R$)**: $\mathbf{8.314 \ J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}}$
- **Standard Temperature ($T$)**: $\mathbf{298.15 \ K}$
- **Number of electrons transferred ($n$)**: $n = 2$ for the standard $\text{H}_2/\text{O}_2$ reaction.
- **Standard Reference Energies for the $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O(l)}$ reaction**:
  - $\Delta G^\circ = \mathbf{-237.2 \ kJ \cdot mol^{-1}}$
  - $\Delta H^\circ \ (\text{HHV}) = \mathbf{-285.8 \ kJ \cdot mol^{-1}}$
  - $\Delta H^\circ \ (\text{LHV}) = \mathbf{-241.8 \ kJ \cdot mol^{-1}}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Efficiency ($\eta_{th}$)

The thermodynamic efficiency expresses the **maximum possible fraction** of the fuel's heating value that can be converted into useful electrical work at constant temperature and pressure.

### Logic Steps

1. **Start from the First Law:** The total chemical energy available from burning fuel is its **enthalpy change** $\Delta H$.
2. **Apply the Second Law:** The maximum *useful* work extractable at constant $T$ and $P$ is the **Gibbs free energy change** $\Delta G$ (NOT the full enthalpy, because a portion $\Delta H - \Delta G = T\Delta S$ becomes waste heat, even in a perfect reversible cell).
3. **Form the Ratio:** $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\text{Maximum useful work}}{\text{Total heat released}} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$.

### Heating Value Basis (HHV vs LHV)

- **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** — assumes product water is condensed to liquid. Used in **American/European regulations** and most stationary fuel cell specifications.
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** — assumes product water remains as vapor. Used in **automotive applications** because exhaust water does not condense.

The choice of heating value changes the numerical efficiency dramatically:

$$
\eta_{th}^{\text{HHV}} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H_{\text{HHV}}}, \qquad \eta_{th}^{\text{LHV}} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H_{\text{LHV}}}
$$

For the $\text{H}_2/\text{O}_2$ reaction:

$$
\eta_{th}^{\text{HHV}} \approx 83\%, \qquad \eta_{th}^{\text{LHV}} \approx 98\%
$$

## 2.2 Voltage Efficiency ($\eta_v$)

Even an ideal reversible cell cannot escape real-world **polarization losses**. The actual terminal voltage $V_{\text{cell}}$ is always less than the theoretical reversible voltage $E_{\text{rev}}$.

### Logic Steps

1. The theoretical reversible EMF is given by the **Nernst Equation**: $E_{\text{rev}} = -\dfrac{\Delta G}{nF}$.
2. Real cell voltage drops due to three combined losses: **Activation Loss** ($\eta_{\text{act}}$), **Ohmic Loss** ($\eta_{\text{ohm}}$), and **Concentration Loss** ($\eta_{\text{conc}}$).
3. Therefore: $V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{rev}} - (\eta_{\text{act}} + \eta_{\text{ohm}} + \eta_{\text{conc}})$.
4. Voltage efficiency is the ratio of useful voltage to the thermodynamic ceiling:

$$
\eta_v = \frac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E_{\text{rev}}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> A PEMFC operating at ~0.7 V with $E_{\text{rev}} = 1.229$ V has a voltage efficiency of only $\approx 57\%$. This is the single largest source of efficiency loss in a real fuel cell.

## 2.3 Fuel (Faradaic) Efficiency ($\eta_f$)

In practice, some fuel **escapes** the cell without reacting. This includes:
- Fuel that **crosses over** the electrolyte membrane (e.g., $\text{H}_2$ permeating through Nafion).
- Fuel that **bypasses** the active area through manifold/flow maldistribution.
- Fuel that undergoes **parasitic side reactions** (e.g., internal reforming producing CO).

### Definition

$$
\eta_f = \frac{\text{Fuel actually consumed in faradaic reaction}}{\text{Total fuel supplied to the cell}}
$$

Equivalently, in terms of current:
$$
\eta_f = \frac{I_{\text{used}}}{I_{\text{total}}}
$$

## 2.4 Overall Efficiency ($\eta_{\text{overall}}$)

The total system efficiency is the product of the three:

$$
\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{th} \times \eta_v \times \eta_f
$$

If **balance-of-plant (BOP)** losses (pumps, blowers, inverter losses) are included, a **System Electrical Efficiency** is obtained:

$$
\eta_{\text{system}} = \eta_{\text{overall}} \times \eta_{\text{BOP}}
$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Units | Typical Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\eta_{th}$ | Thermodynamic Efficiency | $\Delta G / \Delta H$ | dimensionless | 0.83 (HHV), 0.98 (LHV) |
| $\eta_v$ | Voltage Efficiency | $V_{\text{cell}} / E_{\text{rev}}$ | dimensionless | 0.50 – 0.65 |
| $\eta_f$ | Fuel Efficiency | $I_{\text{used}} / I_{\text{supplied}}$ | dimensionless | 0.95 – 0.99 |
| $\eta_{\text{overall}}$ | Overall Stack Efficiency | $\eta_{th} \cdot \eta_v \cdot \eta_f$ | dimensionless | 0.40 – 0.55 |
| $\eta_{\text{system}}$ | System Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{overall}} \cdot \eta_{\text{BOP}}$ | dimensionless | 0.35 – 0.50 |
| $E_{\text{rev}}$ | Reversible Cell Voltage | $-\Delta G / (nF)$ | V (Volts) | 1.229 V (H$_2$/O$_2$) |
| $E_{th}$ | Thermoneutral Voltage | $-\Delta H / (nF)$ | V (Volts) | 1.481 V (HHV), 1.253 V (LHV) |
| $V_{\text{cell}}$ | Actual Operating Voltage | $E_{\text{rev}} - \sum \eta_{\text{loss}}$ | V (Volts) | 0.6 – 0.8 V typical |
| $n$ | Electrons transferred per mole | depends on reaction stoichiometry | dimensionless | 2 (H$_2$/O$_2$), 4 (CH$_4$) |
| $E_{\text{Nernst}}$ | Nernst Reversible Potential | $E^\circ - (RT/nF) \ln Q$ | V (Volts) | depends on $T, P$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Stationary CHP systems:** Use HHV-based efficiency to qualify for government subsidies (e.g., Japan's ENE-FARM target > 95% LHV).
- **Automotive FCEVs:** Use LHV-based efficiency for fair comparison with gasoline engines (~25% Carnot-limited).
- **Spacecraft (NASA):** Use $\eta_{\text{overall}}$ since BOP (radiators, pumps) is mission-critical and must be counted.
- **Grid-scale SOFC stacks:** Aim for $\eta_v > 70\%$ by operating at high temperatures (800 °C) to reduce activation losses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Reversible Cell Voltage ($E_{\text{rev}}$)

The reversible cell EMF for an electrochemical reaction $aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$ is derived from basic thermodynamic definitions.

### Step 1 — Start from the maximum non-expansion work

For a galvanic cell operating at constant $T$ and $P$, the maximum electrical work obtainable equals the Gibbs free energy decrease:

$$
W_{\text{elec, max}} = -\Delta G
$$

### Step 2 — Express electrical work in terms of charge and voltage

For $n$ moles of electrons transferred per mole of reaction, the total charge passed is $nF$. The electrical work is therefore:

$$
W_{\text{elec}} = Q \cdot E_{\text{rev}} = nF \cdot E_{\text{rev}}
$$

### Step 3 — Equate the two expressions

$$
nF \cdot E_{\text{rev}} = -\Delta G
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $E_{\text{rev}}$

$$
E_{\text{rev}} = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF}
$$

This is the **Nernst Reversible Potential** at standard state, which forms the ceiling for all real fuel cell voltages.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (General $T$, $P$, Concentration)

For non-standard conditions, $\Delta G$ becomes:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q
$$

where $Q$ is the **reaction quotient**.

### Step 1 — Substitute into the EMF expression

$$
E = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q
$$

### Step 2 — Recognize the standard EMF

$$
E^\circ = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF}
$$

### Step 3 — Final Nernst Equation

$$
E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q
$$

$$
E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[\text{Reactants}]}{[\text{Products}]}
$$

For the $\text{H}_2/\text{O}_2$ fuel cell, with partial pressures $P_{\text{H}_2}$, $P_{\text{O}_2}$, $P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}$:

$$
E_{\text{rev}} = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left( \frac{P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}} \right)
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Thermoneutral Voltage

Analogous to $E_{\text{rev}}$, the **thermoneutral voltage** $E_{th}$ is the voltage at which the cell operates if **all** the chemical enthalpy (HHV or LHV) were converted into electrical energy with no heat generation or absorption:

### Step 1 — Enthalpy balance

$$
nF \cdot E_{th} = -\Delta H
$$

### Step 2 — Solve

$$
E_{th}^{\text{HHV}} = -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{HHV}}}{nF} = \frac{285{,}800 \ \text{J/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \ \text{C/mol}} = 1.481 \ \text{V}
$$

$$
E_{th}^{\text{LHV}} = -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{LHV}}}{nF} = \frac{241{,}800 \ \text{J/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \ \text{C/mol}} = 1.253 \ \text{V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Operating a fuel cell **above** $E_{th}$ requires **endothermic heat input** (heat must be supplied to maintain the reaction). Operating **below** $E_{th}$ results in **waste heat generation**, which is the normal mode.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Total Heat Generated in a Real Fuel Cell

### Step 1 — Start from the First Law

$$
Q_{\text{heat}} = -\Delta H - W_{\text{elec}} = -\Delta H - nFE_{\text{cell}}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $\Delta H = -nF E_{th}$

$$
Q_{\text{heat}} = -(-nF E_{th}) - nF E_{\text{cell}} = nF(E_{th} - E_{\text{cell}})
$$

This is the **waste heat** that must be removed by cooling in real operation.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (HHV Basis)

**Problem:** A hydrogen PEMFC stack has the following parameters:
- $V_{\text{cell}} = 0.70 \ \text{V}$
- $E_{\text{rev}} = 1.229 \ \text{V}$
- $E_{th} = 1.481 \ \text{V}$
- Fuel utilization $= 0.96$

Compute the **Overall Stack Efficiency**.

### Step 1 — Thermodynamic Efficiency (HHV basis)

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{E_{\text{rev}}}{E_{th}} = \frac{1.229}{1.481} = 0.830 \quad \text{or} \quad 83.0\%
$$

### Step 2 — Voltage Efficiency

$$
\eta_v = \frac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E_{\text{rev}}} = \frac{0.70}{1.229} = 0.5695 \quad \text{or} \quad 56.95\%
$$

### Step 3 — Fuel Efficiency

$$
\eta_f = 0.96 \quad \text{or} \quad 96.0\%
$$

### Step 4 — Overall Stack Efficiency

$$
\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{th} \times \eta_v \times \eta_f = 0.830 \times 0.5695 \times 0.96
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{overall}} = 0.830 \times 0.5467 = 0.4538 \quad \text{or} \quad 45.4\%
$$

This is realistic for a well-designed PEMFC system. The remaining ~55% is mostly waste heat and a small fraction of unreacted fuel.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Which Loss Dominates?

| Loss Mechanism | Dominant In | Magnitude | Mitigation Strategy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Activation Loss | Low-T PEMFC, PAFC | 0.1 – 0.4 V | Better catalysts (Pt-alloys), higher $T$ |
| Ohmic Loss | All types | Linear in $I$ | Thinner electrolyte, better current collectors |
| Concentration Loss | High current density | Sharp at limiting current | Optimized GDL, better flow fields |
| Fuel Crossover | PEMFC (H$_2$ through Nafion) | 1 – 5% loss | Thinner/more selective membranes |
| Internal Currents | SOFC, MCFC | Variable | Dense electrolytes, blocking layers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fuel Cell Efficiency Cascade

The diagram below shows how chemical energy is progressively reduced to useful electrical work through three layers of efficiency filtering.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Input H2 Chemical Energy] --> B[Total Chemical Energy Delta H]
    B --> C{Thermodynamic Filter eta_th}
    C -->|eta_th approx 0.83 HHV| D[Gibbs Free Energy Delta G]
    D --> E{Reversible Voltage Filter E_rev}
    E --> F[Reversible Cell Voltage E_rev approx 1.229 V]
    F --> G{Voltage Filter eta_v}
    G -->|eta_v approx 0.57| H[Actual Operating Voltage V_cell approx 0.7 V]
    H --> I{Fuel Utilization Filter eta_f}
    I -->|eta_f approx 0.96| J[Net Useful Electrical Work Output]
    J --> K[Balance of Plant BOP Loss]
    K --> L[Final AC Electrical Output to Grid]

    C -.Rejected Heat T Delta S.-> M[Waste Heat Q1]
    G -.Activation Ohmic Concentration.-> N[Waste Heat Q2]
    I -.Unreacted Fuel Crossover.-> O[Unreacted Fuel Out]
    K -.Inverter Pump Blower.-> P[Parasitic Power Loss]
```

## 4.2 Energy Flow Schematic — Mapping of Loss Channels

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In[Fuel H2 In] --> Stoich[Stoichiometric Flow Control]
    Stoich --> Stack[Electrochemical Stack]
    Stack -->|eta_th eta_v| DCOut[DC Power Out]
    Stack -->|Q_heat| Thermal[Thermal Output CHP]
    Stack -->|Unreacted H2| Vent[Vent Loss]
    Stack -->|Crossover| Membrane[Crossover Loss]
    DCOut --> Inv[DC-AC Inverter]
    Inv -->|eta_BOP| ACOut[AC Power Out]
    Inv -->|Parasitic| Pump[Blower Compressor Loss]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Performance Curve — Voltage vs Current Density

The voltage efficiency is best visualized as the ratio of the operating point on the polarization curve to the reversible ceiling.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Polarization Curve Coordinate Plane] --> P1[Open Circuit Voltage E_rev 1.229 V at 0 A]
    P1 --> P2[Activation Drop Region eta_act dominant]
    P2 --> P3[Linear Ohmic Region eta_ohm dominant]
    P3 --> P4[Concentration Drop Region eta_conc dominant]
    P4 --> P5[Limiting Current Plateau]

    P2 -.Voltage Efficiency about 75 percent.-> V1[Operating Point Typical PEMFC]
    P3 -.Voltage Efficiency about 60 percent.-> V2[Rated Operating Point 0.7 V]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every step downward from $E_{\text{rev}}$ represents energy lost as heat. The "gap" between $V_{\text{cell}}$ and $E_{\text{rev}}$ multiplied by the current $I$ gives the **total power loss** in Watts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Thermodynamic Efficiency of a Fuel Cell.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECHT721]**

**Model Answer:**

Thermodynamic efficiency is the ratio of the maximum useful electrical work obtainable from the electrochemical reaction to the total chemical energy released by the fuel:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = \frac{-nFE_{\text{rev}}}{-nFE_{th}} = \frac{E_{\text{rev}}}{E_{th}}
$$

For the standard $\text{H}_2/\text{O}_2$ reaction at 298 K, $\Delta G = -237.2$ kJ/mol and $\Delta H = -285.8$ kJ/mol (HHV), giving $\eta_{th} \approx 83\%$. It represents the **fundamental thermodynamic ceiling** imposed by the Second Law, independent of any device design.

### Question 2: What is Fuel Utilization Efficiency? Mention one cause of its reduction.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PECHT721]**

**Model Answer:**

Fuel utilization efficiency (Faradaic efficiency) is the ratio of the fuel consumed in the electrochemical reaction to the total fuel supplied:

$$
\eta_f = \frac{\dot{n}_{\text{consumed}}}{\dot{n}_{\text{supplied}}}
$$

A **common cause** of its reduction is **fuel crossover** through the membrane electrolyte. For example, in a PEMFC, molecular hydrogen diffuses directly through the Nafion membrane from anode to cathode without producing current, reducing the effective $\eta_f$ to typically 95 – 99%.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse HHV and LHV in $\eta_{th}$ calculations — examiners specifically test whether students pick the correct heating value basis based on the application context (automotive = LHV, stationary = HHV).
> - **Always** explicitly state the sign convention of $\Delta G$ (negative for spontaneous reaction).
> - **Show all steps** including unit conversions (kJ → J) before substituting into $nFE$.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Efficiency Analysis of a PEMFC

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, PECHT721]**

A Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell operates on pure hydrogen and air at 1 atm and 80 °C. The relevant data is:

- Gibbs free energy change: $\Delta G = -226.3$ kJ/mol at 80 °C
- HHV: $\Delta H = -285.8$ kJ/mol
- $V_{\text{cell}} = 0.68$ V at rated current
- Fuel utilization = 97%
- $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol

#### Part (a) — Compute the Three Individual Efficiencies (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Thermodynamic Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H_{\text{HHV}}} = \frac{226.3}{285.8} = 0.792 \quad \text{or} \quad 79.2\%
$$

**Step 2 — Reversible Voltage:**

$$
E_{\text{rev}} = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF} = \frac{226{,}300}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 1.173 \ \text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Voltage Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_v = \frac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E_{\text{rev}}} = \frac{0.68}{1.173} = 0.5797 \quad \text{or} \quad 57.97\%
$$

**Step 4 — Fuel Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_f = 0.97 \quad \text{or} \quad 97.0\%
$$

#### Part (b) — Overall Efficiency and Waste Heat Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 5 — Overall Stack Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{th} \times \eta_v \times \eta_f = 0.792 \times 0.5797 \times 0.97
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{overall}} = 0.792 \times 0.5623 = 0.4454 \quad \text{or} \quad 44.54\%
$$

**Step 6 — Thermoneutral Voltage:**

$$
E_{th} = \frac{285{,}800}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 1.481 \ \text{V}
$$

**Step 7 — Waste Heat Generated per mole of H$_2$:**

$$
Q_{\text{heat}} = nF(E_{th} - V_{\text{cell}}) = 2 \times 96{,}485 \times (1.481 - 0.68)
$$

$$
Q_{\text{heat}} = 192{,}970 \times 0.801 = 154{,}569 \ \text{J/mol} \approx 154.6 \ \text{kJ/mol}
$$

**Conclusion:** Roughly 55% of the input chemical energy is dissipated as heat, which is recoverable in CHP applications — boosting total (electrical + thermal) efficiency to about 80%.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Voltage Efficiency and Nernst Analysis

**[KTU University Exam — Alternate Module 1 Choice, 2024 Scheme]**

A hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell operates at 100 °C and 2 atm total pressure. The partial pressures are $P_{\text{H}_2} = 0.8$ atm and $P_{\text{O}_2} = 0.2$ atm (water produced as vapor, $P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 1.0$ atm saturation at 100 °C).

#### Part (a) — Derive the Nernst Equation and Compute Reversible Voltage (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — General Nernst Expression:**

For the reaction $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$:

$$
E_{\text{rev}} = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left( \frac{P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Standard EMF at 298 K:**

$$
E^\circ_{298} = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ_{298}}{nF} = \frac{237{,}200}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 1.229 \ \text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply correction for 100 °C (373 K):** Using temperature-corrected $E^\circ_{373} \approx 1.21$ V (tabulated), or recalculating:

$$
E^\circ_{373} = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ_{373}}{2F} = \frac{228{,}200}{192{,}970} \approx 1.183 \ \text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute operating pressures at 373 K:**

$$
E_{\text{rev}} = 1.183 + \frac{8.314 \times 373}{2 \times 96{,}485} \ln\left( \frac{0.8 \times 0.2^{1/2}}{1.0} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the logarithmic term:**

$$
\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{8.314 \times 373}{192{,}970} = 0.01608 \ \text{V}
$$

$$
\ln\left( \frac{0.8 \times 0.4472}{1.0} \right) = \ln(0.3578) = -1.027
$$

**Step 6 — Final Reversible Voltage:**

$$
E_{\text{rev}} = 1.183 + 0.01608 \times (-1.027) = 1.183 - 0.0165 = 1.1665 \ \text{V}
$$

#### Part (b) — Compute Voltage Efficiency and Total Losses (7 Marks)

**Step 7 — Assume Actual Operating Voltage $V_{\text{cell}} = 0.72$ V:**

$$
\eta_v = \frac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E_{\text{rev}}} = \frac{0.72}{1.1665} = 0.6172 \quad \text{or} \quad 61.72\%
$$

**Step 8 — Total Polarization Loss:**

$$
\eta_{\text{total loss}} = E_{\text{rev}} - V_{\text{cell}} = 1.1665 - 0.72 = 0.4465 \ \text{V}
$$

**Step 9 — Typical Breakdown of Losses:**

| Loss Type | Value (V) | Contribution |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Activation (anode + cathode) | 0.20 | 44.8% |
| Ohmic (membrane + contact) | 0.15 | 33.6% |
| Concentration | 0.0965 | 21.6% |
| **Total** | **0.4465** | **100%** |

**Step 10 — Power Loss at 1 A:**

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = I \times (E_{\text{rev}} - V_{\text{cell}}) = 1 \times 0.4465 = 0.4465 \ \text{W per cell}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Frequency KTU 2024 Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Thermodynamic Efficiency** = $\Delta G / \Delta H$ — fixed by chemistry, **NOT** by cell design.
- ✅ **HHV basis** $\rightarrow$ $\eta_{th} \approx 83\%$ for $\text{H}_2/\text{O}_2$; **LHV basis** $\rightarrow$ $\approx 98\%$.
- ✅ **Voltage Efficiency** is the **largest controllable loss** in fuel cells — improving catalyst and membrane conductivity yields the biggest gains.
- ✅ **Fuel Efficiency** approaches unity for SOFC (no crossover) but is 95 – 99% for PEMFC.
- ✅ **Nernst Equation** provides $E_{\text{rev}}$ as a function of $T$, $P$, and partial pressures; must be used before computing $\eta_v$.
- ✅ **Thermoneutral Voltage** $E_{th} = -\Delta H / (nF)$: above this = endothermic cell; below = heat-generating cell (normal mode).
- ✅ **Overall Efficiency = $\eta_{th} \times \eta_v \times \eta_f$** — do NOT add them; they multiply.
- ✅ **Faraday Constant** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol — every efficiency equation ultimately traces back to this number.
- ✅ **Sign Convention:** Both $\Delta G$ and $\Delta H$ are **negative** for spontaneous fuel cell reactions; use absolute values in efficiency ratios.
- ✅ **Waste Heat** $Q_{\text{heat}} = nF(E_{th} - V_{\text{cell}})$ — recoverable in CHP systems, pushing total system efficiency above 85%.
- ✅ For a PEMFC operating at 0.7 V, $\eta_v \approx 57\%$ — this is the standard benchmark KTU examiners expect.
- ✅ **Automobile FCEVs** use LHV (exhaust water is vapor); **stationary units** use HHV (condensed water).
- ✅ **System Efficiency** further multiplied by $\eta_{\text{BOP}}$ (~90 – 95%) for the final grid-output figure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrochemical Reaction Fundamentals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT721 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrochemical Reaction Fundamentals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

An **electrochemical reaction** is a redox (reduction-oxidation) process in which chemical energy is directly interconverted with electrical energy via the transfer of electrons across an interface between an electrode and an ionic conductor (electrolyte). In the context of fuel cell technology, electrochemical reactions describe the **anodic oxidation of fuel** (e.g., hydrogen) and the **cathodic reduction of oxidant** (e.g., oxygen), coupled through an external circuit that drives useful electrical work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Electrochemical Reaction Fundamentals form the *thermodynamic and kinetic backbone* of every fuel cell. You will not be able to derive cell voltage, explain irreversibilities, or compute efficiency without mastering the Nernst Equation, Butler–Volmer Kinetics, and Tafel Analysis covered in this section.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dam-and-Turbine" Picture

Imagine a **water reservoir at a height**. The potential energy of the water is like the **chemical potential** of fuel molecules. When water flows downhill through a turbine, it produces mechanical work — this is exactly like electrons flowing from a higher energy level (anode, e.g., H₂) to a lower energy level (cathode, e.g., O₂) through an external circuit, producing electrical work.

- **Electrolyte** = the narrow pipe connecting two reservoirs (allows ions, not electrons).
- **Membrane (PEM/AEM/SOFC electrolyte)** = selective barrier.
- **Electrode potential (E)** = the height difference driving force.
- **Overpotential (η)** = friction losses in the pipe and turbine (irreversibility).

### 1.3 The Two Half-Reactions in a Generic Fuel Cell

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Anode (Oxidation):} \quad & \text{Fuel} \rightarrow \text{Oxidised Product} + n e^{-} \\
\text{Cathode (Reduction):} \quad & \text{Oxidant} + n e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Reduced Product} \\
\text{Overall:} \quad & \text{Fuel} + \text{Oxidant} \rightarrow \text{Products} + \text{Electrical Work} + \text{Heat}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the canonical **Hydrogen-Oxygen Fuel Cell**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Anode (HOR):} \quad & \mathrm{H_2} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{H^{+}} + 2e^{-} \\
\text{Cathode (ORR):} \quad & \tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{O_2} + 2\mathrm{H^{+}} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H_2O} \\
\text{Net:} \quad & \mathrm{H_2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H_2O} \quad (E^{0}_{cell} = 1.229\,\mathrm{V})
\end{aligned}
$$

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants (Memorise!)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Constants for KTU Board Exams**
> - **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96{,}485\ \mathrm{C\ mol^{-1}}$
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314\ \mathrm{J\ mol^{-1}\ K^{-1}}$
> - **Absolute Temperature (STP):** $T_{std} = 298.15\ \mathrm{K}$
> - **Transfer Coefficient Range:** $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ (typically $0.3$ to $0.7$)
> - **Standard Cell Voltage (H₂/O₂):** $\Delta E^{0} = 1.229\ \mathrm{V}$ (acidic) and $1.229\ \mathrm{V}$ (theoretical, no losses)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Electrode Potential & The Nernst Equation

The **Standard Electrode Potential** ($E^{0}$) is defined as the potential of a half-cell measured against the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** at $T = 298.15\ \mathrm{K}$, $P = 1\ \mathrm{atm}$, and unit activity of all species.

For a generic half-reaction:

$$
aA + ne^{-} \rightleftharpoons bB
$$

The reversible cell potential at any state is given by the **Nernst Equation**:

$$
E = E^{0} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q
$$

where $Q$ is the reaction quotient defined as $Q = \dfrac{a_B^{\,b}}{a_A^{\,a}}$ (products over reactants, excluding pure solids and electrons).

**Why this matters in fuel cells:** The actual operating voltage of a fuel cell drops as current is drawn because reactant concentrations decrease and product concentrations (especially water) increase. The Nernst equation quantifies this *reversible* drop — it is the *theoretical maximum voltage* available at a given operating point.

### 2.2 The Three Irreversibilities — Overpotentials

Real fuel cells never achieve the Nernst potential at finite current. The deviation is called **overpotential (η)**. Three distinct types exist:

1. **Activation Overpotential (η_act):** Energy barrier to charge transfer at electrode-electrolyte interface. Dominant at **low currents**.
2. **Concentration Overpotential (η_conc):** Mass-transport limitations — reactants cannot arrive at the catalyst surface fast enough. Dominant at **high currents**.
3. **Ohmic Overpotential (η_ohmic):** Ionic resistance of electrolyte and contact resistance of components. Follows **Ohm's Law** throughout the operating range.

**Operating Cell Voltage Equation (the "Fuel Cell Voltage Equation"):**

$$
V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act,a} - \eta_{act,c} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}
$$

### 2.3 Butler–Volmer Equation (The Heart of Electrochemical Kinetics)

For a single-electron transfer step, the net current density is the difference between forward (anodic) and reverse (cathodic) currents:

$$
j = j_{0}\left[\exp\left(\frac{\alpha_{a} F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_{c} F \eta}{RT}\right)\right]
$$

where:
- $j_{0}$ = **exchange current density** (kinetic metric of catalyst activity).
- $\alpha_{a}, \alpha_{c}$ = anodic and cathodic **transfer coefficients** (sum ≈ 1 for a single step).
- $\eta$ = activation overpotential.

### 2.4 Tafel Approximation (High-Overpotential Limit)

When $\vert \eta \vert \gg \dfrac{RT}{F}$, one exponential dominates. The **Tafel Equation** emerges:

$$
\eta = \pm \frac{RT}{\alpha F}\ln\left(\frac{j}{j_{0}}\right) = \pm \frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha F}\log_{10}\left(\frac{j}{j_{0}}\right)
$$

A **Tafel Plot** of $\eta$ vs $\log_{10}(j)$ yields a straight line whose slope gives $\alpha$ and intercept gives $j_{0}$ — a routine KTU numerical problem.

### 2.5 Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis

**First Law:** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is proportional to the charge passed.

**Second Law:** For a given charge, the mass liberated is proportional to the equivalent weight (molar mass ÷ $n$).

Combined:

$$
m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}
$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nernst Equation | $E = E^{0} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | $E$ in V, $Q$ dimensionless | Reversible cell potential at operating conditions |
| 2 | Cell Voltage | $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \sum \eta_i$ | All in V | Real operating voltage |
| 3 | Butler–Volmer | $j = j_{0}\left[\exp(\alpha_{a}F\eta/RT) - \exp(-\alpha_{c}F\eta/RT)\right]$ | $j$ in $\mathrm{A/m^{2}}$, $j_{0}$ in $\mathrm{A/m^{2}}$ | Full kinetic description |
| 4 | Tafel (Anodic) | $\eta_{a} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha_{a}F}\ln(j/j_{0})$ | $\eta$ in V | High-η anodic kinetics |
| 5 | Tafel (Cathodic) | $\eta_{c} = -\dfrac{RT}{\alpha_{c}F}\ln(j/j_{0})$ | $\eta$ in V | High-η cathodic kinetics (ORR) |
| 6 | Faraday's Law | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | $m$ in kg, $I$ in A, $t$ in s | Mass of fuel consumed |
| 7 | $\Delta G^{0} = -nFE^{0}$ | $\Delta G^{0}$ in J/mol | $E^{0}$ in V | Thermodynamic max work |
| 8 | Standard H₂/O₂ Potential | $E^{0} = 1.229\ \mathrm{V}$ | Constant | Reference benchmark |
| 9 | $RT/F$ at 298 K | $0.02569\ \mathrm{V}$ | Constant | Thermal voltage |
| 10 | $2.303\,RT/F$ at 298 K | $0.05916\ \mathrm{V}$ | Constant | Base-10 Nernst constant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** At KTU 2024 standard temperature $T = 298\ \mathrm{K}$, remember the numerical shortcuts $\dfrac{RT}{F} = 0.0257\ \mathrm{V}$ and $\dfrac{2.303\,RT}{F} = 0.0592\ \mathrm{V}$. Examiners *love* asking numericals where you must compute Nernst potentials with these constants.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Catalyst Design:** The exchange current density $j_{0}$ is a *figure of merit* for electrode catalysts. Pt has $j_{0} \sim 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}\ \mathrm{A/cm^{2}}$ for HOR/ORR — orders of magnitude higher than non-precious alternatives, justifying its dominance.
- **Polarisation Curve Fitting:** Engineers extract $\alpha$ and $j_{0}$ from experimental polarisation data to validate catalyst performance.
- **Fuel Cell Sizing:** Faraday's law predicts the stoichiometric fuel flow required for a desired current output, critical for stack design.
- **Loss Accounting:** The Nernst-plus-overpotentials framework enables loss breakdown in any PEMFC, SOFC, or PAFC.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (from Gibbs Free Energy)

**Starting Point — Thermodynamic Link to Potential:**

The change in Gibbs free energy of an electrochemical reaction is related to the cell potential by:

$$
\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}
$$

At standard conditions: $\Delta G^{0} = -nFE^{0}$.

**Step 1 — General Thermodynamic Expression:**

For any reaction at non-standard conditions, the Gibbs free energy change is:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^{0} + RT \ln Q
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Electrochemical Relations:**

Replace $\Delta G$ with $-nFE$ and $\Delta G^{0}$ with $-nFE^{0}$:

$$
-nFE = -nFE^{0} + RT \ln Q
$$

**Step 3 — Divide Both Sides by $-nF$:**

$$
E = E^{0} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q
$$

> **Logic Check:** At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$ which forces $E = 0$ (by definition of a complete cell). Substituting $Q = K$ (equilibrium constant) yields the **Nernst Equation for the standard potential in terms of $K$:** $E^{0} = \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln K$. This is consistent and widely tested.

### 3.2 Numerical Example 1 — Nernst Voltage of a PEMFC

**Problem:** A PEMFC operates at $T = 353\ \mathrm{K}$ ($80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$) with $P_{H_2} = 1.5\ \mathrm{atm}$, $P_{O_2} = 1.0\ \mathrm{atm}$. Compute the Nernst cell voltage. Given $E^{0}_{cell} = 1.229\ \mathrm{V}$ at 353 K (use temperature-corrected value if provided, otherwise standard).

**Solution:**

The overall cell reaction is $\mathrm{H_2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H_2O_{(l)}}$. So $Q = \dfrac{1}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}$ (pure liquid water has $a = 1$).

**Step 1:** Identify $n = 2$ (two electrons transferred per H₂).

**Step 2:** Compute the reaction quotient:

$$
Q = \frac{1}{(1.5)(1.0)^{1/2}} = \frac{1}{1.5} = 0.6667
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the thermal voltage at 353 K:

$$
\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96485)} = 0.01521\ \mathrm{V}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the Nernst equation:

$$
E = 1.229 - (0.01521)\ln(0.6667)
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate $\ln(0.6667) = -0.4055$:

$$
E = 1.229 - (0.01521)(-0.4055) = 1.229 + 0.00617 = 1.235\ \mathrm{V}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A small increase above $1.229\ \mathrm{V}$ because we pressurised H₂ above 1 atm. This is the *theoretical maximum* open-circuit voltage at operating conditions — the actual OCV will be lower due to mixed potentials and internal currents.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Butler–Volmer Equation

**Starting Assumptions:**
- The electrode reaction is elementary (single-step).
- The potential energy barrier at the electrode-electrolyte interface is symmetric but tilts linearly with applied overpotential.
- The transfer coefficient $\alpha$ quantifies the *fraction* of the overpotential that assists the *cathodic* direction (and $1-\alpha$ for anodic).

**Step 1 — Forward (Anodic) Current Density:**

The anodic current density depends exponentially on the *lowered* energy barrier for the forward direction:

$$
j_{a} = j_{0}\exp\left(\frac{\alpha_{a} F \eta}{RT}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Reverse (Cathodic) Current Density:**

Similarly, the cathodic current density depends exponentially on the barrier lowering in the reverse direction:

$$
j_{c} = -j_{0}\exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_{c} F \eta}{RT}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Net Current Density:**

By Kirchhoff's current law (net = anodic − cathodic):

$$
j = j_{a} - j_{c} = j_{0}\left[\exp\left(\frac{\alpha_{a} F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_{c} F \eta}{RT}\right)\right]
$$

This is the **Butler–Volmer Equation**. At $\eta = 0$, both exponentials equal 1 and $j = 0$ (equilibrium).

### 3.4 Derivation of the Tafel Equation (from Butler–Volmer)

**Step 1 — Assume Large Cathodic Overpotential:**

For ORR, $\vert \eta_{c} \vert$ is large and negative. The first exponential in Butler–Volmer becomes negligibly small (since $\alpha_{a}\eta \to$ a large negative number kills it):

$$
\exp\left(\frac{\alpha_{a} F \eta}{RT}\right) \to 0
$$

**Step 2 — Butler–Volmer Reduces To:**

$$
j \approx -j_{0}\exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_{c} F \eta}{RT}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Take the Natural Logarithm and Solve for $\eta$:**

$$
\ln\left(\frac{j}{j_{0}}\right) = -\frac{\alpha_{c} F \eta}{RT}
$$

$$
\eta = -\frac{RT}{\alpha_{c} F}\ln\left(\frac{j}{j_{0}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Base-10:**

$$
\eta = -\frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_{c} F}\log_{10}\left(\frac{j}{j_{0}}\right)
$$

> This is the **cathodic Tafel equation**. The slope $\dfrac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_{c}F}$ is called the **Tafel slope**, typically $60$–$120\ \mathrm{mV/decade}$ for fuel cell cathodes.

### 3.5 Numerical Example 2 — Tafel Slope and Overpotential

**Problem:** A fuel cell cathode has $j_{0} = 10^{-4}\ \mathrm{A/cm^{2}}$ and $\alpha_{c} = 0.5$. Compute the activation overpotential at $j = 1.0\ \mathrm{A/cm^{2}}$ and at $T = 333\ \mathrm{K}$. Also determine the Tafel slope.

**Step 1 — Compute Thermal Voltage:**

$$
\frac{RT}{\alpha_{c}F} = \frac{(8.314)(333)}{(0.5)(96485)} = 0.0574\ \mathrm{V}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Tafel Slope:**

$$
\text{Tafel Slope} = \frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_{c}F} = 2.303 \times 0.0574 = 0.1322\ \mathrm{V/decade} = 132.2\ \mathrm{mV/dec}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Tafel Equation:**

$$
\eta_{c} = -(0.0574)\ln\left(\frac{1.0}{10^{-4}}\right) = -(0.0574)\ln(10^{4}) = -(0.0574)(9.21) = -0.529\ \mathrm{V}
$$

**Interpretation:** The negative sign denotes a cathodic overpotential (voltage loss). A 0.529 V loss just from cathode activation — this is why ORR is the **rate-limiting reaction** in PEMFCs and why researchers invest heavily in cathode catalyst engineering.

### 3.6 Faraday's Law Numerical Example

**Problem:** A PEMFC stack must deliver $I = 100\ \mathrm{A}$ for $t = 1\ \mathrm{hr}$. How many grams of H₂ are consumed? ($M_{H_2} = 2.016\ \mathrm{g/mol}$, $n = 2$)

**Step 1 — Total Charge:**

$$
Q = I \cdot t = 100 \times 3600 = 3.6 \times 10^{5}\ \mathrm{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Faraday's Law:**

$$
m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{(3.6 \times 10^{5})(2.016)}{(2)(96485)} = \frac{7.258 \times 10^{5}}{192970} = 3.76\ \mathrm{g}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This stoichiometric minimum is the basis for fuel supply calculations. Real systems feed 1.2–2× this stoichiometric ratio to ensure complete reaction and avoid fuel starvation.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Electrochemical Cell — Component & Charge Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AnodeSide["ANODE COMPARTMENT"]
        A1[Hydrogen Fuel In]
        A2[Anode Catalyst Layer - Pt]
        A3[Electron Release H2 to 2H plus 2e]
    end

    subgraph ElectrolyteLayer["ELECTROLYTE / MEMBRANE"]
        E1[Ion Conduction - H plus or O2 minus]
        E2[Blocks Electrons]
    end

    subgraph CathodeSide["CATHODE COMPARTMENT"]
        C1[Oxygen or Air In]
        C2[Cathode Catalyst Layer - Pt]
        C3[ORR - O2 plus 4H plus to 2H2O]
    end

    subgraph ExternalCircuit["EXTERNAL CIRCUIT"]
        EC1[Electron Flow - Useful Current]
        EC2[Load - Resistor or Motor]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> A3
    A3 --> E1
    E1 --> C3
    C3 --> C2 --> C1
    A3 --> EC1
    EC1 --> EC2
    EC2 --> C3
    E2 -.separates.- EC1
```

```mermaid
    P1 -.compare.- N1

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q:** Define the **exchange current density ($j_{0}$)**. Why is a high $j_{0}$ desirable for a fuel cell electrode?

**Model Answer:**

The **exchange current density ($j_{0}$)** is defined as the rate of electron transfer (in $\mathrm{A/m^{2}}$ or $\mathrm{A/cm^{2}}$) at the electrode-electrolyte interface when the reaction is at **dynamic equilibrium** (i.e., the net current is zero, but forward and reverse reactions occur at equal rates).

From the Butler–Volmer equation, at $\eta = 0$:

$$
j_{a} = j_{c} = j_{0}
$$

**Why high $j_{0}$ is desirable:** A high $j_{0}$ implies the electrode reaction is *fast and reversible*, meaning only a small activation overpotential is required to drive a given current. In practical terms, a catalyst like **platinum** has $j_{0} \sim 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{A/cm^{2}}$ for the ORR — orders of magnitude greater than non-precious catalysts, which is why it is the benchmark material. A high $j_{0}$ directly translates to higher cell voltage at operating current and hence higher power density and efficiency.

**Keyword hits for examiner:** Equilibrium rate, forward = reverse, Butler–Volmer at $\eta = 0$, faster kinetics → lower overpotential.

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q:** Distinguish between **activation overpotential**, **concentration overpotential**, and **ohmic overpotential** in a fuel cell.

**Model Answer:**

| Overpotential Type | Physical Origin | Governing Equation | Dominant Region |
|---|---|---|---|
| Activation ($\eta_{act}$) | Energy barrier for charge transfer at electrode-electrolyte interface | Butler–Volmer / Tafel | **Low current** (start of polarisation curve) |
| Concentration ($\eta_{conc}$) | Slow mass transport of reactants/products to/from catalyst sites | $RT/nF \ln(j_L/(j_L - j))$ | **High current** (end of polarisation curve) |
| Ohmic ($\eta_{ohmic}$) | Ionic resistance in electrolyte + electronic contact resistance | $\eta_{ohmic} = IR_{ohmic}$ | **Linear, middle region** (throughout) |

**Examiner's logic:** Activation is *kinetic*, concentration is *mass-transport*, ohmic is *resistive*. Each dominates a different zone of the polarisation curve.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model 1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the Gibbs free energy expression, derive the **Nernst equation** for an electrochemical half-cell. Clearly state the meaning of each variable in your final expression.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A PEM fuel cell operates at $T = 343\ \mathrm{K}$ with $P_{H_2} = 2.0\ \mathrm{atm}$, $P_{O_2} = 0.5\ \mathrm{atm}$, and liquid water product. Calculate the Nernst cell voltage. Given $E^{0}_{cell} = 1.229\ \mathrm{V}$ at 343 K (assume standard value for calculation). Also compute the **Tafel slope** for the cathode with $\alpha_{c} = 0.5$.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Start with the thermodynamic identity:**

The Gibbs free energy change of an electrochemical reaction is related to the cell potential by $\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$, and at standard conditions $\Delta G^{0} = -nFE^{0}$.

**Step 2 — General expression at non-standard conditions:**

For any reaction $aA + ne^{-} \rightarrow bB$ at non-standard state:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^{0} + RT \ln Q
$$

where $Q$ is the reaction quotient, $R$ is the universal gas constant, and $T$ is absolute temperature.

**Step 3 — Substitute the electrochemical relations:**

Replace $\Delta G$ with $-nFE$ and $\Delta G^{0}$ with $-nFE^{0}$:

$$
-nFE = -nFE^{0} + RT \ln Q
$$

**Step 4 — Divide both sides by $-nF$:**

$$
E = E^{0} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q
$$

**Variable Definitions:**
- $E$ = reversible cell potential (V) at operating conditions.
- $E^{0}$ = standard electrode potential (V) at 298 K, 1 atm, unit activities.
- $R$ = universal gas constant = $8.314\ \mathrm{J\ mol^{-1}\ K^{-1}}$.
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K).
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction.
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = $96{,}485\ \mathrm{C\ mol^{-1}}$.
- $Q$ = reaction quotient (activities of products raised to stoichiometric powers, divided by reactants).

**Verification:** At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$ → $E = 0$, and $Q = K$ (equilibrium constant). Substituting yields $E^{0} = (RT/nF)\ln K$, the standard form of the Nernst equation relating $E^{0}$ to the equilibrium constant.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:** $T = 343\ \mathrm{K}$, $P_{H_2} = 2.0\ \mathrm{atm}$, $P_{O_2} = 0.5\ \mathrm{atm}$, liquid water product, $E^{0}_{cell} = 1.229\ \mathrm{V}$, $n = 2$, $\alpha_{c} = 0.5$.

**Step 1 — Write the overall reaction and $Q$:**

$$
\mathrm{H_2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H_2O_{(l)}}, \quad Q = \frac{a_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}
$$

Since liquid water has unit activity:

$$
Q = \frac{1}{(2.0)(0.5)^{1/2}} = \frac{1}{(2.0)(0.7071)} = \frac{1}{1.4142} = 0.7071
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the thermal voltage at 343 K:**

$$
\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{(8.314)(343)}{(2)(96485)} = \frac{2851.7}{192970} = 0.01478\ \mathrm{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Nernst equation:**

$$
E = 1.229 - (0.01478)\ln(0.7071)
$$

Since $\ln(0.7071) = -0.3466$:

$$
E = 1.229 - (0.01478)(-0.3466) = 1.229 + 0.00512 = 1.234\ \mathrm{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Tafel slope:**

$$
\text{Tafel slope} = \frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_{c}F} = \frac{(2.303)(8.314)(343)}{(0.5)(96485)} = \frac{6571.5}{48242.5} = 0.1363\ \mathrm{V/decade} = 136.3\ \mathrm{mV/dec}
$$

**Final Answer:** $E_{Nernst} = 1.234\ \mathrm{V}$ and Tafel slope = $136.3\ \mathrm{mV/decade}$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model 2 — Alternative]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Butler–Volmer equation** for a single-step electron transfer reaction. Define all variables and discuss the **two limits** — small overpotential and large overpotential — leading to the linear polarisation and Tafel regimes respectively.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A fuel cell cathode has an exchange current density $j_{0} = 5 \times 10^{-5}\ \mathrm{A/cm^{2}}$, transfer coefficient $\alpha_{c} = 0.6$, and operates at $T = 353\ \mathrm{K}$. Using the Tafel approximation, determine: (i) the activation overpotential at $j = 0.5\ \mathrm{A/cm^{2}}$, (ii) the current density at which the overpotential magnitude reaches $0.4\ \mathrm{V}$, and (iii) the corresponding Tafel slope in mV/decade.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Energy Barrier with Applied Overpotential:**

At an electrode-electrolyte interface, an electrochemical reaction must overcome an activation energy barrier. When an overpotential $\eta$ is applied, the barrier for the forward (anodic) direction is reduced by a fraction $\alpha_{a}$ of $F\eta$, and the barrier for the reverse (cathodic) direction is reduced by $\alpha_{c}$ of $F\eta$, where $\alpha_{a} + \alpha_{c} = 1$.

**Step 2 — Write the Anodic Current Density:**

By Arrhenius-type kinetics, the anodic current density is:

$$
j_{a} = j_{0}\exp\left(\frac{\alpha_{a}F\eta}{RT}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Write the Cathodic Current Density:**

$$
j_{c} = -j_{0}\exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_{c}F\eta}{RT}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Net Current Density (Kirchhoff's Law):**

$$
j = j_{a} - j_{c} = j_{0}\left[\exp\left(\frac{\alpha_{a}F\eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_{c}F\eta}{RT}\right)\right]
$$

**This is the Butler–Volmer Equation.**

**Limit 1 — Small Overpotential ($F\eta \ll RT$):**

Use Taylor expansion $\exp(x) \approx 1 + x$:

$$
j \approx j_{0}\left[\left(1 + \frac{\alpha_{a}F\eta}{RT}\right) - \left(1 - \frac{\alpha_{c}F\eta}{RT}\right)\right] = j_{0}\left(\frac{\alpha_{a} + \alpha_{c}}{RT}\right)F\eta
$$

Defining the **charge-transfer resistance** $R_{ct} = \dfrac{RT}{j_{0}(\alpha_{a}+\alpha_{c})F}$:

$$
\eta \approx R_{ct} \cdot j
$$

This is the **linear polarisation regime** (low current).

**Limit 2 — Large Cathodic Overpotential ($\alpha_{c}F\vert\eta\vert/RT \gg 1$):**

The anodic exponential vanishes:

$$
j \approx -j_{0}\exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_{c}F\eta}{RT}\right)
$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$
\eta = -\frac{RT}{\alpha_{c}F}\ln\left(\frac{j}{j_{0}}\right) = -\frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_{c}F}\log_{10}\left(\frac{j}{j_{0}}\right)
$$

This is the **Tafel regime** (high current, dominant activation loss).

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:** $j_{0} = 5 \times 10^{-5}\ \mathrm{A/cm^{2}}$, $\alpha_{c} = 0.6$, $T = 353\ \mathrm{K}$.

**Part (i) — Activation overpotential at $j = 0.5\ \mathrm{A/cm^{2}}$:**

Compute the Tafel prefactor:

$$
\frac{RT}{\alpha_{c}F} = \frac{(8.314)(353)}{(0.6)(96485)} = \frac{2934.8}{57891} = 0.0507\ \mathrm{V}
$$

Apply the Tafel equation:

$$
\eta_{c} = -(0.0507)\ln\left(\frac{0.5}{5 \times 10^{-5}}\right) = -(0.0507)\ln(10^{4}) = -(0.0507)(9.2103) = -0.467\ \mathrm{V}
$$

So $\vert \eta_{c} \vert = 0.467\ \mathrm{V}$.

**Part (ii) — Current density at $\vert \eta_{c} \vert = 0.4\ \mathrm{V}$:**

Rearrange the Tafel equation:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{j}{j_{0}}\right) = -\frac{\alpha_{c}F\eta_{c}}{RT}
$$

For $\eta_{c} = -0.4\ \mathrm{V}$:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{j}{j_{0}}\right) = -\frac{(0.6)(96485)(-0.4)}{(8.314)(353)} = \frac{23156.4}{2934.8} = 7.892
$$

$$
\frac{j}{j_{0}} = e^{7.892} = 2689
$$

$$
j = (2689)(5 \times 10^{-5}) = 0.1344\ \mathrm{A/cm^{2}}
$$

**Part (iii) — Tafel slope in mV/decade:**

$$
\text{Tafel slope} = \frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_{c}F} = 2.303 \times 0.0507 = 0.1168\ \mathrm{V/dec} = 116.8\ \mathrm{mV/dec}
$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $\eta_{c} = -0.467\ \mathrm{V}$, (ii) $j = 0.134\ \mathrm{A/cm^{2}}$ at $\eta_{c} = -0.4\ \mathrm{V}$, (iii) Tafel slope = $116.8\ \mathrm{mV/decade}$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this topic (KTU Board pattern):**
> 1. **Sign Error in Nernst Equation:** Forgetting that $Q$ has products in the *numerator* and reactants in the *denominator*. Confusing $Q$ with its inverse is the #1 mistake — costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Wrong $\alpha$ in Tafel Equation:** Using $\alpha_{a}$ when computing cathodic overpotential (or vice versa). Always check the *direction* of current flow and the *sign* of $\eta$.
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** $R = 8.314\ \mathrm{J\ mol^{-1}\ K^{-1}}$ and $F = 96{,}485\ \mathrm{C\ mol^{-1}}$ — do not substitute in calories or use $F = 96{,}500$ only when the question rounds. Show units in intermediate steps to get full credit.
> 4. **Confusing $E^{0}$ and $E$:** $E^{0}$ is *standard state*; $E$ (or $E_{Nernst}$) is *operating state*. The fuel cell voltage equation is $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \sum\eta$, not $E^{0}$.
> 5. **Skipping Sign Justification:** When asked for "overpotential magnitude," explicitly state $\vert \eta \vert$ and justify why the sign is negative (cathodic) or positive (anodic). Examiners award 1 mark for this.
> 6. **Forgetting $n = 2$:** In the H₂/O₂ cell, electrons transferred per H₂ molecule = 2. Per mole of O₂, $n = 4$. Read the question carefully!

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Electrochemical reactions** are redox processes that interconvert chemical and electrical energy via charge transfer at an electrode-electrolyte interface.
- **Anode = oxidation (loss of e⁻); Cathode = reduction (gain of e⁻).** In a galvanic (fuel cell) system, the anode is the negative terminal and the cathode is the positive terminal.
- **Nernst Equation** $E = E^{0} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ gives the *reversible* cell potential at any state.
- **Butler–Volmer Equation** describes *kinetics*: $j = j_{0}\left[\exp(\alpha_{a}F\eta/RT) - \exp(-\alpha_{c}F\eta/RT)\right]$.
- **Tafel Equation** is the high-$\eta$ limit: $\eta = -\dfrac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha F}\log_{10}(j/j_{0})$; the slope is typically $60$–$120\ \mathrm{mV/dec}$.
- **Three overpotentials:** Activation (kinetic), Ohmic (resistive), Concentration (mass-transport). They are *additive* and subtracted from the Nernst voltage to obtain the real operating voltage.
- **Exchange current density $j_{0}$** is a *figure of merit* for catalyst activity — higher $j_{0}$ means lower activation loss.
- **Transfer coefficient $\alpha$** quantifies barrier symmetry; for single-step reactions, $\alpha_{a} + \alpha_{c} = 1$, with $0.3 \le \alpha \le 0.7$ typical.
- **Key Constants to Memorise:** $F = 96{,}485\ \mathrm{C\ mol^{-1}}$, $R = 8.314\ \mathrm{J\ mol^{-1}\ K^{-1}}$, $E^{0}_{H_2/O_2} = 1.229\ \mathrm{V}$, $\Delta G^{0} = -nFE^{0}$.
- **Faraday's Law** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{nF}$ gives stoichiometric fuel consumption.
- **Limiting Current $j_L$:** The current beyond which the concentration overpotential diverges — a hard upper bound on fuel cell power.
- **Rule of Thumb for Tafel Slope:** $0.12\ \mathrm{V/dec}$ is a typical cathode benchmark for Pt-based ORR catalysts.
