# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define "Uniform Distribution of Reactants" in the context of a PEM fuel cell. Why is it critical for stack performance?**

**Model Answer:**
Uniform distribution of reactants refers to the homogeneous spatial delivery of hydrogen to the anode catalyst layer and oxygen (from air) to the cathode catalyst layer across the entire active area of the cell. It is critical because: (i) non-uniform distribution leads to localized current density variations, reducing the average cell voltage by 30–80 mV; (ii) it causes reactant starvation at low-flow regions and water flooding at high-flow regions; (iii) non-uniform thermal stresses accelerate membrane degradation and catalyst aging; and (iv) in multi-cell stacks, cell-to-cell imbalance reduces overall stack efficiency by 5–10 %.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Q: State Faraday's Law of Electrolysis and explain its significance in determining reactant consumption rate in a fuel cell.**

**Model Answer:**
Faraday's Law states that the mass (or moles) of a substance consumed at an electrode is directly proportional to the electric charge passed. Mathematically:

$$
\dot{n} = \frac{j \cdot A}{n \cdot F}
$$

where $\dot{n}$ is molar consumption rate (mol/s), $j$ is current density (A/m²), $A$ is active area (m²), $n$ is the number of electrons transferred per molecule (e.g., 4 for O₂), and $F = 96485$ C/mol is Faraday's constant. Its significance lies in establishing the **minimum stoichiometric flow** required to sustain a given current, which directly determines the design of flow fields, blowers, and storage systems for uniform reactant distribution.

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH — INTERNAL CHOICE)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Derive the expression for the pressure drop across a single rectangular flow-field channel of a PEM fuel cell, clearly stating all assumptions. Explain how this pressure drop affects the uniformity of reactant distribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PEMFC has an active area of 25 cm² operating at a current density of 1.2 A/cm². Air is supplied at stoichiometric ratio $\lambda = 2.5$ and pressure of 1.2 atm at 65 °C. The cathode flow field consists of 10 parallel straight channels, each of dimensions $w = 1$ mm, $h = 0.6$ mm, $L = 30$ cm. Calculate: (i) Total air flow rate required, (ii) Volumetric flow per channel, (iii) Pressure drop across the channel. Comment on uniformity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation of Pressure Drop Formula**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, incompressible, laminar flow (Re < 2000).
2. Rectangular channel with $w \gg h$ (wide parallel plates approximation).
3. Fully developed flow with no-slip boundary conditions.
4. Constant fluid viscosity along the channel.

For fully developed laminar flow between parallel plates, the Navier-Stokes equation reduces to:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2} = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{dP}{dx}
$$

Integrating twice with boundary conditions $v_x = 0$ at $y = 0$ and $y = h$:

$$
v_x(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu} \frac{dP}{dx} \cdot y(y - h)
$$

The volumetric flow rate per unit width is obtained by integrating across the gap:

$$
\frac{Q}{w} = \int_0^h v_x \, dy = -\frac{h^3}{12\mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}
$$

For uniform pressure gradient over length $L$:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{12 \mu L Q}{w h^3}
$$

**Effect on Uniformity:** The pressure drop drives convective mass transport. A higher $\Delta P$ ensures adequate reactant delivery to the channel outlet, improving uniformity. However, excessive $\Delta P$ requires more blower power, reducing net system efficiency. The design optimum is typically $\Delta P \leq 0.5$ atm.

**(b) Numerical Solution**

Given: $A = 25$ cm² $= 0.0025$ m², $j = 1.2$ A/cm² $= 12000$ A/m², $\lambda = 2.5$, $P = 1.2$ atm $= 121590$ Pa, $T = 338$ K, $N_{channels} = 10$, $w = 0.001$ m, $h = 0.0006$ m, $L = 0.3$ m, $\mu_{air} \approx 1.9 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s.

**Total Current:**
$$
I = j \cdot A = 12000 \times 0.0025 = 30 \text{ A}
$$

**(i) Total Air Flow Rate (Molar):**

Stoichiometric O₂ requirement (4-electron reaction):
$$
\dot{n}_{O_2,stoich} = \frac{I}{4F} = \frac{30}{4 \times 96485} = 7.77 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/s}
$$

With $\lambda = 2.5$:
$$
\dot{n}_{O_2,in} = \lambda \cdot \dot{n}_{O_2,stoich} = 2.5 \times 7.77 \times 10^{-5} = 1.943 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/s}
$$

Total air (assuming 21% O₂):
$$
\dot{n}_{air} = \frac{\dot{n}_{O_2,in}}{0.21} = \frac{1.943 \times 10^{-4}}{0.21} = 9.252 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/s}
$$

**(ii) Volumetric Flow Per Channel:**

Total volumetric flow:
$$
Q_{total} = \frac{\dot{n}_{air} R T}{P} = \frac{9.252 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.314 \times 338}{121590} = 2.14 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Per channel:
$$
Q = \frac{Q_{total}}{10} = 2.14 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**(iii) Pressure Drop Per Channel:**

$$
\Delta P = \frac{12 \mu L Q}{w h^3} = \frac{12 \times 1.9 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.3 \times 2.14 \times 10^{-6}}{0.001 \times (0.0006)^3}
$$

$$
\Delta P = \frac{1.464 \times 10^{-10}}{2.16 \times 10^{-13}} = 678 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.0067 \text{ atm}
$$

**Comment on Uniformity:** A $\Delta P$ of 678 Pa is very low, indicating **inadequate driving force for uniform distribution**. The parallel channel design with such low $\Delta P$ will exhibit **significant flow maldistribution** due to manufacturing tolerances in channel dimensions. A serpentine flow field or increased stoichiometry ($\lambda \geq 3.0$) is recommended.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the different types of flow-field designs used in PEM fuel cells. Compare their performance with respect to reactant distribution uniformity, pressure drop, and water management. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fuel cell stack with 50 cells operates at 0.65 V/cell and 80 A. Hydrogen is supplied at 1.8 atm and 25 °C with stoichiometric ratio $\lambda = 1.2$. Calculate: (i) H₂ flow rate required at the stack inlet, (ii) Average power output, (iii) If the flow uniformity index is 0.92, calculate the effective cell voltage considering distribution losses. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Flow Field Designs**

The major flow-field designs are:

**1. Parallel Flow Field:** Multiple straight parallel channels between inlet and outlet manifolds.
- **Uniformity:** Low — small pressure differential leads to flow maldistribution.
- **Pressure drop:** Very low.
- **Water management:** Poor — water droplets can accumulate and block channels.

**2. Single Serpentine Flow Field:** A single continuous channel that winds back and forth across the active area.
- **Uniformity:** High — long path length creates substantial pressure drop, forcing uniform flow.
- **Pressure drop:** High — requires powerful blower.
- **Water management:** Excellent — high velocity sweeps water droplets out.

**3. Multiple Serpentine:** Compromise between parallel and single serpentine (2–4 parallel serpentines).
- **Uniformity:** High.
- **Pressure drop:** Moderate-high.
- **Water management:** Good.

**4. Interdigitated Flow Field:** Inlet and outlet channels are not connected at the end — flow must pass through the GDL.
- **Uniformity:** Very high — forced convection through GDL.
- **Pressure drop:** Highest.
- **Water management:** Excellent — water is pushed out through GDL.

**5. Pin-Type (Metal Foam):** Array of pins or foam pillars in the channel.
- **Uniformity:** Moderate — tortuous path.
- **Pressure drop:** Moderate.
- **Water management:** Moderate.

**(b) Numerical Solution**

Given: $N = 50$ cells, $V_{cell} = 0.65$ V, $I = 80$ A, $P_{H_2} = 1.8$ atm $= 182385$ Pa, $T = 298$ K, $\lambda = 1.2$, $\eta_{uniformity} = 0.92$.

**(i) H₂ Flow Rate:**

H₂ consumption (2-electron reaction):
$$
\dot{n}_{H_2,stoich} = \frac{I}{2F} = \frac{80}{2 \times 96485} = 4.146 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/s}
$$

With stoichiometry:
$$
\dot{n}_{H_2,in} = \lambda \cdot \dot{n}_{H_2,stoich} = 1.2 \times 4.146 \times 10^{-4} = 4.975 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/s}
$$

Volumetric flow rate:
$$
Q_{H_2} = \frac{\dot{n}_{H_2,in} R T}{P} = \frac{4.975 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.314 \times 298}{182385}
$$

$$
Q_{H_2} = 6.76 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 6.76 \text{ mL/s} \approx 0.406 \text{ L/min}
$$

**(ii) Average Power Output:**

$$
P = N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I = 50 \times 0.65 \times 80 = 2600 \text{ W} = 2.6 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iii) Effective Voltage with Distribution Losses:**

The uniformity index $\eta = 0.92$ implies 8 % performance penalty. The effective voltage per cell is:

$$
V_{eff} = V_{cell} \cdot \eta = 0.65 \times 0.92 = 0.598 \text{ V}
$$

Effective stack power:
$$
P_{eff} = N \cdot V_{eff} \cdot I = 50 \times 0.598 \times 80 = 2392 \text{ W}
$$

Power loss due to non-uniformity:
$$
\Delta P = 2600 - 2392 = 208 \text{ W} \quad (\approx 8\% \text{ loss})
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (Most Common — Loses 2–3 Marks):** Students frequently forget to convert A/cm² to A/m². Always remember: $1 \text{ A/cm}^2 = 10{,}000 \text{ A/m}^2$. Mixing units results in answers off by factors of 10⁴.
> 2. **n-Value Confusion:** For H₂ (oxidation): $n = 2$ electrons. For O₂ (reduction): $n = 4$ electrons. Using $n = 4$ for H₂ consumption gives **wrong** flow rates.
> 3. **Stoichiometric vs. Utilization Confusion:** $\lambda$ and $U_f$ are **reciprocals only when reactants are fully consumed**: $U_f = 1/\lambda$. Do not assume this blindly; it's true only at the exit.
> 4. **Forgetting the Bruggeman Correction:** In GDL diffusion problems, using $D$ directly instead of $D^{eff} = D \varepsilon^{1.5}$ is a **3-mark penalty**.
> 5. **Missing the Pressure-Drop Cubic Dependence:** Many students treat $\Delta P$ as proportional to $1/h$, but it is actually $\propto 1/h^3$. This is a high-weight concept.
> 6. **Confusing Flow Field Types:** A frequent mistake is claiming "parallel flow fields give best uniformity" — actually the opposite is true. **Serpentine/Interdigitated** give best uniformity.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS — MEMORIZE BEFORE EXAM**

- ✅ **Definition:** Uniform distribution = equal local current density across active area ($j(x,y) = \bar{j}$).
- ✅ **Faraday's Law:** $\dot{n} = jA/(nF)$ — the foundation of all stoichiometric calculations.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric ratio** $\lambda > 1$ always required; typical: H₂ $\lambda = 1.1-1.2$, Air $\lambda = 2.0-3.0$.
- ✅ **Critical air threshold:** For air ($\approx 21\% \text{ O}_2$), $\lambda < 2.38$ causes cathode outlet starvation.
- ✅ **Channel pressure drop formula:** $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu L Q}{w h^3}$ — **cubic dependence on gap height**.
- ✅ **Effective diffusion in GDL:** $D^{eff} = D \cdot \varepsilon^{1.5}$ (Bruggeman correction, exponent = 1.5).
- ✅ **Darcy's Law for GDL flow:** $v = -(k/\mu) \nabla P$ — controls in-plane and through-plane flow.
- ✅ **Knudsen diffusion** dominates in micropores (< 100 nm); molecular diffusion in macropores.
- ✅ **Flow field ranking for uniformity:** Interdigitated > Serpentine > Multi-serpentine > Pin > Parallel.
- ✅ **Trade-off:** Higher uniformity ↔ Higher pressure drop ↔ Higher parasitic power.
- ✅ **Concentration profile along channel:** Linear decrease in $x_{O_2}(x)$ for uniform $j$ assumption.
- ✅ **Non-uniformity consequences:** Starvation, flooding, hot spots, membrane degradation, voltage loss (30–80 mV).
- ✅ **Uniformity metric:** $RDU = (1 - \sigma_j/\bar{j}) \times 100\%$; aim for $> 95\%$ in commercial stacks.
- ✅ **Stack-level distribution:** Manifold design is critical for cell-to-cell balance; coefficient of variation of $\dot{n}_{cell} < 5\%$ target.
- ✅ **Temperature effect:** Higher $T$ → higher $D$ → better distribution, but membrane drying risk.
- ✅ **Pressure effect:** Higher $P$ → higher $C$ (concentration) → better distribution, but compressor work penalty.

> [!NOTE]
> **FINAL EXAM TIP:** In KTU 14-mark questions, **always draw a labeled diagram** of the flow field with inlet/outlet arrows before starting numerical calculations. Examiners allocate **1–2 marks** for proper schematic representation, which is the easiest mark to earn. Additionally, always **state assumptions explicitly** at the start of derivations — this demonstrates engineering rigor and earns partial credit even if the final calculation is incomplete.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT721 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Heat Removal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Heat Removal** in a fuel cell system refers to the systematic management and dissipation of the waste thermal energy generated as a by-product of the electrochemical reactions and internal irreversibilities (overpotentials) occurring within the cell. In the context of KTU 2024 scheme, heat removal is defined as the controlled extraction of heat from the fuel cell stack to maintain the operating temperature within the optimal thermal window prescribed by the membrane electrode assembly (MEA) and balance-of-plant (BOP) components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Heat removal is a critical stack-design topic because fuel cells are only ~40–60% electrically efficient. The remaining 40–60% of the input chemical energy (LHV of hydrogen) is released as waste heat, which *must* be removed continuously to prevent membrane dehydration, performance degradation, and catastrophic thermal runaway.

The general thermodynamic signature of waste heat in a PEMFC arises from two irreversible sources:

$$Q_{gen} = \underbrace{\dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot \Delta H}_{\text{Total chemical energy input}} - P_{elec} = P_{elec} \left(\frac{1}{\eta_{el}} - 1\right)$$

where the electrical efficiency of a fuel cell stack is typically **$\eta_{el} \approx 0.40$–$0.60$**, meaning nearly half the energy must leave the stack as sensible heat.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a marathon runner's body. When the runner exerts effort, only ~20–25% of the chemical energy from food goes into mechanical motion; the rest is dissipated as body heat. If the runner does not sweat, breathe heavily, or cool down, the body overheats and collapses.

A **fuel cell behaves identically**:
- The **electrochemical reaction** = the runner's muscles (chemical → electrical conversion).
- The **waste heat** = body heat that must be rejected to the environment.
- The **cooling system** (radiator, fan, coolant pump) = the runner's sweat and lungs.

Without active heat rejection, the **Polymer Electrolyte Membrane (PEM)** dries out (analogous to a runner's mouth becoming parched), conductivity drops, and the cell "collapses" — i.e., voltage plummets.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU-Requisite Numerical Constants:**
> - Standard stack operating temperature: **$T_{op}$ = 60–80 °C** (PEMFC), 180–200 °C (SOFC), 600–700 °C (MCFC)
> - Faraday constant: **$F$ = 96,485 C/mol**
> - Universal gas constant: **$R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - Entropic heat term: **$T \cdot \Delta S$ = 48.7 kJ/mol** for the H₂/O₂ reaction at 25 °C
> - Typical heat generation rate per cell: **$Q_{gen} \approx$ 1.5–2.5 W/cm²** at rated current density
> - Specific heat capacity of water (coolant): **$c_p$ = 4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**

---

### 1.4 Significance in KTU Examinations

Heat removal is a high-yield topic because it integrates:
- **Mass/energy balance** (Module 1 thermodynamics foundation)
- **Heat transfer fundamentals** (conduction, convection, radiation)
- **System design** (coolant flow, channel geometry)
- **Performance penalty** (temperature uniformity → efficiency)

Students are frequently tested on its formula derivations, numerical calculations, and the comparative analysis of cooling techniques.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources of Heat Generation in a Fuel Cell

A single fuel cell generates heat from **three primary thermodynamic sources**:

#### (a) Activation Losses ($\eta_{act}$)
Energy lost to overcome the activation barrier of electrode reactions. This is recovered as heat.

$$Q_{act} = I \cdot \eta_{act}$$

#### (b) Ohmic Losses ($I \cdot R_{ohm}$)
Joule heating due to ionic resistance of the membrane and electrical resistance of electrodes/bipolar plates.

$$Q_{ohm} = I^2 \cdot R_{ohm}$$

#### (c) Concentration Losses ($\eta_{conc}$)
Heat due to mass-transport limitations at high current densities.

$$Q_{conc} = I \cdot \eta_{conc}$$

The **total heat generation rate** per cell is:

$$Q_{gen} = I \cdot (\eta_{act} + \eta_{ohm} + \eta_{conc})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Hidden Insight (Board-Examiner Favorite):** At low current densities, activation losses dominate the heat output. At high current densities, **ohmic (Joule) heating dominates**, making the cell behave like a resistive heater. This crossover is critical for designing the cooling system because the heat load *increases with current* — not with time.

---

### 2.2 The Steady-State Thermal Balance Equation

For a fuel cell operating at steady state, the **First Law of Thermodynamics** dictates that all generated heat must equal the heat removed:

$$Q_{gen} = Q_{coolant} + Q_{conv} + Q_{rad} + Q_{latent}$$

In practical KTU-level analysis, the latent (water vaporization) term is often neglected or combined into the coolant term, giving:

$$Q_{coolant} = \dot{m}_{cool} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

This is the **workhorse equation** for sizing coolant flow rates.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---------|------------------|---------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | $Q_{gen} = I (\eta_{act} + I R_{ohm} + \eta_{conc})$ | Total waste heat rate | W | Sum of all loss-driven heat |
| 2 | $Q_{cool} = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ | Sensible heat carried by coolant | W | Used for liquid cooling |
| 3 | $Q_{conv} = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Convective heat loss | W | h = convective coefficient |
| 4 | $\dot{Q}_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$ | Radiative heat loss | W | σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²K⁴ |
| 5 | $\Delta T_{max} = \frac{Q_{gen} \cdot t}{k \cdot A}$ | Solid-conduction temperature rise | K | For bipolar plate conduction |
| 6 | $P_{aux} = \frac{\dot{V}_{cool} \Delta P}{\eta_{pump}}$ | Pumping power for coolant loop | W | Parasitic loss for cooling |
| 7 | $T_{stack} = T_{in} + \frac{Q_{gen}}{N \cdot \dot{m} c_p}$ | Stack exit temperature | K | N = number of cells |
| 8 | $Re = \frac{\rho u D_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number for coolant flow | dimensionless | $Re > 2300$ → turbulent preferred |
| 9 | $Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4}$ | Dittus-Boelter Nusselt number | dimensionless | For turbulent flow in channels |
| 10 | $h = \frac{Nu \cdot k}{D_h}$ | Convective coefficient | W/m²K | Channel-based h calculation |
| 11 | $\Delta H = \Delta G + T \Delta S$ | Energy partition (H–G–TS) | J/mol | Entropic heat floor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall Reminder:** Many students incorrectly equate $\Delta H$ (enthalpy of reaction) directly with electrical work. The correct partition is: $\Delta G$ = electrical work, and $T \Delta S$ = reversible (entropic) heat. The rest is irreversible waste heat from overpotentials.

---

### 2.4 Why and How — Engineering Rationale

**Why is heat removal so critical?**
1. **Membrane conductivity** peaks at ~75 °C in Nafion; above ~85 °C it dehydrates and resistance skyrockets.
2. **Temperature non-uniformity** creates local hotspots, accelerating degradation.
3. **Thermal stress** cracks seals, gaskets, and the MEA.
4. **Recoverable energy**: The waste heat can be co-generated (CHP systems) for ~85% total efficiency.

**How is it accomplished? (Engineering pathways)**
- **Conduction** → through bipolar plates to cooling plates.
- **Convection** → forced flow of air or liquid coolant through channels.
- **Evaporation** → latent heat from product water vaporization (passive technique).
- **Radiation** → from stack exterior (negligible below 100 °C, dominant in SOFCs).

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

| Application Domain | Cooling Strategy Used |
|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Automotive PEMFC** (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO) | Liquid coolant + radiator |
| **Stationary SOFC** (Bloom Energy, Siemens) | Cathode air + external heat recovery boiler |
| **Portable PEMFC** | Cathode exhaust gas recirculation (EGR) |
| **Space applications** (Apollo, Shuttle) | Passive water condenser loop |
| **Backup power (telecom)** | Forced-air finned heat sinks |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Balance on a Single Fuel Cell

We begin from the First Law for a control volume surrounding a single cell operating at steady state:

**Step 1:** Identify all energy entering the cell.

$$\dot{E}_{in} = \dot{n}_{H_2,in} \cdot \bar{H}_{H_2} + \dot{n}_{O_2,in} \cdot \bar{H}_{O_2}$$

**Step 2:** Identify all energy leaving the cell.

$$\dot{E}_{out} = P_{elec} + \dot{n}_{H_2O,out} \cdot \bar{H}_{H_2O} + \dot{n}_{O_2,out} \cdot \bar{H}_{O_2} + \dot{Q}_{gen}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the steady-state energy balance $\dot{E}_{in} = \dot{E}_{out}$.

$$\dot{n}_{H_2,in} \bar{H}_{H_2} = P_{elec} + \dot{n}_{H_2O,out} \bar{H}_{H_2O} + \dot{Q}_{gen}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the reaction stoichiometry (per mole of H₂ reacted: $\dot{n}_{H_2,reacted}$).

Since the cell operates at fixed stoichiometry, we use the reacted moles as the basis:

$$\dot{n}_{H_2,rxn} \cdot \Delta H_{H_2O(l)} = P_{elec} + \dot{Q}_{gen}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize that $P_{elec} = \dot{n}_{H_2,rxn} \cdot \Delta G$ (Gibbs free energy becomes electrical work).

$$\dot{n}_{H_2,rxn} \cdot \Delta H = \dot{n}_{H_2,rxn} \cdot \Delta G + \dot{Q}_{gen}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for the heat generation rate.

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{gen} = \dot{n}_{H_2,rxn} \cdot (\Delta H - \Delta G) = \dot{n}_{H_2,rxn} \cdot T \Delta S + \dot{Q}_{irrev}}$$

**Step 7:** Convert to current-based form using Faraday's law: $\dot{n}_{H_2} = \frac{I}{2F}$ (two electrons per H₂ molecule).

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{gen} = \frac{I}{2F}(T \Delta S + I R_{ohm} + \eta_{act} + \eta_{conc})}$$

**Interpretation:** The first term ($T \Delta S$ = **48.7 kJ/mol at 25 °C**) is the **unavoidable reversible heat**; the rest is irreversible waste heat from overpotentials.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coolant Flow Rate from a Given Heat Duty

A PEMFC stack of $N$ cells delivers rated power $P_{stack}$ at electrical efficiency $\eta_{el}$. We wish to find the coolant mass flow rate.

**Step 1:** Total heat to be removed.

$$\dot{Q}_{total} = P_{stack} \left(\frac{1}{\eta_{el}} - 1\right)$$

**Step 2:** Coolant heat capacity equation.

$$\dot{Q}_{total} = \dot{m}_{cool} \cdot c_{p,cool} \cdot \Delta T_{cool}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\dot{m}_{cool}$.

$$\dot{m}_{cool} = \frac{P_{stack} \left(\frac{1}{\eta_{el}} - 1\right)}{c_{p,cool} \cdot \Delta T_{cool}}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to volumetric flow rate using coolant density $\rho_{cool}$.

$$\dot{V}_{cool} = \frac{P_{stack} \left(\frac{1}{\eta_{el}} - 1\right)}{\rho_{cool} \cdot c_{p,cool} \cdot \Delta T_{cool}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Example:** A 50 kW stack at $\eta_{el}$ = 0.50 using water with $\Delta T$ = 10 K, $\rho$ = 1000 kg/m³, $c_p$ = 4186 J/(kg·K).
>
> $$\dot{V}_{cool} = \frac{50{,}000 \times (1/0.50 - 1)}{1000 \times 4186 \times 10} = \frac{50{,}000}{4.186 \times 10^7} \approx 1.19 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
>
> $$\dot{V}_{cool} \approx 4.30 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$
>
> This is the order of magnitude for an automotive 50 kW fuel cell cooling loop.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Temperature Distribution Along a Cooling Channel

Consider a single cooling channel of length $L$, hydraulic diameter $D_h$, through which coolant flows. The local convective heat transfer governs the wall temperature.

**Step 1:** Energy balance on a differential channel element $dx$.

$$d\dot{Q} = h \cdot P_{wetted} \cdot (T_w - T_f(x)) \cdot dx$$

where $P_{wetted} = \pi D_h$ for a circular tube.

**Step 2:** Coolant enthalpy rise in the same element.

$$d\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{cool} \cdot c_p \cdot dT_f(x)$$

**Step 3:** Equate and rearrange into a separable ODE.

$$\dot{m}_{cool} c_p \, dT_f = h \pi D_h (T_w - T_f) \, dx$$

**Step 4:** Apply the NTU (Number of Transfer Units) parameter $\beta = \frac{h \pi D_h L}{\dot{m}_{cool} c_p}$.

Integrating with $T_f(0) = T_{in}$ and $T_f(L) = T_{out}$:

$$\boxed{T_{out} = T_w - (T_w - T_{in}) \exp(-\beta)}$$

**Step 5:** Useful corollary — mean temperature difference.

$$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{(T_w - T_{in}) - (T_w - T_{out})}{\ln \frac{T_w - T_{in}}{T_w - T_{out}}}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Maximum Allowable Current Density Without Active Cooling

For passive (no external coolant) operation, the cell relies only on natural convection + radiation:

**Step 1:** Equate generated heat to natural-convection heat loss.

$$\dot{Q}_{gen} = I \cdot V_{loss} = h_{nat} A_{cell} (T_s - T_\infty)$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange for current.

$$I_{max,passive} = \frac{h_{nat} A_{cell} (T_s - T_\infty)}{V_{loss}}$$

For a 25 cm² cell with $h_{nat} \approx 10 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, $T_s - T_\infty = 50$ K, and $V_{loss} = 0.4$ V:

$$I_{max} = \frac{10 \times 25 \times 10^{-4} \times 50}{0.4} \approx 0.31 \text{ A}$$

This corresponds to **$\sim$12 mA/cm²** — far below practical use, justifying active cooling for stacks above a few watts.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance

| Parameter | Air Cooling | Liquid Cooling | Cathode Recirculation | Phase-Change |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|----------------------|--------------|
| Heat removal capacity | Low–Medium | High | Medium | Very High |
| Complexity | Low | High | Medium | High |
| Parasitic power | Medium (fan) | High (pump) | Low–Medium | Low |
| Uniformity | Poor | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| Water management burden | High (dries membrane) | Low | Medium | Low |
| Typical application | <1 kW portable | 1–250 kW automotive/stationary | 5–50 kW mobile | High-power military |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Why is heat removal essential in PEM fuel cells? List any two consequences of inadequate thermal management.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks: 1+1+1):**
Heat removal is essential because a PEM fuel cell converts only ~40–60% of hydrogen's chemical energy into electricity; the remaining 40–60% is released as waste heat. **(1 Mark)**

Two consequences of inadequate heat removal:
1. **Membrane dehydration** — At temperatures above ~85 °C, the Nafion membrane loses its bound water, ionic conductivity drops sharply, and cell performance degrades irreversibly. **(1 Mark)**
2. **Thermal runaway / mechanical failure** — Uneven temperature distribution causes local hotspots, leading to seal failure, pin-hole formation in the membrane, and accelerated catalyst degradation. **(1 Mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between "reversible heat" and "irreversible heat" in a fuel cell.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks: 1.5 + 1.5):**

| Aspect | Reversible Heat | Irreversible Heat |
|--------|----------------|-------------------|
| Origin | Entropy change of reaction ($T \Delta S$) | Activation, ohmic, and concentration overpotentials |
| Avoidable? | No, fundamental to the reaction | Yes, reducible via better catalysts/flow design |
| Magnitude | ~48.7 kJ/mol H₂ at 25 °C (constant) | Increases with current density |

Reversible heat represents the unavoidable thermodynamic minimum that must be rejected even at the reversible cell voltage. Irreversible heat scales with current and represents the "engineering inefficiency" of the cell. **(1.5 + 1.5 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a) Derive the energy balance equation for a single PEM fuel cell and show that the total waste heat is the sum of reversible and irreversible contributions.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Statement of First Law** (1 Mark)

For a control volume enclosing a single cell at steady state:

$$\dot{E}_{in} = \dot{E}_{out}$$

**Step 2: Energy in and out identification** (1 Mark)

$$\dot{n}_{H_2} \bar{H}_{H_2} + \dot{n}_{O_2} \bar{H}_{O_2} = P_{elec} + \dot{n}_{H_2O} \bar{H}_{H_2O} + \dot{Q}_{gen}$$

**Step 3: Substitute stoichiometry and simplify** (1 Mark)

$$\dot{n}_{H_2,rxn} \cdot \Delta H_{rxn} = P_{elec} + \dot{Q}_{gen}$$

**Step 4: Relate electrical work to Gibbs free energy** (1 Mark)

$$P_{elec} = \dot{n}_{H_2,rxn} \cdot \Delta G_{rxn}$$

**Step 5: Express heat generation** (1 Mark)

$$\dot{Q}_{gen} = \dot{n}_{H_2,rxn} \cdot (\Delta H - \Delta G)$$

**Step 6: Use thermodynamic identity $\Delta H = \Delta G + T \Delta S$** (1 Mark)

$$\dot{Q}_{gen} = \dot{n}_{H_2,rxn} \cdot T \Delta S \quad \text{(reversible)}$$

**Step 7: Add irreversible contributions from overpotentials** (1 Mark)

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{gen} = \frac{I}{2F} \left[ T \Delta S + I R_{ohm} + \eta_{act} + \eta_{conc} \right]}$$

---

**(b) A 40-cell PEM fuel cell stack operates at 1.2 A/cm² current density, with cell active area 200 cm², average cell voltage 0.65 V, and LHV efficiency 48%. Calculate (i) total stack electrical power output, (ii) total waste heat generated, and (iii) required water coolant flow rate if inlet temperature is 50 °C and outlet must not exceed 65 °C.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N = 40$ cells
- $j = 1.2 \text{ A/cm}^2$, $A = 200 \text{ cm}^2$
- $V_{cell} = 0.65$ V
- $\eta_{LHV} = 0.48$
- $T_{in} = 50$ °C, $T_{out} = 65$ °C, $\Delta T = 15$ K

**Step 1: Current per cell** (1 Mark)

$$I = j \cdot A = 1.2 \times 200 = 240 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Total stack electrical power** (1 Mark)

$$P_{stack} = N \cdot I \cdot V_{cell} = 40 \times 240 \times 0.65 = 6240 \text{ W} \approx 6.24 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Total waste heat from efficiency** (1 Mark)

$$\dot{Q}_{total} = P_{stack} \left(\frac{1}{\eta_{LHV}} - 1\right) = 6240 \times \left(\frac{1}{0.48} - 1\right) = 6240 \times 1.0833 = 6760 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Coolant mass flow rate equation** (1 Mark)

$$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{total}}{c_p \Delta T}$$

**Step 5: Substitute water properties $c_p = 4186$ J/(kg·K), $\Delta T = 15$ K** (1 Mark)

$$\dot{m} = \frac{6760}{4186 \times 15} = \frac{6760}{62790} = 0.1077 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 6: Convert to L/min** (1 Mark)

$$\dot{V} = \frac{0.1077}{1000} \times 60 \times 1000 = 6.46 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 7: Final Answer with units** (1 Mark)

$$\boxed{\dot{V}_{coolant} \approx 6.46 \text{ L/min}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

**(a) With neat sketches, explain the working of (i) liquid cooling and (ii) cathode exhaust gas recirculation cooling methods for PEM fuel cell stacks. Compare their relative merits.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Liquid Cooling (4 Marks of 7):**

A liquid coolant (typically deionized water + glycol mixture) is circulated through cooling plates inserted between every 3–5 cells of the stack. The coolant absorbs heat by forced convection from the bipolar plates and carries it to an external radiator.

**Key Components:**
- Cooling plates (machined graphite or metal)
- Coolant pump
- Radiator / heat exchanger
- Expansion tank and de-aerator

**Working Principle:** Hot coolant leaving the stack enters the radiator where it rejects heat to ambient air via a fan. The cooled liquid returns to the stack inlet.

**Heat Transfer Equation:**

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{cool} c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

**(ii) Cathode Exhaust Recirculation (3 Marks of 7):**

A portion of the hot, humid cathode exhaust stream is recirculated and mixed with fresh inlet air. The latent heat of water vapor in the exhaust provides additional cooling capacity while keeping the membrane humidified.

**Working Principle:**
1. Cathode exhaust → blower → split into vent and recirculation paths
2. Recirculated stream → mixer → rejoins fresh air
3. The mixing lowers the inlet temperature while adding moisture

**Comparative Merits:**

| Feature | Liquid Cooling | Cathode EGR |
|---------|----------------|-------------|
| Heat capacity | Very high (water: 4186 J/kg·K) | Medium (air: 1005 J/kg·K) |
| Stack uniformity | Excellent | Good |
| Parasitic power | High (pump) | Lower (single blower) |
| Humidification | Separate humidifier needed | Built-in |
| Complexity | High (seals, fittings) | Medium |
| Cost | High | Lower |

---

**(b) Define the Dittus-Boelter correlation for turbulent flow in cooling channels. A cooling channel has hydraulic diameter 2 mm, water flow velocity 1.5 m/s, water temperature 60 °C. Calculate the convective heat transfer coefficient. Given: water properties at 60 °C: $k = 0.654$ W/(m·K), $\mu = 4.67 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s, $\rho = 983$ kg/m³, $Pr = 3.0$.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Dittus-Boelter definition** (1 Mark)

$$Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4} \quad \text{(for turbulent flow, Re > 10,000, 0.7 < Pr < 160)}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Reynolds number** (1 Mark)

$$Re = \frac{\rho u D_h}{\mu} = \frac{983 \times 1.5 \times 0.002}{4.67 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$Re = \frac{2.949}{4.67 \times 10^{-4}} = 6314$$

**Step 3: Check for turbulence** (1 Mark)

Since $Re = 6314 < 10000$, flow is technically in the transitional regime. The Dittus-Boelter correlation may overestimate h, but KTU convention accepts its use for $Re > 2300$ with caution.

**Step 4: Calculate Nusselt number** (1 Mark)

$$Nu = 0.023 \times (6314)^{0.8} \times (3.0)^{0.4}$$

$$(6314)^{0.8} = e^{0.8 \ln 6314} = e^{0.8 \times 8.751} = e^{7.001} \approx 1101.6$$

$$(3.0)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \times 1.0986} = e^{0.4394} \approx 1.552$$

$$Nu = 0.023 \times 1101.6 \times 1.552 = 39.32$$

**Step 5: Calculate convective coefficient** (1 Mark)

$$h = \frac{Nu \cdot k}{D_h} = \frac{39.32 \times 0.654}{0.002} = \frac{25.71}{0.002} = 12{,}857 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$$

**Step 6: Convert to practical units and interpret** (1 Mark)

$$\boxed{h \approx 12{,}857 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)} \approx 12.9 \text{ kW/(m}^2\text{·K)}}$$

**Step 7: Comment on physical meaning** (1 Mark)

This high value of $h$ confirms that water flow in microchannels is highly effective at heat removal, justifying the widespread use of liquid cooling in mid-to-high power PEMFC stacks (1 kW to 250 kW).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Heat Removal Problems:**
> 1. **Units mismatch** — Students frequently mix up per-cell and stack quantities. Always clarify whether $I$, $Q$, or $\dot{m}$ refer to one cell or the entire stack of $N$ cells.
> 2. **Forgetting $N$ in stack formulas** — The stack power is $N \cdot I \cdot V_{cell}$, not $I \cdot V_{cell}$. This single mistake loses ~2 marks.
> 3. **Sign convention errors** — Heat *generated* is positive; heat *removed* is positive; the balance is $Q_{gen} = Q_{removed}$, not a subtraction.
> 4. **Using $\Delta G$ instead of $\Delta H$** — Heat generation uses $\Delta H - \Delta G$, not $\Delta G$ alone. Confusing these loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Neglecting coolant temperature rise** — Using ambient temperature instead of coolant inlet/outlet temperatures in $\Delta T$ calculations is a frequent error.
> 6. **Misapplying correlations** — Dittus-Boelter is only valid for fully turbulent flow ($Re > 10{,}000$). For lower $Re$, KTU expects an explicit mention of the validity range.
> 7. **Missing irreversibility** — A common conceptual error is writing $\dot{Q} = T \Delta S$ only, forgetting the overpotential terms. Board examiners specifically test for the **complete** equation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Heat Removal (Module 4):**
>
> - ✅ **Heat generation sources**: Activation + Ohmic + Concentration + Reversible (TΔS) — *all four must be summed*.
> - ✅ **Energy balance master equation**: $\dot{Q}_{gen} = \frac{I}{2F}(\Delta H - \Delta G)$ with $V_{loss}$ being the equivalent of $(\Delta H - \Delta G)/(2F)$.
> - ✅ **PEMFC operating temperature**: 60–80 °C; **membrane dehydration above 85 °C** is the *primary failure mode* from poor heat removal.
> - ✅ **SOFC operating temperature**: 600–1000 °C; requires ceramic interconnects and external heat recovery.
> - ✅ **Coolant sizing equation**: $\dot{m} = \dot{Q}/(c_p \Delta T)$ — memorize and dimension-check.
> - ✅ **Dittus-Boelter correlation**: $Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4}$ — only for fully turbulent single-phase flow.
> - ✅ **Three cooling methods**: Air (simple, low power), Liquid (efficient, complex), Cathode EGR (balanced).
> - ✅ **Parasitic loss trade-off**: Higher cooling capacity = higher pump/fan power = lower net system efficiency.
> - ✅ **Temperature uniformity target**: ±2 °C across stack face for premium automotive stacks (Toyota Mirai specification).
> - ✅ **Farquhar Number / NTU**: $\beta = \frac{h P L}{\dot{m} c_p}$ — used in channel exit temperature calculations.
> - ✅ **Unavoidable reversible heat at 25 °C**: $T \Delta S = 48.7$ kJ/mol H₂ (or 0.252 V equivalent heat per mole).
> - ✅ **Heat flux range**: 1.5–2.5 W/cm² is typical at rated operation — comparable to a small heat exchanger.
> - ✅ **Recuperation**: In SOFCs and MCFCs, exhaust heat is recovered for inlet preheating, raising total system efficiency to ~85%.
> - ✅ **Board-favorite formula**: $\dot{Q}_{cool} = \dot{m} c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})$ — must appear with full units in numerical answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stack Clamping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT721 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Stack Clamping** # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stack Clamping** is the controlled application of a uniform mechanical compressive load across a fuel cell stack assembly, achieved through end-plates, tie-rods (tension members), and fastening hardware, in order to simultaneously (i) maintain low interfacial contact resistance between the bipolar plate, gas diffusion layer (GDL), and membrane electrode assembly (MEA), (ii) compress elastomeric gaskets to achieve internal gas-tight sealing, and (iii) provide overall mechanical integrity of the multi-cell stack against internal reactant pressures, thermal gradients, and external vibration. In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **PEMFC and SOFC stack design**, the clamping system is treated as a **load-bearing sub-assembly** that converts an external mechanical input (bolt torque or hydraulic pressure) into a uniformly distributed interfacial contact pressure across every repeat unit of the stack. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stack Sandwich" Imagine a tall sandwich made of 50 identical layers: bread (end plate), then repeating layers of meat (MEA), cheese (gasket), and a tray (bipolar plate). If you simply stack them on a table, the layers slip, the meat slides out, and air pockets form between the cheese and bread. Now press the sandwich between two wooden boards using four long bolts at the corners and tighten the nuts. The boards press down uniformly, the cheese oozes just enough to seal the gaps, and the meat stays in perfect electrical "contact" with every layer. The boards are the **end plates**, the long bolts are **tie-rods**, the nuts provide the **preload force**, and the cheese is the **elastomeric gasket**. The amount you tighten the nuts decides whether the sandwich is too loose (high resistance, leaky) or too tight (meat gets crushed, MEA gets punctured). > [!NOTE] > **Target Clamping Pressure for PEMFC**: typically **0.5 MPa to 2.0 MPa** on the active area. Going below 0.5 MPa causes excessive contact resistance; going above 2.0 MPa crushes the GDL carbon fibers and blocks gas diffusion channels. ## 1.3 Primary Objectives of Stack Clamping | # | Objective | Engineering Function | |---|-----------|----------------------| | 1 | **Electrical continuity** | Minimize contact resistance at BPP–GDL–MEA interfaces | | 2 | **Hermetic sealing** | Compress gaskets to prevent H₂ / O₂ crossover and coolant leakage | | 3 | **Mechanical integrity** | Hold 20–500 repeat units together as a single rigid body | | 4 | **Thermal management** | Maintain uniform thermal contact with cooling plates | | 5 | **Dimensional stability** | Resist swelling of MEA, creep of GDL, and thermal expansion | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Components of a Clamping Sub-Assembly A typical fuel cell stack clamping system consists of the following elements, arranged in series along the loading axis: 1. **Current Collector / Insulating Plate** — Copper or gold-plated plate that collects current; electrically isolated from the metallic end-plate using a polymer insulator to prevent short-circuiting the entire stack through the tie-rods. 2. **End Plate (Compression Plate)** — A thick, rigid plate (aluminium, stainless steel, or composite) that distributes the bolt preload over the entire active area of the cell. 3. **Tie-Rods (Tension Rods)** — High-tensile steel rods (typically 4, 6, or 8 in number) that pass through the corners of the end plates, transmitting the clamping force in tension. 4. **Nuts and Washers** — Provide the preload via controlled torque application; spring washers or belleville washers may be used to compensate for thermal expansion and gasket creep. 5. **Insulation Sleeves** — Non-conductive polymer sleeves around tie-rods where they pass through metallic components, to prevent internal short circuits. ## 2.2 Why Clamping Pressure Matters — The Underlying Physics ### (a) Contact Resistance at BPP–GDL Interface The electrical contact resistance $R_c$ between the bipolar plate (BPP) and the gas diffusion layer (GDL) is governed by the **Holm contact theory** — actual contact occurs only at a small number of microscopic asperities (real area of contact << apparent area). As clamping pressure $P_c$ increases: $$R_c \;\propto\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{P_c}}$$ This is why a *minimum* pressure of **0.5 MPa** is required. Beyond ~1.0 MPa, the curve flattens — diminishing returns set in. ### (b) Gasket Sealing Mechanics Elastomeric gaskets seal by **viscoelastic deformation** under load. The minimum sealing pressure $P_{seal}$ depends on the internal reactant pressure $P_{reactant}$ and the gasket geometry. For a flat gasket: $$P_{seal} \;\geq\; m \cdot P_{reactant}$$ where $m$ is the **gasket factor** (typically **1.5 to 3.0** for compressed rubber gaskets in fuel cell service). ### (c) GDL Compression Damage Above ~**2.0 MPa**, the porous carbon-fiber GDL begins to collapse, reducing porosity from ~70% to below ~50%, which blocks gas transport to the catalyst layer and causes severe mass-transport losses. ## 2.3 Types of Clamping Mechanisms > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners frequently ask a **comparison question** on the four main clamping approaches. | Type | Mechanism | Advantages | Disadvantages | |------|-----------|------------|---------------| | **Tie-rod + Bolt** | Steel rods through end plates, nuts apply torque | Simple, low cost, easily serviced | Non-uniform pressure, bolt loosening under vibration | | **Band / Strap Clamping** | Metal bands wrap around stack perimeter | Uniform perimeter pressure, compact | Cannot access active area; limited load | | **Hydraulic / Pneumatic** | Inflatable bladder or piston in end plate | Highly uniform, controllable pressure, auto-compensation | Complex, leak-prone, expensive | | **Compression Mold Frame** | Two-part moulded frame with snap-fit | Excellent uniformity, low profile | Non-adjustable, single-use | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |---|---------|-------------|----------------| | 1 | $F_{clamp} = P_{contact} \times A_{active}$ | Total clamping force from target contact pressure | N | | 2 | $F_{bolt} = F_{clamp} / n$ | Preload per tie-rod (n = number of rods) | N | | 3 | $T_{bolt} = k \cdot F_{bolt} \cdot d$ | Bolt tightening torque (k = nut factor, d = nominal bolt diameter) | N·m | | 4 | $\sigma_{end\,plate} = \dfrac{3 \cdot F_{clamp} \cdot L}{2 \cdot b \cdot t^{2}}$ | Maximum bending stress in a rectangular end plate (L = unsupported span, b = width, t = thickness) | Pa | | 5 | $\Delta L_{thermal} = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | Free thermal expansion of a tie-rod | m | | 6 | $F_{thermal} = E \cdot A \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Constrained thermal force generated if expansion is fully restrained | N | | 7 | $P_{seal} \geq m \cdot P_{internal}$ | Minimum gasket sealing pressure | Pa | | 8 | $R_{c} \propto 1 / \sqrt{P_{c}}$ | Contact resistance vs. clamping pressure (Holm relation) | $\Omega$ | | 9 | $\delta_{gasket} = \varepsilon_{gasket} \cdot t_{gasket}$ | Gasket deflection under load (ε = strain, typically 15–25%) | m | | 10 | $\tau_{bolt} = \dfrac{16 \cdot T_{bolt}}{\pi \cdot d^{3}}$ | Torsional shear stress in tie-rod | Pa | > [!IMPORTANT] > In all KTU numerical answers, use the **SI unit convention**: Force in **N**, pressure in **Pa** (or MPa with $10^{6}$ conversion), torque in **N·m**, and stress in **Pa** (or MPa). The nut factor $k$ for steel-on-steel with mild lubrication is **k ≈ 0.20**. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In commercial fuel cell stacks (e.g., Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO, Ballard FCgen), the clamping system is one of the **top-three contributors** to stack weight and volume. Engineers use **finite element analysis (FEA)** to model the pressure distribution across the active area, often discovering that corner cells receive up to **30% more pressure** than centre cells. Modern designs use **hydraulic bladder end plates** or **discrete-spring-loaded tie-rods** to actively equalise pressure, improving both performance and durability by 2–3×. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Clamping Force from Target Contact Pressure **Problem:** A PEMFC stack has 30 cells, each with an active area of 250 cm². The designer wants a uniform contact pressure of 1.2 MPa on the active area. Calculate the total clamping force required. **Step 1 — Identify the knowns** $$A_{active} = 250 \text{ cm}^{2} = 250 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2} = 0.0250 \text{ m}^{2}$$ $$P_{contact} = 1.2 \text{ MPa} = 1.2 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the fundamental clamping equation** The total clamping force $F_{clamp}$ is the contact pressure multiplied by the area over which it acts. For a uniform distribution: $$F_{clamp} = P_{contact} \times A_{active}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute numerical values** $$F_{clamp} = (1.2 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}) \times (0.0250 \text{ m}^{2})$$ $$F_{clamp} = 30{,}000 \text{ N} = 30 \text{ kN}$$ > [!NOTE] > The number of cells (30) does **not** appear in this calculation because the target pressure is the *interfacial* pressure on **one** cell. The clamping system must deliver this pressure on every interface simultaneously, and the tie-rods carry the cumulative load of all 30 interfaces acting in series. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bolt Preload and Tightening Torque **Problem:** For the same 30-cell stack above, the design uses **4 tie-rods** (one at each corner). The bolts have a nominal diameter of **12 mm**. Calculate the required tightening torque per bolt. Use nut factor $k = 0.20$. **Step 1 — Compute the load per tie-rod** Assuming the load is shared equally among the 4 tie-rods: $$F_{bolt} = \frac{F_{clamp}}{n} = \frac{30{,}000 \text{ N}}{4} = 7{,}500 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the bolt-torque equation** The standard relationship between applied torque $T_{bolt}$ and the resulting bolt preload $F_{bolt}$ is: $$T_{bolt} = k \cdot F_{bolt} \cdot d$$ **Step 3 — Substitute values** $$T_{bolt} = 0.20 \times 7{,}500 \text{ N} \times 0.012 \text{ m}$$ $$T_{bolt} = 18.0 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 4 — Check the torsional shear stress in the bolt** $$\tau_{bolt} = \frac{16 \cdot T_{bolt}}{\pi \cdot d^{3}} = \frac{16 \times 18.0}{\pi \times (0.012)^{3}}$$ $$\tau_{bolt} = \frac{288.0}{5.428 \times 10^{-6}} = 5.30 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 53.0 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 5 — Verify safety** For a grade 8.8 steel bolt, yield strength $\sigma_{y} \approx 640 \text{ MPa}$. The allowable shear stress (using von Mises approximation, $\tau_{allow} \approx 0.577 \sigma_{y}$): $$\tau_{allow} \approx 0.577 \times 640 = 369 \text{ MPa}$$ Since $53.0 \text{ MPa} \ll 369 \text{ MPa}$, the bolt design is **safe with a factor of safety ≈ 7**, indicating the bolt is *over-designed* for this load — a common practice to ensure long-term reliability against vibration and creep. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: End-Plate Bending Stress **Problem:** The end plate is a rectangular aluminium plate of width $b = 0.20$ m, unsupported span $L = 0.15$ m, and thickness $t = 0.025$ m. It must support a total clamping force of 30 kN distributed over its area. Calculate the maximum bending stress. **Step 1 — Identify loading condition** The end plate behaves as a beam supported at the four tie-rod locations. The maximum bending moment at the centre of the plate is approximated by treating it as a simply-supported beam carrying the distributed load: $$M_{max} = \frac{F_{clamp} \cdot L}{8}$$ Wait — for accuracy, we treat the 4 corner rods as point supports and the distributed load as uniform: $$M_{max} = \frac{F_{clamp} \cdot L}{8} = \frac{30{,}000 \times 0.15}{8} = 562.5 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 2 — Section modulus of the rectangular plate** $$Z = \frac{b \cdot t^{2}}{6} = \frac{0.20 \times (0.025)^{2}}{6} = \frac{0.20 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{6}$$ $$Z = 2.083 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}$$ **Step 3 — Maximum bending stress** $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{562.5}{2.083 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$\sigma_{max} = 2.70 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 27.0 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 4 — Verify against yield** For aluminium 6061-T6, $\sigma_{y} \approx 240 \text{ MPa}$. Factor of safety: $$FoS = \frac{240}{27.0} \approx 8.9$$ The end plate is **safely designed**. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Thermal Expansion Compensation **Problem:** A steel tie-rod of length $L = 0.30$ m is heated from 25 °C (assembly) to 70 °C (operating). Young's modulus $E = 200$ GPa, thermal expansion coefficient $\alpha_{steel} = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$. Calculate the *constrained* thermal force generated. **Step 1 — Free thermal expansion** $$\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T = (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.30 \times (70 - 25)$$ $$\Delta L = 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.30 \times 45 = 1.62 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.162 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Constrained thermal stress** If both ends are fixed (no expansion allowed), the strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ produces a stress: $$\sigma_{thermal} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$ $$\sigma_{thermal} = (200 \times 10^{9}) \times (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 45$$ $$\sigma_{thermal} = 1.08 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 108 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 3 — Constrained thermal force (rod cross-section A = 113 mm² = 1.13 × 10⁻⁴ m²)** $$F_{thermal} = \sigma_{thermal} \times A = 108 \times 10^{6} \times 1.13 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$F_{thermal} = 12{,}204 \text{ N} \approx 12.2 \text{ kN}$$ > [!WARNING] > This thermal force is **~40% of the original 30 kN clamping load**. If the designer does not account for differential thermal expansion (steel rod vs. graphite/composite end plate), the bolt preload either *loses tension* (loosens) or *over-compresses* the stack, both of which cause stack failure. This is why KTU examiners consider this a **14-mark favourite** in Module 4. **Step 4 — Differential expansion check** Aluminium end plate: $\alpha_{Al} = 23 \times 10^{-6} /°C$; Steel rod: $\alpha_{steel} = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$. Differential expansion: $\Delta L_{Al} - \Delta L_{steel}$ over the same 0.30 m length and 45 °C rise: $$\delta = (\alpha_{Al} - \alpha_{steel}) \cdot L \cdot \Delta T = (11 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.30 \times 45$$ $$\delta = 1.485 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.149 \text{ mm}$$ This 0.15 mm differential must be absorbed by the **gasket compliance** or by using **spring washers** in series with each nut. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Clamped Fuel Cell Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ExternalLoad["External Mechanical Input"] Torque["Applied Bolt Torque T_bolt"] end subgraph TieRodAssembly["Tie-Rod Assembly (4 corners)"] RodA["Tie-Rod A
F_bolt = F_clamp / n"] RodB["Tie-Rod B"] RodC["Tie-Rod C"] RodD["Tie-Rod D"] end subgraph EndPlates["End Plate Sub-Assembly"] EndPlate1["Top End Plate
Al 6061-T6, t=25 mm"] Insulator1["Electrical Insulator
Polymer plate"] Coll1["Current Collector
Cu or Au-plated"] end subgraph StackBody["Stack Body - N Repeat Units"] Cell1["Cell 1
BPP / GDL / MEA / GDL / BPP"] Cell2["Cell 2"] Cell3["Cell N"] SealG["Gasket Layer
Silicone / FKM"] end subgraph LowerEnd["Lower End Plate Sub-Assembly"] Coll2["Current Collector"] Insulator2["Insulator"] EndPlate2["Bottom End Plate"] end subgraph ForceOutput["Output: Contact Pressure"] PcActive["P_contact = 0.5 to 2.0 MPa
on Active Area"] Rcontact["R_contact decreases
with sqrt of P_c"] end Torque --> RodA Torque --> RodB Torque --> RodC Torque --> RodD RodA --> EndPlate1 RodB --> EndPlate1 RodC --> EndPlate1 RodD --> EndPlate1 EndPlate1 --> Insulator1 Insulator1 --> Coll1 Coll1 --> Cell1 Cell1 --> SealG SealG --> Cell2 Cell2 --> SealG SealG --> Cell3 Cell3 --> Coll2 Coll2 --> Insulator2 Insulator2 --> EndPlate2 EndPlate2 --> RodA EndPlate2 --> RodB EndPlate2 --> RodC EndPlate2 --> RodD EndPlate1 --> PcActive PcActive --> Rcontact ``` ## 4.2 Force Flow and Pressure Distribution ```mermaid flowchart LR Input["Bolt Preload
F_bolt = 7.5 kN per rod"] --> Distribute["End Plate
Distributes Load"] Distribute --> Corner["Corner Cells
Receive 30% Extra
P_local > P_target"] Distribute --> Centre["Centre Cells
Receive Lower Load
P_local less than P_target"] Corner --> GDLcrush["Risk: GDL Crushing
if P_local above 2.0 MPa"] Centre --> ContactLoss["Risk: High Contact
Resistance
if P_local below 0.5 MPa"] GDLcrush --> Mitigation["Mitigation:
Use Hydraulic Bladder
or Spring-Loaded Rods"] ContactLoss --> Mitigation Mitigation --> Uniform["Uniform Pressure
Field across Active Area"] ``` ## 4.3 Clamping Method Comparison Block ```mermaid flowchart TB Root["Clamping Method Selection"] Root --> M1["Tie-Rod and Bolt
Simple, low cost, field-serviceable
Drawback: non-uniform pressure"] Root --> M2["Band Clamp
Uniform perimeter load
Drawback: limited to small stacks"] Root --> M3["Hydraulic Bladder
Actively uniform pressure
Drawback: leak risk, complex"] Root --> M4["Compression Frame
Moulded CFRP or polymer frame
Drawback: non-adjustable, brittle"] M1 --> App1["Used in: Research stacks,
small-scale prototypes"] M2 --> App2["Used in: Portable FC,
drones, small UAVs"] M3 --> App3["Used in: Automotive stacks
Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO"] M4 --> App4["Used in: Consumer electronics
and small stationary units"] ``` ## 4.4 Key Interface Pressure Windows ```mermaid flowchart LR P0["0.0 MPa
No Contact"] --> P1["0.3 MPa
Initial Asperity Contact"] P1 --> P2["0.5 MPa
Minimum Operating
Contact Pressure"] P2 --> P3["1.0 to 1.5 MPa
Optimum Window
Low R_c, Intact GDL"] P3 --> P4["2.0 MPa
GDL Crushing Begins
Porosity drops below 50%"] P4 --> P5["greater than 3.0 MPa
MEA Puncture Risk
Sealant Extrusion"] P2 -.Safe Zone.-> P3 P3 -.Safe Zone.-> P4 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define stack clamping in a fuel cell and list any three of its primary objectives.** **Model Answer:** Stack clamping is the application of a controlled, uniform mechanical compressive force across a fuel cell stack using end plates, tie-rods, and fasteners, to maintain electrical contact, gas sealing, and mechanical integrity of the multi-cell assembly. **Three primary objectives:** 1. To ensure low electrical contact resistance at the bipolar plate–GDL–MEA interfaces. 2. To compress the elastomeric gaskets sufficiently to prevent hydrogen and oxygen crossover and coolant leakage. 3. To hold all repeat units together as a single rigid structure capable of withstanding internal pressures, thermal cycling, and external vibration. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Why is the typical operating clamping pressure window for a PEMFC stack limited to 0.5–2.0 MPa?** **Model Answer:** The lower bound (**0.5 MPa**) is the minimum pressure at which the microscopic asperities of the GDL and bipolar plate achieve sufficient real-area contact to keep interfacial contact resistance acceptably low (per Holm's contact theory, $R_c \propto 1/\sqrt{P_c}$). The upper bound (**2.0 MPa**) is dictated by the structural integrity of the porous GDL: above this value, the carbon fibres of the GDL begin to collapse, reducing porosity from ~70% to below ~50%, which blocks gas transport pathways to the catalyst layer and induces severe mass-transport polarisation. Higher pressures also risk puncturing the MEA and extruding the gaskets. Thus the 0.5–2.0 MPa window balances **electrical performance** (low $R_c$) against **mechanical survivability** of the soft porous components. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > KTU ESE Module Internal Choice: **Answer ANY ONE** of the following two questions. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the **components of a fuel cell stack clamping assembly** and explain the role of each component. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A PEMFC stack has **40 cells**, each with an active area of **300 cm²**. The design target is a uniform contact pressure of **1.5 MPa**. The clamping uses **6 tie-rods** of nominal diameter **10 mm** with a nut factor of **0.20**. Calculate: (i) the total clamping force, (ii) the load per tie-rod, (iii) the tightening torque per bolt, and (iv) the torsional shear stress in the bolt. **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution (a):** The clamping assembly consists of the following components arranged in a series from top to bottom: 1. **Top End Plate (Compression Plate):** A thick, rigid plate (Aluminium 6061-T6 or stainless steel 316L) that distributes the bolt preload uniformly over the entire active area of the cell. 2. **Electrical Insulator Plate:** A polymer or ceramic plate placed between the metallic end plate and the current collector to prevent the tie-rods from short-circuiting the entire stack electrically. 3. **Current Collector Plate:** A copper or gold-plated plate that collects the cumulative stack current with minimum ohmic loss. 4. **Tie-Rods (4 or 6 in number):** High-tensile steel rods (grade 8.8 or 10.9) that pass through clearance holes in the corners of both end plates and transmit the clamping force in pure tension. 5. **Insulating Sleeves and Bushings:** Polymer (PTFE or PEEK) sleeves around tie-rods where they pass through metallic components, providing additional electrical isolation. 6. **Nuts and Washers:** Hex nuts tightened to a specified torque value to apply the preload; belleville washers may be used to accommodate thermal expansion and creep. 7. **Bottom End Plate:** Identical to the top end plate, completing the load path. **Roles:** End plates distribute load, tie-rods carry tension, insulators isolate electrically, current collectors gather current, and nuts apply the preload that maintains the entire assembly under compression. #### **Model Solution (b):** **(i) Total Clamping Force:** $$A_{active} = 300 \text{ cm}^{2} = 300 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2} = 0.0300 \text{ m}^{2}$$ $$P_{contact} = 1.5 \text{ MPa} = 1.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$ $$F_{clamp} = P_{contact} \times A_{active} = (1.5 \times 10^{6}) \times (0.0300)$$ $$F_{clamp} = 45{,}000 \text{ N} = 45 \text{ kN}$$ **(ii) Load per Tie-Rod (n = 6):** $$F_{bolt} = \frac{F_{clamp}}{n} = \frac{45{,}000}{6} = 7{,}500 \text{ N}$$ **(iii) Tightening Torque per Bolt (d = 10 mm = 0.010 m, k = 0.20):** $$T_{bolt} = k \cdot F_{bolt} \cdot d = 0.20 \times 7{,}500 \times 0.010$$ $$T_{bolt} = 15.0 \text{ N·m}$$ **(iv) Torsional Shear Stress in the Bolt:** $$\tau_{bolt} = \frac{16 \cdot T_{bolt}}{\pi \cdot d^{3}} = \frac{16 \times 15.0}{\pi \times (0.010)^{3}}$$ $$\tau_{bolt} = \frac{240.0}{3.1416 \times 10^{-6}} = 7.64 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 76.4 \text{ MPa}$$ This stress is well below the allowable torsional shear stress for grade 8.8 bolts (~369 MPa), confirming a safe design. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **four major types of clamping mechanisms** used in fuel cell stacks, with their advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A steel tie-rod of length **0.40 m** and cross-sectional area **85 mm²** is preloaded to **10 kN** at an assembly temperature of **25 °C**. During operation, the stack temperature rises to **80 °C**. Given $E_{steel} = 200$ GPa and $\alpha_{steel} = 12 \times 10^{-6}/°C$, calculate: (i) the free thermal expansion of the rod, (ii) the constrained thermal stress generated, and (iii) comment on whether the original preload is retained, lost, or increased. **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution (a):** The four major clamping mechanisms are: 1. **Tie-Rod and Bolt Clamping:** Steel rods pass through the corners of the end plates; nuts are tightened to a specified torque. *Advantages:* simple, low cost, easy field servicing, well-established engineering practice. *Limitations:* non-uniform pressure distribution (corners receive more load), susceptibility to bolt loosening under vibration and thermal cycling. 2. **Band or Strap Clamping:** Metal bands or straps wrap around the perimeter of the stack and tension to apply a uniform radial compressive load. *Advantages:* very uniform perimeter pressure, lightweight, compact footprint. *Limitations:* cannot apply high loads, limited to small stacks, difficult to integrate with active cooling. 3. **Hydraulic / Pneumatic Clamping:** A hydraulic bladder or pneumatic piston integrated into the end plate applies a controlled uniform pressure. *Advantages:* highly uniform pressure across the active area, actively adjustable in real time, automatic compensation for thermal expansion and creep. *Limitations:* complex auxiliary system (pump, accumulator, seals), risk of hydraulic fluid leakage into the cell, expensive. 4. **Compression Frame / Moulded CFRP Frame:** A single-piece compression-moulded frame (carbon-fibre reinforced polymer or engineering thermoplastic) clamps the stack without separate fasteners. *Advantages:* excellent uniformity, very low profile, lightweight. *Limitations:* non-adjustable, brittle failure mode, single-use replacement. #### **Model Solution (b):** **Given data:** $L = 0.40 \text{ m}$, $A = 85 \text{ mm}^{2} = 85 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2}$, $F_0 = 10 \text{ kN} = 10{,}000 \text{ N}$, $T_1 = 25 °C$, $T_2 = 80 °C$, $E = 200 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa}$, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$. **(i) Free thermal expansion:** $$\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T = (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.40 \times (80 - 25)$$ $$\Delta L = 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.40 \times 55 = 2.64 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.264 \text{ mm}$$ **(ii) Constrained thermal stress (if both ends fully fixed):** $$\sigma_{thermal} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T = (200 \times 10^{9}) \times (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 55$$ $$\sigma_{thermal} = 1.32 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 132 \text{ MPa}$$ **(iii) Constrained thermal force:** $$F_{thermal} = \sigma_{thermal} \times A = 132 \times 10^{6} \times 85 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$F_{thermal} = 11{,}220 \text{ N} = 11.22 \text{ kN}$$ **Comment on preload:** The thermal force generated (**11.22 kN**) is **greater** than the original bolt preload (**10 kN**). If the rod is fully constrained (gaskets and stack components do not yield), the rod goes into *additional tension*, increasing the effective clamping force on the stack by ~12%. However, in reality, the elastomeric gaskets compress (creep) and the MEA/GDL assemblies yield slightly, partially absorbing the differential expansion. The net effect depends on the **relative stiffness** of the tie-rod versus the stack components: - If $k_{rod} \gg k_{stack}$ → most of the expansion is absorbed by stack compression (gasket creep), preload may *decrease*. - If $k_{rod} \ll k_{stack}$ → rod goes into additional tension, preload *increases*. In practice, designers use **spring washers** or **in-series disc springs** to maintain a nearly constant preload across the operating temperature range. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Mixing up units:** Forgetting to convert cm² to m² when calculating force is the most common error (eats **2 marks** instantly). > 2. **Forgetting the nut factor:** Writing $T = F \cdot d$ without the nut factor $k$ is a **3-mark deduction**. > 3. **Ignoring differential thermal expansion:** Examiners award marks for acknowledging that the *rod* and the *end plates/gaskets* expand at *different rates*. A bare thermal expansion of the rod alone is incomplete. > 4. **Confusing active area with total plate area:** The contact pressure acts on the *active* area, not the entire plate area — students who use the full plate area in $F = P \times A$ lose 1–2 marks. > 5. **Stating safety factors without calculation:** Always *compute* the factor of safety and *compare* it with a code value (e.g., FoS > 4 for static load). Vague statements are penalised. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Stack clamping** is the application of a controlled compressive load to maintain electrical contact, gasket sealing, and mechanical integrity. - **Optimum contact pressure window for PEMFC:** **0.5–2.0 MPa**. Below 0.5 MPa → high $R_c$; above 2.0 MPa → GDL crushing and MEA damage. - **Core equation:** $F_{clamp} = P_{contact} \times A_{active}$. Convert cm² to m² before substitution. - **Bolt preload:** $F_{bolt} = F_{clamp} / n$, where $n$ is the number of tie-rods (commonly 4, 6, or 8). - **Tightening torque:** $T_{bolt} = k \cdot F_{bolt} \cdot d$, with **k ≈ 0.20** for lubricated steel-on-steel. - **Torsional shear stress:** $\tau_{bolt} = 16 T_{bolt} / (\pi d^{3})$. Always check against allowable $\tau \approx 0.577 \sigma_y$. - **End-plate bending:** $\sigma_{max} = M_{max} / Z$, with $Z = bt^{2}/6$ for a rectangular cross-section. - **Thermal compensation:** Differential expansion $\delta = (\alpha_{rod} - \alpha_{plate}) \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ must be absorbed by gasket compliance or spring washers. - **Clamping types:** Tie-rod (simple, non-uniform), Band (uniform perimeter, low load), Hydraulic (uniform, adjustable, complex), Compression frame (uniform, non-adjustable, brittle). - **Design rule of thumb:** Always aim for a **factor of safety ≥ 4** on bolts and ≥ 6 on end plates to account for vibration, thermal cycling, and long-term creep. - **KTU exam hot points:** Total clamping force calculation, bolt torque, thermal expansion compensation, comparison of clamping methods, and identification of components in a sketch.

topics.title: Fuel Cell System Design: Hydrogen-Oxygen Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT721 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fuel Cell System Design: Hydrogen-Oxygen Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fuel Cell System Design: Hydrogen-Oxygen Systems** refers to the integrated engineering discipline of configuring, sizing, and interconnecting all subsystems required to deliver a fully operational electrochemical power plant that consumes molecular hydrogen ($\text{H}_2$) at the anode and molecular oxygen ($\text{O}_2$) at the cathode to generate direct-current electricity, byproduct water, and usable heat.

A complete $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ fuel cell system is *not* simply the electrochemical stack. It comprises four engineering layers:

1. **The Stack (Engine Core):** Membrane Electrode Assembly (MEA), bipolar plates, flow-fields, current collectors, and end-plates.
2. **Reactant Subsystem (Fuel & Oxidant Delivery):** Hydrogen supply train (storage, pressure regulation, humidification, recirculation) and oxygen supply train (tank, blower/compressor, filter, humidifier).
3. **Thermal & Water Management Subsystem (BoP):** Coolant loops, heat exchangers, condensers, and water separators.
4. **Power Conditioning & Control Subsystem (PCS):** DC-DC converters, DC-AC inverters, sensors, and the control logic governing stoichiometry, pressure differential, and temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A fuel cell *system* is the sum of **Stack + BoP (Balance of Plant) + BOP (Balance of Plant auxiliaries) + PCS**. The stack alone is a laboratory artefact; only the *system* is a deployable product.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Living Engine" Picture

Think of a $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ fuel cell system the way you would think of a human body:
- The **Stack** is the **heart** — it pumps out the useful work (electric current).
- The **Hydrogen Subsystem** is the **lungs + fuel tank** — it supplies the food ($\text{H}_2$) and removes waste.
- The **Oxygen Subsystem** is the **mouth/nose** — it draws in air and filters it.
- The **Thermal Subsystem** is the **sweat glands + blood circulation** — it removes excess heat and distributes water.
- The **PCS** is the **nervous system** — it monitors and signals every other organ.

Just as a heart by itself cannot sustain life, a fuel cell stack by itself cannot power a load. You need the entire "body" of the system to function.

### 1.3 Why Hydrogen-Oxygen Specifically?

Hydrogen is the simplest, most reactive fuel, and the $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ reaction has the **highest theoretical specific energy** of any common fuel cell chemistry: **$\mathbf{120 \; MJ/kg}$** (Lower Heating Value basis) — roughly **3× that of gasoline**.

The reversible cell potential at standard conditions is **$\mathbf{E^{\circ}_{cell} = 1.229 \; V}$**, derived from the global reaction:

$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$$

with a corresponding molar enthalpy of **$\mathbf{\Delta H^{\circ} = -285.83 \; kJ/mol}$** and Gibbs free energy of **$\mathbf{\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.13 \; kJ/mol}$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant (Faraday's constant):** $\mathbf{F = 96{,}485 \; C/mol}$ — the cornerstone of every charge-balance calculation in fuel cell design.

### 1.4 Classification of $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ Systems

| Sub-Type | Electrolyte | Operating T | Stack Size Range | Primary Application |
|----------|-------------|------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **PEMFC** | Proton Exchange Membrane | 60–80 °C | 1 W – 250 kW | Automobiles, backup power |
| **AFC** | Alkaline (KOH) | 60–90 °C | 1 kW – 100 kW | Space (Apollo/Shuttle heritage) |
| **PAFC** | Phosphoric Acid | 150–200 °C | 50 kW – 11 MW | Stationary CHP |
| **SOFC** | Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia | 600–1000 °C | 1 kW – multi-MW | Large stationary, APU |

All four can in principle be run on pure $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ — but the BoP design changes drastically with temperature and electrolyte.


<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation: From Half-Cells to System Voltage

The operating voltage of a single cell in the stack is governed by the **cell-voltage-loss equation**:

$$V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$$

Each term is a *loss* that the system designer must quantify and minimise.

#### 2.1.1 The Nernst Reversible Potential

The open-circuit thermodynamic potential of the $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ cell, valid at any combination of temperature and partial pressures, is:

$$E_{Nernst} = E^{\circ}_{cell} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\frac{p_{H_2}\sqrt{p_{O_2}}}{p_{H_2O}}\right)$$

- $E^{\circ}_{cell} = 1.229 \; V$ at **298.15 K**, 1 atm, liquid water product.
- $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$ — universal gas constant.
- The factor **$n = 2$** electrons per $\text{H}_2$ molecule reacted.
- Partial pressures in **atm** for the KTU standard form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication:** Doubling the $\text{H}_2$ partial pressure raises $E_{Nernst}$ by only **~17 mV at 80 °C** — meaning pressurisation has a *thermodynamic* benefit but a *much larger* parasitic cost (compressor work). The BoP designer must compute the net gain.

#### 2.1.2 Activation Overpotential — The Tafel Equation

$$\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\!\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right) = A \ln\!\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$$

- $i$ = current density ($\text{A/cm}^2$).
- $i_0$ = exchange current density (electrocatalyst property).
- $\alpha$ = charge-transfer coefficient (typically 0.5 for Pt).
- $A$ = Tafel slope (V/decade), typically **$\approx 0.06$ V/dec** at 80 °C for Pt/cathode.

#### 2.1.3 Ohmic Overpotential — Ohm's Law for the MEA

$$\eta_{ohm} = i \cdot R_{ASR}$$

where $R_{ASR}$ is the **Area-Specific Resistance** in $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$, combining membrane proton resistance, contact resistance, and bulk electrolyte resistance.

#### 2.1.4 Concentration Overpotential — Mass-Transport Ceiling

$$\eta_{conc} = -\frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(1 - \frac{i}{i_L}\right)$$

where $i_L$ is the **limiting current density** — the current at which reactant cannot arrive at the catalyst fast enough. The system designer sizes the flow-field and stoichiometry to keep operating $i \le 0.5\,i_L$.

### 2.2 System-Level Thermodynamic Efficiency

The **electrical efficiency** of the system, accounting only for the fuel input (LHV basis), is:

$$\eta_{sys} = \eta_{th} \times \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{far} \times \eta_{PCS}$$

- $\eta_{th} = \Delta G / \Delta H$ = **83% (LHV)** at 25 °C, **94% (HHV)**.
- $\eta_{vol} = V_{cell}/E_{Nernst}$ = voltage efficiency.
- $\eta_{far}$ = Faraday efficiency (typically **>99%** for $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$).
- $\eta_{PCS}$ = power-conditioning efficiency (**92–97%** for modern DC-DC + inverter stages).

> [!NOTE]
> **Heating Value Choice — KTU Pitfall:** Many KTU questions ask "why LHV is preferred for vehicle fuel cells?" The reason: **the water product exits the fuel cell as vapour**, so its latent heat is NOT available as useful thermal output. Hence LHV is the correct denominator.

### 2.3 Mass Balance on the Stack

For a stack of $N$ cells delivering current $I$:

**Hydrogen consumption:**
$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \frac{N \cdot I}{2F} \quad [\text{mol/s}]$$

**Oxygen consumption:**
$$\dot{n}_{O_2} = \frac{N \cdot I}{4F} \quad [\text{mol/s}]$$

**Water produced (liquid):**
$$\dot{m}_{H_2O} = \frac{N \cdot I \cdot M_{H_2O}}{2F} \quad [\text{kg/s}], \quad M_{H_2O}=18.015 \text{ g/mol}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stoichiometric Multiplier (Design Lever):** In practice, the BoP supplies reactants at $\zeta_{H_2} \approx 1.2$ and $\zeta_{O_2} \approx 2.0$ (excess to prevent starvation). This must be accounted for in the sizing of the air compressor/blower and the $\text{H}_2$ recirculator.

### 2.4 Heat Generation in the System

The **waste heat** that the thermal subsystem must remove is:

$$Q_{gen} = N \cdot I \cdot (E_{th} - V_{cell}) = \dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot (\Delta H - \Delta G)$$

where $E_{th} = \Delta H / (nF) = 1.481 \; V$ (HHV) or **$1.253 \; V$ (LHV)** — the so-called **thermo-neutral voltage**.

This heat is split between the coolant loop, the cathode exhaust (water + nitrogen), and radiation/convection. A typical 50 kW PEMFC system rejects **~50–60 kW of heat** that must be evacuated by the BoP.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|----------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $E^{\circ}_{cell} = 1.229$ V | Standard reversible potential at 25 °C | V |
| 2 | $E_{Nernst} = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\big(\frac{p_{H_2}\sqrt{p_{O_2}}}{p_{H_2O}}\big)$ | Reversible potential at any T, P | V |
| 3 | $\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln(i/i_0)$ | Activation loss (Tafel) | V |
| 4 | $\eta_{ohm} = i \cdot R_{ASR}$ | Ohmic loss (linear in i) | V |
| 5 | $\eta_{conc} = -\frac{RT}{nF}\ln(1 - i/i_L)$ | Mass-transport loss | V |
| 6 | $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$ | Polarisation curve | V |
| 7 | $\eta_{th} = \Delta G / \Delta H$ | Thermodynamic efficiency | 0.83 (LHV) |
| 8 | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{far} \cdot \eta_{PCS}$ | Net system efficiency | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\dot{n}_{H_2} = N I / 2F$ | Hydrogen flow rate | mol/s |
| 10 | $\dot{n}_{O_2} = N I / 4F$ | Oxygen flow rate | mol/s |
| 11 | $E_{th} = 1.481$ V (HHV) | Thermo-neutral voltage | V |
| 12 | $Q_{gen} = N I (E_{th} - V_{cell})$ | Waste heat load on BoP | W |
| 13 | $P_{stack} = N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I$ | Gross electrical output | W |
| 14 | $P_{net} = P_{stack} - P_{BoP}$ | Net system output | W |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility of $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ System Design

- **Aerospace (NASA, Boeing):** Pure $\text{O}_2$ reactant enables high open-circuit voltage (>1.3 V) and high power density; the alkaline Apollo system ran for 18 mission cycles.
- **Automotive (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo):** PEMFC at 650–700 kPa $\text{H}_2$ with ambient air; the BoP parasitic load is the limiting factor for cold start and altitude performance.
- **Submarines (Type 212, Type 214):** Closed-cycle $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ system with cryogenic storage; produces potable water as a co-product (240 kW → ~480 L/day).
- **Backup power (data centres):** 5–50 kW PEMFC units using compressed $\text{H}_2}$ tanks with grid-independent operation.


<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Gibbs Free Energy to the Nernst Equation

**Start:** The general Nernst equation for any cell reaction $aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$ is:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the reaction quotient.

**Step 1 — Identify Q for the $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ reaction:**

$$Q = \frac{a_{H_2O}}{a_{H_2}\,a_{O_2}^{1/2}}$$

For ideal gases and assuming liquid water product ($a_{H_2O} = 1$):

$$Q \;\approx\; \frac{p_{H_2O}/p^{\circ}}{(p_{H_2}/p^{\circ})(p_{O_2}/p^{\circ})^{1/2}} \;=\; \frac{p_{H_2O}}{p_{H_2}\,\sqrt{p_{O_2}}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\frac{p_{H_2O}}{p_{H_2}\sqrt{p_{O_2}}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Flip the log to put products in the numerator (Nernst's historical form):**

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\frac{p_{H_2}\sqrt{p_{O_2}}}{p_{H_2O}}\right)$$

with $n = 2$ electrons per mole of $\text{H}_2$.

> **Result — the canonical KTU form:** $\;E_{Nernst} = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\frac{p_{H_2}\sqrt{p_{O_2}}}{p_{H_2O}}\right)$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stoichiometric Air Flow for the Cathode

**Given:** A PEMFC stack with $N = 100$ cells, $V_{cell} = 0.70$ V at the design point, $I = 200$ A.

**Step 1 — Compute the moles of $\text{H}_2}$ consumed:**
