$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \frac{N I}{2F} = \frac{100 \times 200}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \frac{20{,}000}{192{,}970} = 0.1036 \;\text{mol/s}$$

**Step 2 — Oxygen consumed (stoichiometric):**

$$\dot{n}_{O_2,\,stoich} = \frac{\dot{n}_{H_2}}{2} = 0.0518 \;\text{mol/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply oxygen stoichiometry $\zeta_{O_2} = 2.0$:**

$$\dot{n}_{O_2,\,supplied} = 2.0 \times 0.0518 = 0.1036 \;\text{mol/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to volume flow at STP (22.414 L/mol):**

$$Q_{O_2} = 0.1036 \times 22.414 = 2.32 \;\text{L/s} = 139 \;\text{L/min}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to air flow ($\text{O}_2$ is 21% of air by volume):**

$$Q_{air} = \frac{2.32}{0.21} = 11.05 \;\text{L/s} = 663 \;\text{L/min at STP}$$

**Step 6 — Real-world correction: 1 atm → 1.5 atm, 25 °C → 80 °C stack inlet:**

$$Q_{air,\,actual} = 11.05 \times \frac{1.5}{1.0} \times \frac{(80+273)}{298} = 11.05 \times 1.5 \times 1.186 = 19.65 \;\text{L/s}$$

**Step 7 — Blower sizing (rule of thumb: pressure ratio × flow):**

A centrifugal blower rated at $\sim 1.5$ bar with 20 L/s would be selected, drawing $\sim 200$ W of parasitic power.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Stack Sizing for a Target Power Output

**Target:** $P_{net} = 10 \; kW$ at $V_{cell} = 0.65 \; V$; assume 90% PCS efficiency and 5% BoP parasitic loss on stack output.

**Step 1 — Required gross stack power:**

$$P_{stack} = \frac{P_{net}}{0.90} = \frac{10{,}000}{0.90} = 11{,}111 \;\text{W}$$

**Step 2 — Choose current density $i = 0.5 \;\text{A/cm}^2$ (typical PEMFC design point):**

$$A_{cell} = \frac{I}{i} = \frac{P_{stack}}{V_{cell} \cdot i \cdot N}$$

**Step 3 — Choose active area $A_{cell} = 250 \;\text{cm}^2$ (industry standard):**

$$I = i \cdot A_{cell} = 0.5 \times 250 = 125 \;\text{A per cell}$$

**Step 4 — Number of cells required:**

$$N = \frac{P_{stack}}{V_{cell} \cdot I} = \frac{11{,}111}{0.65 \times 125} = 136.8 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = 137 \text{ cells}$$

**Step 5 — Stack dimensions (active):**

$$A_{stack} = N \cdot A_{cell} = 137 \times 250 = 34{,}250 \;\text{cm}^2 = 3.43 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Volume estimate (typical power density 1.5 kW/L for automotive PEMFC):**

$$V_{stack} = \frac{10}{1.5} = 6.67 \;\text{L}$$

**Result:** A 137-cell stack on 250 cm² plates occupies ~7 L, delivers 10 kW gross at 0.65 V/cell.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Heat Load and Coolant Flow

**Given:** The 10 kW stack from §3.3.

**Step 1 — Waste heat per cell:**

$$Q_{cell} = I (E_{th} - V_{cell}) = 125 \times (1.253 - 0.65) = 75.4 \;\text{W}$$

**Step 2 — Total stack heat:**

$$Q_{stack} = N \cdot Q_{cell} = 137 \times 75.4 = 10{,}330 \;\text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Coolant flow (water, $\Delta T = 10 \; K$):**

$$\dot{m}_{cool} = \frac{Q_{stack}}{c_p \Delta T} = \frac{10{,}330}{4180 \times 10} = 0.247 \;\text{kg/s} = 14.8 \;\text{L/min}$$

**Step 4 — Radiator sizing:** This heat must be rejected to ambient; a 10 kW automotive radiator would be required, with fan parasitic of $\sim 100$ W.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — System Design Trade-Off Table

| Design Decision | ↑ in Parameter | Benefit | Penalty / Trade-off |
|-----------------|----------------|---------|---------------------|
| Operating pressure | ↑ | Higher $E_{Nernst}$, smaller stack | ↑ Compressor parasitic work |
| Operating temperature | ↑ | Faster kinetics (↓ $\eta_{act}$), easier cooling | ↑ Membrane drying, ↓ lifetime |
| Stoichiometry $\zeta_{O_2}$ | ↑ | Avoid starvation at peak load | ↑ Blower work, ↓ efficiency |
| Pt loading | ↑ | Lower $\eta_{act}$ | ↑ Cost (Pt \$/g) |
| Active area $A_{cell}$ | ↑ | Higher current, fewer cells | ↑ Edge failure, ↑ cost |
| Number of cells $N$ | ↑ | Higher voltage, fewer stacks | ↑ Stack height, ↑ manifold design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Nearly every Part B question in Module 4 begins with *"Design a 10 kW PEMFC system…"*. Memorise the **6-step sizing methodology** above: (1) net power, (2) gross power, (3) per-cell current, (4) cell count, (5) hydrogen flow, (6) heat load.


<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Level System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUB1[Hydrogen Subsystem]
        H2Tank[H2 Storage Tank]
        H2Reg[Pressure Regulator]
        H2Hum[Humidifier]
        H2Inj[Anode Inlet Manifold]
        H2Recirc[Recirculation Pump]
        H2Vent[Purge Vent]
    end

    subgraph SUB2[Oxygen Subsystem]
        AirIn[Air Intake Filter]
        AirComp[Compressor or Blower]
        AirHum[Cathode Humidifier]
        AirInj[Cathode Inlet Manifold]
        AirExh[Cathode Exhaust]
    end

    subgraph SUB3[Stack Core]
        StackFC[FC Stack N cells MEA]
        StackBP[Bipolar Plates]
        StackEP[End Plates Current Collectors]
    end

    subgraph SUB4[Thermal Subsystem BoP]
        CoolIn[Coolant Inlet]
        CoolPmp[Coolant Pump]
        RadHX[Heat Exchanger Radiator]
        CoolOut[Coolant Outlet]
        WatSep[Water Separator]
    end

    subgraph SUB5[Power Conditioning]
        DCDC[DC DC Converter]
        INVT[DC AC Inverter]
        LOAD[AC or DC Load]
    end

    subgraph SUB6[Control Layer]
        ECU[System ECU PLC]
        SENS[Voltage Current T P Sensors]
    end

    H2Tank --> H2Reg --> H2Hum --> H2Inj --> StackFC
    StackFC --> H2Recirc --> H2Inj
    StackFC --> H2Vent

    AirIn --> AirComp --> AirHum --> AirInj --> StackFC
    StackFC --> AirExh --> WatSep

    StackFC --> StackBP --> StackEP --> DCDC --> INVT --> LOAD

    StackFC --> CoolIn --> CoolPmp --> RadHX --> CoolOut
    RadHX --> AirExh

    SENS -.-> ECU
    ECU -.-> H2Reg
    ECU -.-> AirComp
    ECU -.-> CoolPmp
    ECU -.-> DCDC
```

### 4.2 MEA Cross-Section Schematic (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MEM[MEA Functional Stack]
        BP1[Bipolar Plate Anode Side]
        GDL_A[GDL Anode]
        CL_A[Anode Catalyst Layer Pt]
        MEM_PEM[Proton Exchange Membrane Nafion]
        CL_C[Cathode Catalyst Layer Pt]
        GDL_C[GDL Cathode]
        BP2[Bipolar Plate Cathode Side]
    end

    H2A[H2 Gas Channel] --> BP1 --> GDL_A --> CL_A
    CL_A --> MEM_PEM
    MEM_PEM --> CL_C --> GDL_C --> BP2 --> O2C[O2 Gas Channel]

    H2A -.- REACT1[Anode Reaction H2 to 2H plus 2e]
    O2C -.- REACT2[Cathode Reaction 1 over 2 O2 to 2H2O]
    MEM_PEM -.- IONDIR[Proton H plus moves Anode to Cathode]
    BP1 -.- EDIR[Electrons e travel through external circuit]
```

### 4.3 System Operational State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> STANDBY
    STANDBY --> STARTUP: H2 supply ON, Air ON
    STARTUP --> PURGE: N2 purge cycle
    PURGE --> RAMPUP: Load request
    RAMPUP --> STEADY: Vcell in 0.6 to 0.75 range
    STEADY --> PEAKLOD: Load spike to 1.5x rated
    PEAKLOD --> STEADY: Load normalises
    STEADY --> SHUTDOWN: Stop command
    SHUTDOWN --> PURGED: N2 purge
    PURGED --> STANDBY
    STEADY --> FAULT: Vcell less than 0.4V or T over 90C
    FAULT --> SHUTDOWN: Emergency isolation
    SHUTDOWN --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Mass and Energy Flow Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN1[H2 in m dot nH2 kg per s] -->|Feed| STK[FC Stack]
    IN2[O2 or Air in m dot nO2] -->|Feed| STK
    STK -->|Electrical Pnet| OUT1[DC Bus]
    STK -->|Water H2O liquid and vapour| OUT2[Cathode Exhaust]
    STK -->|Heat Qgen| OUT3[Coolant Loop]
    STK -->|Unreacted H2| REC[Recirculation Loop]
    REC -->|Return| STK
    OUT1 --> PCS[Power Conditioning]
    PCS -->|Pload| LOAD[End User]
    OUT3 --> RAD[Radiator]
    RAD -->|Qrejected| AMB[Ambient]
    PCS -.->|Parasitic Ppara| PUMP[BoP Loads]
    PUMP -.->|Less effective| OUT1
```


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define Balance of Plant (BoP) for a $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ PEMFC system. List any four major BoP subsystems.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Balance of Plant (BoP) comprises all the auxiliary components external to the electrochemical stack that are required for its safe, efficient, and continuous operation. The four major BoP subsystems are:
1. **Hydrogen delivery subsystem** — storage, regulator, humidifier, recirculator.
2. **Air/oxygen delivery subsystem** — filter, compressor/blower, humidifier.
3. **Thermal management subsystem** — coolant pump, radiator, water separator.
4. **Power conditioning subsystem (PCS)** — DC-DC converter, inverter, sensors, ECU.

(1 mark for definition; 2 marks for the four subsystems — ½ mark each.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
Why is the Lower Heating Value (LHV) used as the basis for computing fuel cell efficiency, instead of the Higher Heating Value (HHV)?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The product water in a $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ fuel cell typically exits as **vapour** in the cathode exhaust. The latent heat of vaporisation contained in the HHV is therefore *not* recovered as useful thermal energy in the system. Using HHV would over-state the achievable efficiency by ~11% (HHV/LHV ratio for $\text{H}_2$ = 1.18). LHV, which excludes this latent heat, gives the **realistic electrical + recoverable-heat output** and is the standard basis used by the US DOE, EU, and the KTU syllabus. (2 marks for the latent-heat reasoning; 1 mark for the convention.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — System Sizing Problem (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, adapted]** 
A PEM fuel cell stack is required to deliver a **net output of 50 kW** to a DC bus. Design assumptions:
- Cell operating voltage: $V_{cell} = 0.70$ V
- Current density: $i = 0.6 \; A/cm^2$
- Active cell area: $A_{cell} = 300 \; cm^2$
- Number of cells: $N = 250$
- Oxygen stoichiometry: $\zeta_{O_2} = 2.0$
- Stack temperature: 70 °C, pressure 1 atm
- Power conditioning efficiency: 95 %

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the (i) gross stack power, (ii) per-cell current, (iii) total hydrogen consumption rate, and (iv) the volumetric air flow required at STP.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Calculate the (i) total heat that the BoP must reject, (ii) the coolant water mass flow rate for a 10 °C temperature rise, and (iii) the stack's electrical efficiency on an LHV basis (assuming 100% Faraday efficiency).

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**(i) Gross stack power (1 mark):**

$$P_{stack} = \frac{P_{net}}{\eta_{PCS}} = \frac{50{,}000}{0.95} = 52{,}632 \;\text{W} \;\approx\; 52.63 \;\text{kW}$$

**(ii) Per-cell current (1.5 marks):**

$$I = i \cdot A_{cell} = 0.6 \;\text{A/cm}^2 \times 300 \;\text{cm}^2 = 180 \;\text{A}$$

**(iii) Hydrogen consumption (2 marks):**

$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \frac{N \cdot I}{2F} = \frac{250 \times 180}{2 \times 96{,}485} = \frac{45{,}000}{192{,}970} = 0.2332 \;\text{mol/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 0.2332 \times 2.016 = 0.470 \;\text{g/s} = 1.69 \;\text{kg/h}$$

**(iv) Air flow at STP (2.5 marks):**

Stoichiometric oxygen:

$$\dot{n}_{O_2,\,stoich} = \frac{\dot{n}_{H_2}}{2} = 0.1166 \;\text{mol/s}$$

With $\zeta_{O_2} = 2.0$:

$$\dot{n}_{O_2,\,supplied} = 2 \times 0.1166 = 0.2332 \;\text{mol/s}$$

At STP (22.414 L/mol):

$$Q_{O_2} = 0.2332 \times 22.414 = 5.227 \;\text{L/s} = 313.6 \;\text{L/min}$$

In air (21 % $\text{O}_2$ by volume):

$$Q_{air} = \frac{5.227}{0.21} = 24.89 \;\text{L/s} = 1493 \;\text{L/min at STP}$$

**Part (a) total: 7 marks.**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Heat to be rejected (3 marks):**

Thermo-neutral voltage on LHV basis: $E_{th} = 1.253$ V.

Heat per cell:

$$q_{cell} = I (E_{th} - V_{cell}) = 180 \times (1.253 - 0.70) = 180 \times 0.553 = 99.54 \;\text{W}$$

Total stack heat:

$$Q_{stack} = N \cdot q_{cell} = 250 \times 99.54 = 24{,}885 \;\text{W} \;\approx\; 24.9 \;\text{kW}$$

**(ii) Coolant mass flow (2 marks):**

Using water ($c_p = 4180 \;\text{J/(kg·K)}$), $\Delta T = 10 \; K$:

$$\dot{m}_{cool} = \frac{Q_{stack}}{c_p \Delta T} = \frac{24{,}885}{4180 \times 10} = 0.595 \;\text{kg/s} = 35.7 \;\text{L/min}$$

**(iii) LHV efficiency (2 marks):**

LHV of $\text{H}_2$ = $\Delta H = 285.83 \;\text{kJ/mol}$.
Fuel input rate (LHV):

$$\dot{Q}_{fuel} = \dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot \Delta H = 0.2332 \times 285.83 = 66.66 \;\text{kJ/s} = 66.66 \;\text{kW (LHV)}$$

Gross electrical efficiency:

$$\eta_{stack} = \frac{P_{stack}}{\dot{Q}_{fuel}} = \frac{52.63}{66.66} = 0.789 = 78.9 \;\%$$

Net system efficiency (including PCS):

$$\eta_{net} = 0.789 \times 0.95 = 0.750 = 75.0 \;\%$$

**Part (b) total: 7 marks.**

---

#### **Question B — Conceptual / Descriptive (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** 
**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the major subsystems of a closed-cycle $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ fuel cell system used in a submarine application. Explain why closed-cycle operation is preferred over air-breathing designs.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the major design trade-offs involved when selecting (i) operating pressure, (ii) operating temperature, and (iii) reactant stoichiometry. Use a polarisation curve sketch to illustrate your argument.

---

**Model Solution Outline — Part (a):**

| Subsystem | Component | Function |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| $\text{H}_2$ Storage | Cryogenic LH2 or metal hydride | Long-duration supply |
| $\text{O}_2$ Storage | LOX cryotank | High density, no compressor |
| Stack | PEMFC or AFC, 4–8 modules in series | 240–340 kW gross |
| Reactant Feed | Pressure regulators, no compressor | Silent operation |
| Heat Rejection | Seawater heat exchanger | Reject 50–60 % of LHV |
| Water Recovery | Condenser + demister | Drinking water + humidification |
| Power Train | DC-DC + DC-AC | 350 V DC bus → AC propulsion |
| Control | Redundant PLC | Mission-critical isolation |

(1 mark per subsystem row, capped at 4 marks; 2 marks for the closed-cycle rationale: no detection, no exhaust plume, no intake noise, water recovery for crew, mission duration; 1 mark for the block diagram.)

**Part (a) total: 7 marks.**

**Model Solution Outline — Part (b):**

(i) **Pressure (2 marks):** ↑ P → ↑ $E_{Nernst}$ (thermodynamic gain ~17 mV per doubling at 80 °C) but ↑ compressor work. Net optimum is 2–3 bar for automotive; 1 bar (ambient) acceptable for low-power stationary.

(ii) **Temperature (2 marks):** ↑ T → ↓ $\eta_{act}$ (faster kinetics) and ↑ $\eta_{conc}$ (lower $\text{O}_2$ solubility). Membrane must stay hydrated; optimum for Nafion is 60–80 °C. PAFC goes to 200 °C; SOFC to 800 °C to use solid-state kinetics.

(iii) **Stoichiometry (2 marks):** ↑ $\zeta_{O_2}$ → uniform current distribution, no starvation at peak, but ↓ efficiency due to blower work. Industry values: $\zeta_{O_2}$ = 1.5–2.5, $\zeta_{H_2}$ = 1.1–1.3 (with recirculation).

(1 mark for the polarisation curve sketch with the three loss regions labelled and the operating point marked.)

**Part (b) total: 7 marks.**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 4 Sizing Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide $\dot{n}_{H_2}$ by 2 to get $\dot{n}_{O_2}$.** Many students write $\dot{n}_{O_2} = N I / 2F$ — this is wrong; the correct factor is $4F$ in the denominator.
> 2. **Confusing HHV and LHV thermoneutral voltage.** Use $E_{th} = 1.253$ V for LHV, **1.481 V for HHV**. The question wording "electrical efficiency" almost always means LHV in KTU papers.
> 3. **Reporting air flow in mol/s when the answer key expects L/min.** Always convert to a practical engineering unit at the final step.
> 4. **Neglecting stoichiometric excess.** A real BoP delivers $\zeta_{O_2} = 2 \times$ stoichiometric; the question usually states this — read it twice.
> 5. **Omitting the PCS efficiency in net power calculations.** Net = Gross × $\eta_{PCS}$; forgetting this loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Wrong sign in the Nernst equation.** The standard KTU form has $\ln(p_{H_2}\sqrt{p_{O_2}}/p_{H_2O})$ in the numerator — students often invert it.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **System ≠ Stack.** Always include BoP, BOP, and PCS in a system-level design.
- **Standard potentials to memorise:** $E^{\circ}_{cell} = 1.229$ V, $E_{th,LHV} = 1.253$ V, $E_{th,HHV} = 1.481$ V.
- **Universal gas constant and Faraday's constant:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.
- **Polarisation losses:** activation, ohmic, concentration — listed in that order as $i$ increases.
- **Mass-balance formulas:** $\dot{n}_{H_2} = NI/2F$, $\dot{n}_{O_2} = NI/4F$, $\dot{m}_{H_2O} = N I M_{H_2O}/2F$.
- **Heat load:** $Q = NI(E_{th} - V_{cell})$ — designer must always provide a heat-rejection path.
- **Stoichiometry rules of thumb:** $\zeta_{H_2} = 1.1–1.3$ (with recirculation), $\zeta_{O_2} = 1.5–2.5$.
- **Efficiency chain:** $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{th} \times \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{far} \times \eta_{PCS}$.
- **Coolant sizing:** $ \dot{m}_{cool} = Q_{stack} / (c_p \Delta T)$ — for water, $c_p = 4180$ J/(kg·K).
- **Closed-cycle $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ systems** (submarines, space) avoid parasitic compressor work and produce potable water — a key KTU contrast point.
- **Always convert** to engineering units (L/min, kg/h, kW) at the final step of any calculation.
- **Polarisation curve sketching** is a high-frequency 2-mark sub-question — know the four regions: kinetic, mixed, ohmic, and concentration-limited.


<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen-Air Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT721 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen-Air Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hydrogen-Air System** in the context of fuel cell technology refers to the integrated electrochemical power generation subsystem in which **gaseous hydrogen (H₂)** acts as the fuel (anode reactant) and **filtered, humidified atmospheric air** (≈21% O₂, 78% N₂) acts as the oxidant (cathode reactant). The standard cell potential for the Hydrogen-Air reaction at standard temperature and pressure (**25 °C, 1 atm**) is **E° = 1.229 V**, while the lower heating value (LHV) based efficiency of the theoretical Hydrogen-Air Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) fuel cell is **83%**.

The overall half-cell and full-cell electrochemical reaction is given by:

**Anode (Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction — HOR):**

$$\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \quad E^0_{anode} = 0.00\ \text{V}$$

**Cathode (Oxygen Reduction Reaction — ORR):**

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad E^0_{cathode} = 1.229\ \text{V}$$

**Overall Cell Reaction:**

$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Heat} + \text{Electrical Energy}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Hydrogen-Air System" explicitly refers to the **balance-of-plant (BOP)** components that condition, deliver, and manage the reactants and products. It does **NOT** mean a hydrogen-air *fuel mixture* (which would be dangerously explosive). The air is supplied *separately* to the cathode, and hydrogen to the anode.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Two-Pipe Plumbing System**

Imagine a kitchen sink. You have two separate water supply pipes — one for **hot water** and one for **cold water** — and they only meet at the faucet. The faucet is the **fuel cell membrane (MEA)**. The hot pipe is the **hydrogen supply line**; the cold pipe is the **air supply line**. They are kept strictly isolated until they reach the membrane. Just as the faucet mixes water only at the output point, the MEA mixes ions (H⁺) only at the membrane interface.

Now, why do we need "system-level" components? Because neither the hot water nor the cold water arrives at the right pressure, temperature, or purity straight from the source. The Hydrogen-Air System is essentially the **plumbing, pumps, filters, humidifiers, and heat exchangers** that ensure:

1. The **hydrogen** arrives at the anode at the correct **pressure (typically 1.2–3 atm)**, **purity (>99.99%)**, and **humidity** (for humidified operation).
2. The **air** arrives at the cathode at the correct **flow rate (2–3× stoichiometric)**, filtered of particulates, and humidified to prevent membrane dehydration.
3. The **product water** and unreacted gases are safely exhausted.

The **physical constants** critical to this system are:

- **Faraday's Constant:** **F = 96,485 C/mol** (charge per mole of electrons)
- **Universal Gas Constant:** **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Standard Cell Potential (E°):** **1.229 V** at 25 °C
- **Nernst Open-Circuit Voltage at typical operating conditions (80 °C):** **≈ 1.18 V**

## 1.3 Why Hydrogen-Air (and not Hydrogen-Oxygen)?

| Feature | Hydrogen-Air System | Hydrogen-Oxygen System |
|---|---|---|
| Oxidant Source | Ambient atmosphere (free) | Cryogenic LOX or pressurized tanks (expensive) |
| System Weight | Lighter for portable/automotive | Very heavy (cryogenic tanks) |
| Dilution Effect | Nitrogen dilutes O₂ → activation losses ↑ | Pure O₂ → high ORR kinetics |
| Cost | Low operational cost | High operational cost |
| Application | Automotive, stationary, portable | Space missions, submarines |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 examinations, always remember: the presence of **inert nitrogen (N₂)** in air causes **mass-transport (concentration) losses** and reduces the **partial pressure of O₂** to only **0.21 atm** at the cathode inlet. This is a primary reason for voltage loss in air-fed systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Subsystems of a Hydrogen-Air PEMFC System

A complete Hydrogen-Air PEM Fuel Cell System comprises the following functional subsystems:

### 2.1.1 Hydrogen Subsystem (Anode Loop)
- **Hydrogen Storage**: Compressed gas cylinders (350 bar / 700 bar), liquid hydrogen cryotanks, or metal hydride canisters.
- **Pressure Regulator**: Reduces storage pressure (350–700 bar) to operating pressure (1.2–3 bar gauge).
- **Humidifier / Heat Exchanger**: Pre-conditions H₂ to required relative humidity and temperature.
- **Recirculation Blower / Ejector**: Recycles unreacted H₂ from anode exit back to anode inlet (improves utilization from 70% to 95%+).
- **Purge Valve**: Periodically vents accumulated nitrogen and water from anode dead-end mode.

### 2.1.2 Air Subsystem (Cathode Loop)
- **Air Filter**: Removes dust, pollen, and contaminants.
- **Compressor / Blower**: Pressurizes air to 1.2–3 atm (gauge); major parasitic load (~30% of stack power).
- **Intercooler / Heat Exchanger**: Cools compressed air before humidification.
- **Membrane Humidifier**: Adds moisture to dry compressed air using enthalpy exchange with cathode exhaust.
- **Backpressure Valve**: Maintains cathode pressure setpoint.

### 2.1.3 Water & Thermal Management Subsystem
- **Radiator / Cooling Fan**: Rejects waste heat to ambient.
- **Deionized Water Loop**: Maintains membrane hydration; uses ion-exchange resin to maintain < 1 μS/cm conductivity.
- **Condenser / Separator**: Recovers product water from cathode exhaust.

### 2.1.4 Electronic Control Unit (ECU)
- Monitors cell voltages, temperatures, pressures, and flow rates.
- Executes purges, shut-down sequences, and fault diagnostics.

## 2.2 The Core 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Subsystem

**Why recirculate hydrogen?**
If hydrogen is fed in **dead-end mode** (no outlet), utilization reaches only ~70–85% because concentration gradients build up and inert N₂ (crossed over from cathode) accumulates. A recirculation loop (blower or ejector) raises H₂ utilization to **95–99%**, dramatically improving fuel economy.

**Why humidify the air?**
The PEM (e.g., Nafion™) requires **liquid water contact** to maintain proton conductivity. Proton transport occurs via the **Grotthuss mechanism** (proton hopping) and **vehicular mechanism** (hydronium diffusion), both of which require a fully hydrated membrane. Without humidification, membrane conductivity drops by an order of magnitude and ohmic losses skyrocket.

**Why pressurize the air?**
Increasing cathode pressure raises the **Nernst voltage** (improves thermodynamics) and improves **ORR kinetics** (higher O₂ partial pressure). However, the compressor's parasitic power consumption grows. The net trade-off yields an **optimal operating pressure** in the range **1.2–3 bar (absolute)** for most automotive systems.

**How does the system respond to load changes?**
When load (current) increases:
1. ECU signals **air compressor** to ramp up flow.
2. **H₂ flow** is increased proportionally (or pulsed in dead-end mode).
3. **Coolant pump** speed increases to remove additional waste heat.
4. **Purge cycles** may be triggered more frequently to clear accumulated water/N₂.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E^0_{cell} = E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode} = 1.229\ \text{V}$ | Standard cell potential at 25 °C, 1 atm | Volts (V) | Pure H₂ and O₂ at 1 atm |
| 2 | $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$ | Total cell operating voltage | V (0.5 – 0.85 typical) | Under load |
| 3 | $E_{Nernst} = E^0 + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln(P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{0.5})$ | Nernst reversible voltage (with water as vapor) | V | $P$ in atm; **T in Kelvin** |
| 4 | $E_{Nernst} = 1.229 - 0.85\times10^{-3}(T-298.15) + \frac{RT}{4F}\ln(P_{H_2}^2 P_{O_2})$ | Nernst voltage (with liquid water product) | V | T in Kelvin; P in atm |
| 5 | $\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$ | Activation overpotential (Tafel equation) | V | High current density |
| 6 | $\eta_{ohmic} = i \cdot R_{ASR}$ | Ohmic overpotential | V | Linear in current |
| 7 | $\eta_{conc} = \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\frac{i_L}{i_L - i}\right)$ | Concentration overpotential | V | $i < i_L$ |
| 8 | $i_0 = i_0^{ref}\exp\left[-\frac{E_{act}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{ref}}\right)\right]$ | Exchange current density (Arrhenius) | A/cm² | Reference T = 298 K |
| 9 | $P_{stack} = n_{cells} \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I$ | Stack power output | Watts (W) | $n_{cells}$ = number of cells |
| 10 | $\eta_{util,H_2} = \frac{\dot{n}_{H_2,reacted}}{\dot{n}_{H_2,supplied}} \times 100\%$ | Hydrogen utilization | % | 70–99% in practice |
| 11 | $\lambda_{air} = \frac{\dot{n}_{O_2,supplied}}{\dot{n}_{O_2,consumed}}$ | Stoichiometric ratio (air) | dimensionless | Typically 2.0–3.0 |
| 12 | $P_{parasitic} \propto P_{cathode}^{0.4}$ to $P_{cathode}^{0.6}$ | Compressor parasitic power scaling | W | Increases with pressure |
| 13 | $\dot{n}_{H_2,stoich} = \frac{I}{2F}$ | Molar H₂ consumption rate | mol/s | From Faraday's law |
| 14 | $\dot{n}_{O_2,stoich} = \frac{I}{4F}$ | Molar O₂ consumption rate | mol/s | From Faraday's law |
| 15 | $\dot{V}_{air,min} = \frac{\lambda \cdot I}{4F \cdot 0.21} \times 22.4 \times 10^{-3}$ | Required air flow at STP | m³/s | λ = stoichiometric ratio |
| 16 | $P_{H_2O}^{sat}(T) = \text{function of T (Antoine eq.)}$ | Water vapor saturation pressure | atm | Used in humidifier design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Nernst equation (rows 3 and 4) is used by ECU to **predict** expected open-circuit voltage; deviations from this prediction trigger **fault diagnostics** (membrane drying, fuel starvation, air blockage). The Tafel equation (row 5) is the foundation of **cell-level performance modeling** in tools like MATLAB/Simulink fuel cell libraries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Nernst Voltage for the Hydrogen-Air System

We will derive the reversible cell voltage for the overall reaction:

$$\text{H}_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l \text{ or } g)$$

### Step 1: Start from the Fundamental Thermodynamic Definition

The Gibbs free energy change determines the maximum reversible electrical work:

$$\Delta G = -n F E_{rev}$$

Solving for the reversible voltage:

$$E_{rev} = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF}$$

For our reaction, **n = 2** (two electrons transferred per mole of H₂).

### Step 2: Evaluate ΔG at Standard Conditions

The standard Gibbs free energy change for the hydrogen-oxygen reaction forming **liquid water** at 25 °C (298.15 K) and 1 atm is:

$$\Delta G^0 = -237.2\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

Therefore, the standard cell voltage is:

$$E^0 = -\frac{\Delta G^0}{nF} = -\frac{-237{,}200\ \text{J/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}}$$

$$E^0 = \frac{237{,}200}{192{,}970} = 1.229\ \text{V}$$

This matches the universally quoted value.

### Step 3: Apply the General Nernst Equation

For a generic reaction $aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$, the Nernst equation is:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln(Q)$$

where $Q$ is the reaction quotient. For our system:

$$Q = \frac{a_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}$$

For **liquid water product** (activity = 1):

$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\left(\frac{1}{P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{0.5}}\right) = E^0 + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln(P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{0.5})$$

### Step 4: Account for Temperature Dependence

Using the standard-state relationship with the **standard entropy change** $\Delta S^0$:

$$\Delta G(T) = \Delta H^0 - T\Delta S^0$$

At 298.15 K: $\Delta H^0 = -285.8\ \text{kJ/mol}$, $\Delta S^0 = -163.2\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$.

The temperature-corrected standard voltage:

$$E^0(T) = -\frac{\Delta G(T)}{2F} = -\frac{\Delta H^0}{2F} + \frac{T\Delta S^0}{2F}$$

$$E^0(T) = \frac{285{,}800}{2 \times 96{,}485} - \frac{T \times 163.2}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$E^0(T) = 1.481 - (8.456 \times 10^{-4})\,T \quad [\text{T in K}]$$

At **T = 353.15 K (80 °C)**, typical PEM operating temperature:

$$E^0(353.15) = 1.481 - 8.456 \times 10^{-4} \times 353.15$$

$$E^0(353.15) = 1.481 - 0.2986 = 1.182\ \text{V}$$

### Step 5: Insert Partial Pressures for Hydrogen-Air System

For a **hydrogen-air system** with cathode fed by air at total pressure $P$:

$$P_{H_2} = P \cdot y_{H_2} \quad \text{and} \quad P_{O_2} = 0.21 \cdot P \cdot y_{air,dry}$$

If $P_{total} = 1$ atm (with 50% RH air at 80 °C, the dry-air mole fraction is ≈ 0.94):

$$P_{O_2} = 0.21 \times 1 \times 0.94 \approx 0.197\ \text{atm}$$

Substituting into the Nernst equation:

$$E_{Nernst} = 1.182 + \frac{8.314 \times 353.15}{2 \times 96{,}485}\ln(1.0 \times 0.197^{0.5})$$

$$\frac{8.314 \times 353.15}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 0.01522\ \text{V}$$

$$\ln(\sqrt{0.197}) = \ln(0.4438) = -0.812$$

$$E_{Nernst} = 1.182 + 0.01522 \times (-0.812) = 1.182 - 0.01236 = 1.170\ \text{V}$$

### Step 6: Final Operating Voltage Under Load

The actual terminal voltage is reduced from $E_{Nernst}$ by irreversibilities:

$$V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$

For a typical current density of **0.5 A/cm²** at 80 °C:
- $\eta_{act,cathode} \approx 0.30\ \text{V}$ (dominant, due to sluggish ORR)
- $\eta_{act,anode} \approx 0.01\ \text{V}$ (HOR is fast)
- $\eta_{ohmic} \approx 0.10\ \text{V}$ (from membrane + contact resistance)
- $\eta_{conc} \approx 0.05\ \text{V}$ (small at moderate current)

$$V_{cell} \approx 1.170 - 0.31 - 0.10 - 0.05 = 0.71\ \text{V}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Performance Comparison

| Parameter | Hydrogen-Air (Low Pressure) | Hydrogen-Air (Pressurized 3 atm) | Hydrogen-Oxygen (1 atm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E^0$ (V) | 1.229 | 1.229 | 1.229 |
| $E_{Nernst}$ at 80 °C (V) | 1.170 | **1.221** | **1.232** |
| $V_{cell}$ at 0.5 A/cm² (V) | 0.65 – 0.72 | 0.72 – 0.78 | 0.78 – 0.82 |
| Cathode $P_{O_2}$ (atm) | 0.21 × 0.94 = 0.197 | 0.21 × 2.5 × 0.94 = 0.494 | 1.00 |
| Compressor Parasitic Power | Low | **High (~30% of stack)** | None |
| $\eta_{system}$ (LHV) | 45 – 55% | 50 – 60% | 60 – 70% |
| Application Suitability | Portable, small stationary | **Automotive (most common)** | Aerospace, military |

## 3.3 Stoichiometry & Air Flow Calculation (Worked Example)

**Problem:** A 50-cell PEM fuel cell stack operates at **100 A** current. Calculate:
(a) Molar consumption rates of H₂ and O₂.
(b) Minimum required air flow rate (in L/min at STP) for $\lambda = 2.5$.
(c) Hydrogen flow rate if utilization is 95%.

### Solution:

**(a) Molar Consumption Rates (Faraday's Law):**

$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \frac{I}{2F} = \frac{100}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 5.183 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mol/s}$$

$$\dot{n}_{O_2} = \frac{I}{4F} = \frac{100}{4 \times 96{,}485} = 2.591 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mol/s}$$

**(b) Required Air Flow at λ = 2.5:**

Mole fraction of O₂ in air: $y_{O_2} = 0.21$

$$\dot{n}_{air} = \frac{\lambda \cdot \dot{n}_{O_2}}{y_{O_2}} = \frac{2.5 \times 2.591 \times 10^{-4}}{0.21}$$

$$\dot{n}_{air} = 3.085 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol/s}$$

Converting to volume at STP (using ideal gas, 22.4 L/mol):

$$\dot{V}_{air} = 3.085 \times 10^{-3} \times 22.4 \times 60\ \text{L/min}$$

$$\dot{V}_{air} = 4.146\ \text{L/min} \approx 4.15\ \text{L/min}$$

**(c) Hydrogen Supply at 95% Utilization:**

$$\dot{n}_{H_2,supply} = \frac{\dot{n}_{H_2,reacted}}{\eta_{util}} = \frac{5.183 \times 10^{-4}}{0.95} = 5.456 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mol/s}$$

In standard liters per minute:

$$\dot{V}_{H_2} = 5.456 \times 10^{-4} \times 22.4 \times 60 = 0.733\ \text{L/min (STP)}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hydrogen-Air PEMFC System

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the major functional blocks, fluid streams, and control signals in a complete hydrogen-air PEM fuel cell system. It represents the **BOP (Balance of Plant)** interactions rather than a physical pictorial schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H2tank["H2 STORAGE TANK 350 bar / 700 bar"]
    H2reg["H2 PRESSURE REGULATOR 1 to 3 bar"]
    H2hum["H2 HUMIDIFIER AND HEAT EXCHANGER"]
    H2recirc["H2 RECIRCULATION BLOWER OR EJECTOR"]
    H2purge["H2 PURGE VALVE PERIODIC VENT"]
    AnodeIn["ANODE INLET MANIFOLD"]
    MEA["PEM MEMBRANE ELECTRODE ASSEMBLY MEA"]
    CathodeIn["CATHODE INLET MANIFOLD"]
    AirFilt["AIR FILTER PARTICULATE REMOVAL"]
    AirComp["AIR COMPRESSOR 1 to 3 atm"]
    AirCool["INTERCOOLER AND HEAT EXCHANGER"]
    AirHum["MEMBRANE HUMIDIFIER ENTHALPY EXCHANGE"]
    CathodeExit["CATHODE EXIT WATER VAPOR AND UNUSED AIR"]
    WaterSep["WATER SEPARATOR AND CONDENSER"]
    CoolLoop["COOLANT LOOP DEIONIZED WATER"]
    Rad["RADIATOR AND COOLING FAN"]
    ECU["ELECTRONIC CONTROL UNIT ECU SENSORS AND ACTUATORS"]
    StackOut["DC BUS ELECTRICAL OUTPUT"]
    Load["EXTERNAL LOAD MOTOR OR GRID"]

    H2tank --> H2reg --> H2hum --> AnodeIn
    H2purge --> AnodeIn
    AnodeIn --> MEA
    CathodeIn --> MEA
    AirFilt --> AirComp --> AirCool --> AirHum --> CathodeIn
    MEA --> CathodeExit --> WaterSep
    WaterSep --> CoolLoop --> Rad
    H2recirc --> AnodeIn
    MEA --> StackOut --> Load
    ECU -. control signals .-> AirComp
    ECU -. control signals .-> H2reg
    ECU -. control signals .-> H2purge
    ECU -. feedback .-> StackOut
    ECU -. feedback .-> MEA
    CoolLoop -. thermal coupling .-> MEA
    Rad -. exhaust heat .-> Env["AMBIENT ENVIRONMENT"]
    CathodeExit -. exhaust .-> Env
    H2purge -. vent .-> Env
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Solid arrows represent physical flow of fluids (hydrogen, air, water, electricity, heat). Dashed arrows represent **control signals** (ECU → actuators) and **sensor feedback** (sensors → ECU). Note that the **Coolant Loop** is thermally coupled to the MEA stack (heat removal) and rejects to the radiator.

## 4.2 Polarization Curve Schematic (Mermaid)

The polarization curve shows how cell voltage varies with current density. The three major loss regions are highlighted.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["OPEN CIRCUIT VOLTAGE E_OCV approx 1.17 V"] -->|"Activation Loss Region eta_act"| B["Voltage drops sharply approx 0.85 V at 0.1 A/cm2"]
    B -->|"Ohmic Loss Region linear"| C["Voltage drops linearly approx 0.7 V at 0.5 A/cm2"]
    C -->|"Concentration Loss Region"| D["Voltage drops rapidly approx 0.5 V near limiting current"]
    D --> E["Limiting Current i_L approx 1.5 A/cm2"]

    A:::ocv
    B:::actloss
    C:::ohmicloss
    D:::concloss
    E:::limitcurrent

    classDef ocv fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef actloss fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef ohmicloss fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef concloss fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef limitcurrent fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation Guide:** The "knee" of the polarization curve where ohmic loss dominates is the **most efficient operating region** for steady-state design. Operating in the activation-loss region wastes fuel; operating past the concentration knee causes irreversible membrane damage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three key balance-of-plant (BOP) components in a hydrogen-air PEM fuel cell system and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Air Compressor / Blower:** Pressurizes ambient air to 1.2–3 atm (gauge) for delivery to the cathode. It is the largest parasitic load in the system, consuming 5–30% of stack power depending on pressure ratio.

2. **Hydrogen Humidifier:** Pre-humidifies the dry hydrogen stream before it enters the anode. This prevents membrane dehydration on the anode side and maintains high proton conductivity (Grotthuss hopping mechanism) across the Nafion™ membrane.

3. **Membrane Humidifier (Cathode Side):** Transfers moisture from the hot, humid cathode exhaust to the incoming dry compressed air using a permeable membrane. This is an enthalpy-recuperative device that improves system water balance and reduces external water requirements.

*(Any other correct BOP component with function: recirculation blower, purge valve, deionized water reservoir, radiator, ECU, pressure regulator — accepted.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is the open-circuit voltage of a hydrogen-air PEMFC (≈1.17 V) lower than the thermodynamic standard value (1.229 V)?**

**Model Answer:**

The standard cell voltage of 1.229 V assumes pure H₂ and pure O₂ at 1 atm partial pressure and 25 °C, with **liquid water** as the product. In a real hydrogen-air system, the voltage is lower because:

1. **Dilution of oxidant:** Air is only 21% O₂. The effective partial pressure of O₂ is ≈ 0.197 atm (after subtracting water vapor at 80 °C). This reduces the Nernst voltage.

2. **Operating temperature:** PEM fuel cells operate at 60–80 °C, not 25 °C. The Nernst voltage decreases with temperature at the rate of **−0.85 mV/°C** (per the empirical form), giving ≈ 1.18 V at 80 °C.

3. **Internal crossover / mixed potential:** Small amounts of H₂ cross through the membrane to the cathode and O₂ crosses to the anode, creating a **mixed potential** at each electrode that reduces the OCV below the Nernst value.

4. **Activation overpotential baseline:** Even at zero external current, the sluggish ORR kinetics begin to manifest as a small activation loss.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Part B Module-Internal Choice questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]*

**Q.A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the major components of a hydrogen-air PEM fuel cell system. Discuss the function of each component. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Concise Outline):**

A hydrogen-air PEMFC system consists of four major subsystems:

**I. Hydrogen Delivery Subsystem:**
- Storage tank (compressed gas, 350/700 bar)
- Pressure regulator (reduces to 1.2–3 bar)
- Humidifier (adds water vapor to prevent anode drying)
- Recirculation loop (ejector/blower) and purge valve

**II. Air Delivery Subsystem:**
- Air filter (particulate removal)
- Compressor/blower (raises pressure)
- Intercooler (reduces temperature post-compression)
- Membrane humidifier (enthalpy exchange)
- Backpressure regulator

**III. Thermal & Water Management Subsystem:**
- Coolant pump, radiator, deionized water reservoir
- Water separator/condenser at cathode exhaust

**IV. Control Subsystem (ECU):**
- Sensors (T, P, V, flow), actuators, and the central processor
- Algorithms for purge scheduling, water management, and fault shutdown

**Functions (to elaborate for full marks):**
- Each component is justified by the **system requirement** (e.g., humidifier → membrane conductivity; compressor → cathode pressure for ORR kinetics).

*(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram for visual support.)*

---

**Q.A (b)** A 100-cell PEMFC stack delivers **200 A** at an average cell voltage of **0.70 V**. The system uses **hydrogen at 95% utilization** and **air at 2.5 stoichiometric ratio**. Calculate:
- (i) Hydrogen consumption rate (g/s)
- (ii) Air flow rate at STP (L/min)
- (iii) Stack power output (kW)
- (iv) System efficiency if the LHV of hydrogen is 240 kJ/mol. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $n = 100$ cells, $I = 200$ A, $V_{cell} = 0.70$ V, $\eta_{util,H_2} = 0.95$, $\lambda = 2.5$, LHV = 240 kJ/mol.

**(i) Hydrogen consumption rate:**

Molar consumption per cell stack = $I / (2F) = 200 / (2 \times 96{,}485) = 1.0366 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/s.

Since the 100 cells are in **series**, the **stack current = single-cell current = 200 A** (series connection does not multiply current; it multiplies voltage).

Mass flow rate of H₂ reacted:

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 1.0366 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol/s} \times 2.016\ \text{g/mol}$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 2.090 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{g/s} \approx 2.09\ \text{mg/s}$$

**H₂ supplied** (at 95% utilization):

$$\dot{m}_{H_2,supplied} = \frac{2.090 \times 10^{-3}}{0.95} = 2.20 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{g/s}$$

**(ii) Air flow rate at STP:**

$$\dot{n}_{O_2} = \frac{I}{4F} = \frac{200}{4 \times 96{,}485} = 5.183 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mol/s}$$

$$\dot{n}_{air,supplied} = \frac{\lambda \times \dot{n}_{O_2}}{0.21} = \frac{2.5 \times 5.183 \times 10^{-4}}{0.21}$$

$$\dot{n}_{air,supplied} = 6.170 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol/s}$$

Converting to volume at STP:

$$\dot{V}_{air} = 6.170 \times 10^{-3} \times 22.4\ \text{L/mol} \times 60\ \text{s/min}$$

$$\dot{V}_{air} = 8.293\ \text{L/min} \approx 8.29\ \text{L/min}$$

**(iii) Stack power output:**

$$P_{stack} = n \times V_{cell} \times I = 100 \times 0.70 \times 200 = 14{,}000\ \text{W} = 14\ \text{kW}$$

**(iv) System efficiency (LHV basis):**

$$\dot{n}_{H_2,consumed} = 1.0366 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol/s}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{H_2,LHV} = 1.0366 \times 10^{-3} \times 240{,}000 = 248.8\ \text{J/s} = 248.8\ \text{W (chemical input)}$$

$$\eta_{LHV} = \frac{14{,}000}{248.8} \times 100\% \rightarrow \text{(this is the cell-level efficiency, but more correctly for 100 cells, the chemical input rate is per stack)}$$

**Correcting using per-cell basis:** Chemical power input to the entire stack = $248.8 \times 100 / 1 = $ (each cell consumes 1.0366×10⁻³ mol/s, but 100 cells in series still consume the same molar rate because current is the same).

$$\eta_{LHV} = \frac{P_{stack}}{\dot{n}_{H_2} \times LHV} = \frac{14{,}000}{1.0366 \times 10^{-3} \times 240{,}000} = \frac{14{,}000}{248.8} = 56.27\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Series current confusion:** Many students multiply current by the number of cells when computing molar flow. In a series-connected stack, **molar consumption depends only on total current**, not on cell count. The cell count multiplies **voltage**, not current.
> 2. **Unit error in LHV:** The LHV of H₂ is **240 kJ/mol = 240,000 J/mol**, not 240 J/mol. Always convert to **J** when multiplying by mol/s to get Watts.
> 3. **Air flow at NTP vs STP:** The factor 22.4 L/mol applies at **STP (0 °C, 1 atm)**. If the problem specifies 25 °C, use 24.47 L/mol.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]*

**Q.B (a)** Derive the Nernst equation for a hydrogen-air fuel cell. Show how the cell voltage depends on the partial pressures of H₂ and O₂. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*(See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation. The student should reproduce:)*

**Step 1:** $E^0 = -\Delta G^0 / (nF) = 237{,}200 / (2 \times 96{,}485) = 1.229$ V

**Step 2:** General Nernst: $E = E^0 - (RT/nF)\ln(Q)$

**Step 3:** For the reaction H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O(l): $Q = 1/(P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{0.5})$; activity of liquid water = 1

**Step 4:** Therefore: $E = 1.229 + (RT/2F)\ln(P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{0.5})$

**Step 5:** At 80 °C (353 K): $RT/2F = 0.01522$ V

**Step 6:** For a hydrogen-air system, $P_{O_2} = 0.21 \times P_{cathode,dry} \approx 0.21$ atm at 1 atm total.

**Step 7:** With $P_{H_2} = 1$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 0.21$ atm: $E = 1.229 + 0.01522 \ln(\sqrt{0.21}) = 1.229 - 0.0121 = 1.217$ V at 80 °C (approximate value; precise value including T-correction is ≈ 1.17 V).

**Key conclusions for the examiner:**
- $E$ increases logarithmically with both $P_{H_2}$ and $P_{O_2}$.
- Air dilution causes ~10 mV loss compared to pure O₂.
- Higher temperature decreases the **standard** voltage but increases the **logarithmic** sensitivity to pressure.

---

**Q.B (b)** With the help of a polarization curve diagram, explain the three major voltage losses in a hydrogen-air PEMFC. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The polarization curve plots **cell voltage (V)** vs **current density (A/cm²)** and shows three dominant irreversible losses:

**1. Activation Loss (η_act) — "The Kinetic Barrier":**
- Most pronounced at low current densities (< 0.1 A/cm²).
- Caused by the **sluggish oxygen reduction reaction (ORR)** at the cathode, which has a high activation energy barrier.
- Modeled by the Tafel equation: $\eta_{act} = (RT/\alpha nF)\ln(i/i_0)$.
- The cathode contributes ~90% of the activation loss because ORR kinetics are 100× slower than HOR.

**2. Ohmic Loss (η_ohmic) — "The Resistive Loss":**
- Linear in current density: $\eta_{ohmic} = i \cdot R_{ASR}$.
- Caused by **ionic resistance** of the membrane (dominant) and **electronic resistance** of the electrodes, bipolar plates, and contact interfaces.
- $R_{ASR}$ (area-specific resistance) depends on membrane hydration; dehydration dramatically increases this loss.

**3. Concentration Loss (η_conc) — "The Mass-Transport Limit":**
- Becomes significant at high current densities (> 1 A/cm²).
- Caused by the **depletion of reactants** at the catalyst surface faster than they can be supplied.
- At the cathode, water flooding or insufficient O₂ diffusion through the GDL causes this loss.
- Modeled as: $\eta_{conc} = (RT/nF)\ln[i_L/(i_L - i)]$, where $i_L$ is the limiting current density.

**Diagrammatic Representation (for the exam):**

| Current Region | Dominant Loss | Cause | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 – 0.1 A/cm² | Activation | Sluggish ORR kinetics | Better catalyst (Pt alloys), higher temperature |
| 0.1 – 1.0 A/cm² | Ohmic | Membrane + contact resistance | Thinner membrane, better humidification |
| > 1.0 A/cm² | Concentration | Mass-transport limitation | Better GDL, higher air stoichiometry |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap: Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Standard cell potential** for H₂/O₂ at 25 °C, 1 atm: **E° = 1.229 V** (LHV basis).
- [ ] **Faraday's constant:** F = **96,485 C/mol**; universal gas constant: R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**.
- [ ] **Nernst equation:** $E = 1.229 + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln(P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{0.5})$ for liquid water product.
- [ ] **Nernst equation (vapor product):** $E = 1.229 - 0.85\times10^{-3}(T-298.15) + \frac{RT}{4F}\ln(P_{H_2}^2 P_{O_2})$.
- [ ] **Operating cell voltage** in a real hydrogen-air system: **0.6 – 0.75 V** at typical current densities (0.4 – 0.8 A/cm²).
- [ ] **Air stoichiometric ratio (λ):** typically **2.0 – 3.0**; below 1.5 causes oxygen starvation.
- [ ] **Hydrogen utilization:** typically **70 – 99%** depending on dead-end or flow-through mode.
- [ ] **N₂ dilution** reduces the effective O₂ partial pressure to **0.21 × P_cathode** → ~10–15 mV loss vs pure O₂.
- [ ] **Compressor parasitic load:** the largest BOP loss, scaling as $P_{comp} \propto P^{0.4}$ to $P^{0.6}$ of cathode pressure.
- [ ] **Membrane humidification** is essential for proton conductivity; dehydration increases ohmic loss by **> 5×**.
- [ ] **Three voltage losses:** Activation (low i, ORR-dominated), Ohmic (linear, membrane-dominated), Concentration (high i, mass-transport).
- [ ] **Tafel equation:** $\eta_{act} = (RT/\alpha nF)\ln(i/i_0)$ — the primary tool for modeling activation loss.
- [ ] **Series stacks** multiply **voltage**, not current; molar H₂ consumption is determined by total stack **current only**.
- [ ] **Water management** is dual-purpose: must keep membrane **hydrated** for conductivity but **prevent cathode flooding** to avoid concentration loss.
- [ ] **Purge valves** are mandatory in dead-end anode operation to remove accumulated **N₂ crossover** and water.
- [ ] **System efficiency (LHV)** of typical hydrogen-air automotive PEMFC: **50 – 60%**; with heat recovery (CHP): up to **85 – 90%**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy Tip:** When asked to "explain the hydrogen-air system" in a 14-mark question, always structure your answer as: **(i) overall reaction → (ii) block diagram of components → (iii) detailed function of each BOP component → (iv) Nernst voltage calculation with realistic partial pressures → (v) polarization curve with three losses.** Following this 5-point template consistently will secure full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Cell Systems with Fuel Processor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT721 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Fuel Cell Systems with Fuel Processor # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Fuel Cell System with Fuel Processor** is an integrated electrochemical power generation assembly in which a hydrocarbon-based or oxygenated hydrocarbon fuel (such as natural gas, methane, methanol, ethanol, gasoline, or diesel) is first catalytically converted into a hydrogen-rich reformate stream before being fed into the fuel cell stack. The fuel processor acts as the *upstream chemical engineering interface* between the primary fuel reservoir and the electrochemical membrane-electrode assembly (MEA), while the downstream side handles electrochemical oxidation of hydrogen and parasitic balance-of-plant (BOP) components. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT721 – Module 4):** A standalone fuel cell stack cannot directly consume hydrocarbons. A **fuel processing subsystem** is therefore *mandatory* for any practical system intended to operate on logistic fuels. The processor typically consists of (a) **desulfurization unit**, (b) **reformer** (SR/POX/ATR), (c) **water-gas shift (WGS) reactor**, and (d) **CO clean-up unit** (PROX/methanator/PSA). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **coffee machine (the fuel processor)** that takes coffee beans (hydrocarbon fuel) and grinds + brews them into ready-to-drink espresso (hydrogen-rich gas). Your body (the fuel cell stack) cannot digest whole beans, but it can instantly use the brewed liquid. The coffee machine also discards the grounds (CO, CO₂) along the way. Similarly: - **Hydrocarbon fuel** (methanol, methane, gasoline) = coffee beans - **Fuel processor** = coffee machine - **Hydrogen-rich reformate** = brewed espresso - **CO/CO₂ waste** = spent coffee grounds - **Fuel cell stack** = human body using the espresso for energy > [!NOTE] > **Why Hydrogen is the "Preferred Currency":** Almost all practical low-temperature fuel cells (PEMFC, AFC, PAFC) require H₂ at the anode. Direct hydrocarbon fuel cells (DHFC) exist but suffer from low power density, catalyst poisoning, and poor durability — hence the dominance of indirect (reformer-based) systems. ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Significance | |-----------|---------------|--------------| | Lower Heating Value (LHV) of H₂ | **120 MJ/kg** | Energy currency of the cell | | HHV of H₂ | **142 MJ/kg** | Higher heating value reference | | LHV of Methane (CH₄) | **50 MJ/kg** | Typical reformer feed | | LHV of Methanol (CH₃OH) | **20 MJ/kg** | Easier reforming fuel | | CO tolerance of Pt-PEMFC | **< 10 ppm** | Critical clean-up target | | CO tolerance of Pt-Ru-PEMFC | **< 100 ppm** | Alloy-anode tolerance | | Reformate temperature | **600–800 °C** (SR), **800–1000 °C** (POX) | Operating envelope | | Steam-to-carbon ratio (S/C) | **2.5 – 3.5** | Anti-coking margin | > [!TIP] > Examiners frequently test the difference between **LHV** and **HHV** in fuel processor heat-balance problems. Always check whether the question specifies *latent heat of water vapor* is recovered. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Functional Architecture of a Fuel Processor A complete fuel processor subsystem executes **four sequential chemical transformations** before the gas is qualified for anode admission. The architecture is: 1. **Desulfurization Stage** — Adsorptive or hydro-desulfurization (HDS) on ZnO, activated carbon, or Ni-Mo catalysts to drop sulfur to **< 0.1 ppm** because sulfur permanently poisons reforming and anode catalysts. 2. **Reforming Stage** — Converts the desulfurized fuel into a syngas mixture (H₂ + CO + CO₂ + H₂O + N₂). 3. **Water-Gas Shift (WGS) Stage** — Reduces CO and boosts H₂ via the exothermic shift reaction. 4. **CO Clean-up Stage** — Brings CO below the strict PEMFC tolerance (typically **PROX** or **selective methanation**). ## 2.2 Reforming Chemistries (The Heart of the Processor) ### A. Steam Reforming (SR) — Endothermic $$ \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H_{298}^{0} = +206 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ - **Catalyst:** Ni/Al₂O₃ or Ni/MgO (Rh, Pt for higher activity) - **Operating T:** 700–850 °C - **S/C ratio:** 2.5–3.5 - **H₂ yield:** Highest among all reforming routes (theoretical H₂:CH₄ = 3:1) - **Why 'Steam'?** Extra water suppresses carbon deposition (coking) via the reverse Boudouard reaction. ### B. Partial Oxidation (POX) — Exothermic $$ \text{CH}_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H_{298}^{0} = -36 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ - **Catalyst:** Ni, Rh, Pt on ceramic monoliths - **Operating T:** 800–1000 °C (adiabatic flame) - **H₂:CO ratio:** 2:1 - **Advantage:** Fast startup, no external heat needed. - **Disadvantage:** Lower H₂ yield, hot-spot management critical. ### C. Autothermal Reforming (ATR) — Thermally Neutral ATR is a **hybrid** of SR and POX in a single catalytic bed: $$ \text{CH}_4 + a\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + b\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow c\,\text{CO} + d\,\text{CO}_2 + e\,\text{H}_2 + f\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ The ratio of steam to oxygen is tuned so that the endothermic SR enthalpy exactly balances the exothermic POX enthalpy, making the reactor **thermally self-sustaining**. This is the **industry-preferred technology** for compact automotive fuel processors. ### D. Methanol Steam Reforming (Special Case) $$ \text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H_{298}^{0} = +49 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ - Runs at low temperature (**200–300 °C**) over Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalysts. - Ideal for portable / micro-CHP applications due to liquid handling and low thermal stress. ## 2.3 Water-Gas Shift (WGS) Reactor $$ \text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H_{298}^{0} = -41 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ Two-stage design is industry standard: - **High-Temperature Shift (HTS):** 350–450 °C, Fe₃O₄/Cr₂O₃ catalyst, reduces CO to ~3–5%. - **Low-Temperature Shift (LTS):** 200–250 °C, Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst, reduces CO to ~0.5–1%. > [!IMPORTANT] > WGS is **moderately exothermic and equilibrium-limited**. Lower temperature favors higher CO conversion, but kinetics become sluggish — hence the HTS+LTS cascade. ## 2.4 CO Clean-Up (PROX) $$ \text{CO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H_{298}^{0} = -283 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ - Operates at 80–200 °C over **Au/Fe₂O₃, Pt/CeO₂, or Cu/CeO₂** catalysts. - **Selective** oxidation of CO in the presence of excess H₂ (must not over-oxidize H₂ to water). - Brings CO from ~0.5–1% down to **< 10 ppm**. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Use | |---|-------------------|---------------| | 1 | $\text{H}_2\text{ yield (SR)} = 3 \cdot n_{\text{CH}_4}$ | Max H₂ from steam reforming of CH₄ | | 2 | $\text{H}_2\text{ yield (POX)} = 2 \cdot n_{\text{CH}_4}$ | Max H₂ from partial oxidation | | 3 | $X_{CO} = \dfrac{n_{CO}^{in} - n_{CO}^{out}}{n_{CO}^{in}}$ | WGS / PROX CO conversion | | 4 | $K_{eq,WGS} = \dfrac{[CO_2][H_2]}{[CO][H_2O]}$ | Equilibrium constant (function of T) | | 5 | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{FC} \cdot \eta_{proc} \cdot \eta_{BOP}$ | Overall system efficiency | | 6 | $\text{S/C} = \dfrac{n_{H_2O}^{in}}{n_{C}^{in}}$ | Steam-to-carbon ratio | | 7 | $P_{el} = \dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_2} \cdot \eta_{FC}$ | Electric power output | | 8 | $\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0_{cell}$ | Fundamental cell potential link | | 9 | $\dot{Q}_{proc} = \sum \dot{n}_i \, h_i^{in} - \sum \dot{n}_j \, h_j^{out}$ | Process heat balance | | 10 | $\dot{n}_{H_2}^{out} = \text{C}_{\text{fuel}} \cdot \dot{V}_{\text{fuel}} \cdot Y_{H_2}$ | Reformate hydrogen flow rate | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Stationary CHP (Combined Heat & Power):** Natural gas fuel processor + PAFC/PEMFC stack → 1–250 kW residential/industrial units (e.g., Fuji Electric, Ballard, Plug Power). - **APU (Auxiliary Power Units) for trucks:** Diesel reformer + SOFC for cabin power (Delphi, BMW). - **Portable power:** Methanol reformer + PEMFC for laptops/camping/military (UltraCell, SFC Energy). - **Backup telecom towers:** LPG reformer + PEMFC replacing diesel gensets. - **Hydrogen refueling stations:** On-site reformers producing 99.999% H₂ for FCEV dispensing. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reformate Composition from SR of Methane **Problem Setup:** Methane (CH₄) is fed at 1 mol/s with steam at S/C = 3.0. The reformer operates at **800 °C, 1 atm** with 90% CH₄ conversion. Calculate the dry reformate composition (mole fractions) and H₂ yield. **Step 1 — Define the extent of reaction.** Let $\xi$ be the extent of SR. Initial molar flow: $\dot{n}_{CH_4}^{0} = 1.0$ mol/s, $\dot{n}_{H_2O}^{0} = 3.0$ mol/s (since S/C = 3). After reaction: - $\dot{n}_{CH_4} = 1.0 - \xi$ - $\dot{n}_{H_2O} = 3.0 - \xi$ - $\dot{n}_{CO} = \xi$ - $\dot{n}_{H_2} = 3\xi$ **Step 2 — Apply 90% conversion constraint.** $$ X_{CH_4} = \frac{\xi}{1.0} = 0.90 \Rightarrow \xi = 0.90 \text{ mol/s} $$ **Step 3 — Compute outlet molar flow rates.** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{n}_{CH_4}^{out} &= 1.0 - 0.90 = 0.10 \text{ mol/s} \\ \dot{n}_{H_2O}^{out} &= 3.0 - 0.90 = 2.10 \text{ mol/s} \\ \dot{n}_{CO}^{out} &= 0.90 \text{ mol/s} \\ \dot{n}_{H_2}^{out} &= 3(0.90) = 2.70 \text{ mol/s} \\ \dot{n}_{total}^{wet} &= 0.10 + 2.10 + 0.90 + 2.70 = 5.80 \text{ mol/s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Remove water to obtain *dry* reformate.** $$ \dot{n}_{total}^{dry} = 0.10 + 0.90 + 2.70 = 3.70 \text{ mol/s} $$ **Step 5 — Compute dry mole fractions.** $$ \begin{aligned} y_{H_2} &= \frac{2.70}{3.70} = 0.7297 = 72.97\% \\ y_{CO} &= \frac{0.90}{3.70} = 0.2432 = 24.32\% \\ y_{CH_4} &= \frac{0.10}{3.70} = 0.0270 = 2.70\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — H₂ yield per mole of CH₄ fed.** $$ Y_{H_2} = \frac{2.70 \text{ mol/s}}{1.0 \text{ mol/s}} = 2.70 \text{ mol H}_2 / \text{mol CH}_4 $$ **Comparison:** The theoretical maximum is 3.0 mol H₂/mol CH₄, so the **reactor efficiency** = 2.70/3.00 = **90.0%** (which equals the conversion — true for SR due to 1:3 stoichiometry). ## 3.2 Derivation 2: WGS Equilibrium Calculation **Given:** Inlet to WGS contains $y_{CO} = 0.10$, $y_{H_2O} = 0.40$, $y_{H_2} = 0.30$, $y_{CO_2} = 0.20$ (basis: dry-plus-steam basis, $P = 1$ atm). At $T = 227\,°C$ (500 K), $K_p = 4.886$. Find equilibrium CO conversion. **Step 1 — Initial moles (basis: 1 mol total).** $$ \begin{aligned} n_{CO}^{0} &= 0.10,\; n_{H_2O}^{0} = 0.40 \\ n_{H_2}^{0} &= 0.30,\; n_{CO_2}^{0} = 0.20 \\ n_{total}^{0} &= 1.0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Define extent of WGS = $\xi_e$. Equilibrium moles:** $$ \begin{aligned} n_{CO} &= 0.10 - \xi_e \\ n_{H_2O} &= 0.40 - \xi_e \\ n_{H_2} &= 0.30 + \xi_e \\ n_{CO_2} &= 0.20 + \xi_e \\ n_{total} &= 1.0 \text{ (no change in total moles)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Formulate $K_p$ in terms of partial pressures** (since $P = 1$ atm, $y_i = P_i$): $$ K_p = \frac{(0.20 + \xi_e)(0.30 + \xi_e)}{(0.10 - \xi_e)(0.40 - \xi_e)} = 4.886 $$ **Step 4 — Expand and solve the quadratic.** $$ \begin{aligned} (0.20 + \xi_e)(0.30 + \xi_e) &= 0.06 + 0.50\xi_e + \xi_e^2 \\ (0.10 - \xi_e)(0.40 - \xi_e) &= 0.04 - 0.50\xi_e + \xi_e^2 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \frac{0.06 + 0.50\xi_e + \xi_e^2}{0.04 - 0.50\xi_e + \xi_e^2} = 4.886 $$ Cross-multiplying: $$ 0.06 + 0.50\xi_e + \xi_e^2 = 4.886\,(0.04 - 0.50\xi_e + \xi_e^2) $$ $$ 0.06 + 0.50\xi_e + \xi_e^2 = 0.19544 - 2.443\xi_e + 4.886\xi_e^2 $$ Rearranging to standard form $A\xi_e^2 + B\xi_e + C = 0$: $$ (1 - 4.886)\xi_e^2 + (0.50 + 2.443)\xi_e + (0.06 - 0.19544) = 0 $$ $$ -3.886\,\xi_e^2 + 2.943\,\xi_e - 0.13544 = 0 $$ Or: $$ 3.886\,\xi_e^2 - 2.943\,\xi_e + 0.13544 = 0 $$ **Step 5 — Apply the quadratic formula.** $$ \xi_e = \frac{2.943 \pm \sqrt{(2.943)^2 - 4(3.886)(0.13544)}}{2(3.886)} $$ $$ \xi_e = \frac{2.943 \pm \sqrt{8.661 - 2.105}}{7.772} = \frac{2.943 \pm \sqrt{6.556}}{7.772} $$ $$ \xi_e = \frac{2.943 \pm 2.561}{7.772} $$ The two roots are $\xi_e = 0.7087$ and $\xi_e = 0.0491$. The smaller root is physically admissible (cannot consume more CO than present). Hence: $$ \xi_e = 0.0491 \text{ mol} $$ **Step 6 — Compute CO conversion and exit composition.** $$ X_{CO} = \frac{0.0491}{0.10} = 0.491 = 49.1\% $$ $$ \begin{aligned} y_{CO}^{out} &= 0.10 - 0.0491 = 0.0509 \\ y_{H_2}^{out} &= 0.30 + 0.0491 = 0.3491 \\ y_{H_2O}^{out} &= 0.40 - 0.0491 = 0.3509 \\ y_{CO_2}^{out} &= 0.20 + 0.0491 = 0.2491 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** Even at the favorable low temperature (500 K), a single-stage WGS leaves ~5% CO, which is still **500× too high** for PEMFC. A cascade (HTS → LTS) or a PROX polishing stage is mandatory. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: System Energy Balance for an Integrated FC+Processor **Given:** A 5 kW PEMFC stack operates on reformate. Processor efficiency $\eta_{proc} = 0.80$ (LHV basis). FC electrical efficiency $\eta_{FC} = 0.50$. BOP parasitic fraction $f_{BOP} = 0.10$. Find the LHV of fuel required at the reformer inlet. **Step 1 — Net electric power delivered to load.** $$ P_{net} = P_{el,stack}(1 - f_{BOP}) = P_{el,stack}(0.90) $$ For $P_{net} = 5$ kW: $$ P_{el,stack} = \frac{5}{0.90} = 5.556 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 2 — Hydrogen LHV flow required at stack inlet.** $$ \dot{Q}_{H_2}^{in} = \frac{P_{el,stack}}{\eta_{FC}} = \frac{5.556}{0.50} = 11.11 \text{ kW (LHV)} $$ **Step 3 — Fuel LHV at reformer inlet (after processor losses).** $$ \dot{Q}_{fuel}^{in} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{H_2}^{in}}{\eta_{proc}} = \frac{11.11}{0.80} = 13.89 \text{ kW (LHV)} $$ **Step 4 — Overall system efficiency.** $$ \eta_{sys} = \frac{P_{net}}{\dot{Q}_{fuel}^{in}} = \frac{5.0}{13.89} = 0.360 = 36.0\% $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Trick:** Examiners love to make you compute the *fuel input rate in kg/s* by dividing the LHV flow by the specific LHV (e.g., for methane: 50 MJ/kg). Keep the unit chain consistent — **W = J/s**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Fuel Cell System with Fuel Processor ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Fuel Tank
CH4 or CH3OH] --> B[Desulfurizer
ZnO or HDS] B --> C[Reformer
SR or POX or ATR] W[Water Reservoir] --> C C --> D[WGS Reactor
HTS then LTS] C --> E[Heat Exchanger
Reformer Burner] D --> F[PROX Unit
CO Clean-up] F --> G[PEMFC Stack] G --> H[Power
Conditioning] G --> I[Heat Rejection
Radiator] E --> A H --> J[DC or AC Load] I --> K[CHP Thermal
Loop] classDef proc fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef elec fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 class A,B,C,D,E,F proc class G,H,J elec ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Reformer Variants Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SR[Steam Reformer - Endothermic] SR1[CH4 + 3H2O] --> SR2[CO + 3H2] SR2 -.-> SR3[Heat In 206 kJ/mol] end subgraph POX[Partial Oxidation - Exothermic] POX1[CH4 + 0.5 O2] --> POX2[CO + 2H2] POX2 -.-> POX3[Heat Out 36 kJ/mol] end subgraph ATR[Autothermal Reformer - Neutral] ATR1[CH4 + H2O + O2] --> ATR2[CO + H2 + CO2] ATR2 -.-> ATR3[Heat Balanced] end classDef srf fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#990000,color:#000 classDef poxf fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#006600,color:#000 classDef atrf fill:#d6d6ff,stroke:#000099,color:#000 class SR1,SR2,SR3 srf class POX1,POX2,POX3 poxf class ATR1,ATR2,ATR3 atrf ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Process Control & Heat Integration ```mermaid flowchart TD T1[Reformer Outlet
800 C] --> HX1[Primary Heat Exchanger] HX1 --> T2[Pre-heated Air Inlet] HX1 --> T3[Shift Reactor Inlet
350 C] T3 --> WGS1[HTS 400 C] WGS1 --> WGS2[LTS 220 C] WGS2 --> T4[PROX Inlet
180 C] T4 --> PROX[PROX Reactor] PROX --> T5[Stack Inlet
80 C] STK[PEMFC Stack
60 to 80 C] --> T6[Cathode Exhaust] STK --> T7[Anode Exhaust] T6 --> HX2[Cathode Air HX] T7 --> BURN[Reformer Burner
Recycle] BURN --> T1 HX2 --> RAD[Radiator] classDef hot fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 classDef mid fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600 classDef cold fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 class T1,WGS1 hot class T3,T4,WGS2 mid class T5,T6,T7,STK cold ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **What is meant by "Steam-to-Carbon ratio" (S/C) in a steam reformer? Why is it kept between 2.5 and 3.5 in practice?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - *Definition (1 mark):* S/C ratio is the molar ratio of steam (H₂O) input to carbon atoms (C) in the fuel feed, i.e., $\text{S/C} = \dot{n}_{H_2O}^{in}/\dot{n}_C^{in}$. - *Operational role (1 mark):* It is maintained at 2.5–3.5 to suppress carbon deposition (coking) on the Ni catalyst via the reverse Boudouard and methane-cracking equilibria. - *Process effect (1 mark):* Higher S/C also shifts WGS favorably, increasing H₂ yield and reducing CO in the reformate. The upper bound (~3.5) is set by the cost of vaporizing water and the additional thermal load on the reformer. ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between PROX and selective methanation as CO clean-up methods for PEMFC reformate.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - *PROX (Preferential Oxidation) (1.5 marks):* CO is selectively oxidized to CO₂ by injecting a small amount of O₂ (or air) at 80–200 °C over Au/Fe₂O₃ or Pt/CeO₂ catalysts. Limitation: Risk of H₂ co-oxidation, hot-spot control required. - *Selective Methanation (1.5 marks):* CO is hydrogenated to CH₄ over Ru or Ni catalysts at 200–300 °C. Advantage: No H₂ loss. Limitation: Consumes 3 moles of H₂ per mole of CO, reducing system efficiency. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` **Part (a)** Explain with neat sketches the working of a **Steam Reformer (SR)** and a **Partial Oxidation (POX)** reformer for fuel cell applications. Compare their merits and demerits. **(7 marks)** **Part (b)** A fuel cell system uses methane (LHV = 50 MJ/kg) as feed. The reformer produces **2.5 mol of H₂ per mole of CH₄**, the FC efficiency is **45%**, and the BOP parasitic load is **8%** of stack power. Compute: (i) the overall system efficiency, (ii) the fuel consumption rate (kg/h) for delivering **10 kW** net electric power. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Question A** **Part (a) — 7 marks** **Steam Reformer (SR) – 3.5 marks** - **Reaction:** $CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2$, $\Delta H = +206$ kJ/mol (endothermic). - **Catalyst:** Ni on Al₂O₃ support; operating at 700–850 °C, 1–30 atm. - **Reactor type:** Fixed-bed tubular reformer with catalyst packed inside catalyst-filled tubes; combustion of anode off-gas / fuel on the shell side supplies the endothermic heat. - **Feed preparation:** Water is vaporized and superheated before mixing with desulfurized CH₄ at S/C = 2.5–3.5. - **Merits:** Highest H₂ yield (theoretical 3 mol H₂/mol CH₄), lowest CO in reformate when followed by WGS. - **Demerits:** Endothermic → large external heat input; slow startup; bulky; expensive metallurgy. **Partial Oxidation Reformer (POX) – 3.5 marks** - **Reaction:** $CH_4 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2$, $\Delta H = -36$ kJ/mol (exothermic). - **Catalyst:** Rh, Pt, or Ni on monolith; operates at 800–1000 °C (sometimes non-catalytic thermal POX at >1200 °C). - **Reactor type:** Short contact-time catalytic monolith; no external heating required. - **Feed preparation:** Controlled sub-stoichiometric O₂ (O/C ≈ 0.5–0.6) mixed with preheated fuel. - **Merits:** Fast dynamic response, compact, no steam generation needed, no external heat. - **Demerits:** Lower H₂/CO ratio (2:1), higher outlet temperature requiring heat-resistant materials, risk of carbon deposition at fuel-rich conditions, more CO that burdens the WGS unit. --- **Part (b) — 7 marks** **Given:** H₂ yield = 2.5 mol/mol CH₄, $\eta_{FC} = 0.45$, $f_{BOP} = 0.08$, $P_{net} = 10$ kW, LHV(CH₄) = 50 MJ/kg. **Step 1 — Stack output (gross) required.** $$ P_{stack} = \frac{P_{net}}{1 - f_{BOP}} = \frac{10}{0.92} = 10.870 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 2 — Chemical (LHV) power in H₂ required.** $$ \dot{Q}_{H_2} = \frac{P_{stack}}{\eta_{FC}} = \frac{10.870}{0.45} = 24.155 \text{ kW (LHV of H}_2\text{)} $$ **Step 3 — Molar H₂ flow rate.** Using LHV(H₂) = 240 kJ/mol (≈ 120 MJ/kg ÷ 0.5 kg/mol... actually 120,000 kJ/kg ÷ 2 kg/kmol = 240 kJ/mol): $$ \dot{n}_{H_2} = \frac{24.155 \text{ kW}}{240 \text{ kJ/mol}} = 0.1006 \text{ mol/s} = 362.3 \text{ mol/h} $$ **Step 4 — Molar CH₄ feed rate.** $$ \dot{n}_{CH_4} = \frac{\dot{n}_{H_2}}{2.5} = \frac{362.3}{2.5} = 144.9 \text{ mol/h} $$ **Step 5 — Mass flow rate of CH₄.** Molar mass of CH₄ = 16 g/mol. $$ \dot{m}_{CH_4} = 144.9 \times 16 = 2318.6 \text{ g/h} = 2.319 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Step 6 — Overall system efficiency (LHV basis).** $$ \eta_{sys} = \frac{P_{net}}{\dot{m}_{CH_4} \cdot \text{LHV}_{CH_4}} = \frac{10,000 \text{ W}}{2.319/3600 \text{ kg/s} \times 50 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg}} $$ $$ \eta_{sys} = \frac{10,000}{0.000644 \times 50 \times 10^6} = \frac{10,000}{32,200} = 0.3106 = 31.06\% $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently forget to **subtract the BOP parasitic power** *before* applying the FC efficiency. The correct sequence is: (i) gross-up stack power for BOP losses → (ii) divide by $\eta_{FC}$ → (iii) divide by reformer H₂ yield. Reversing the order yields an efficiency that is ~8% too high, costing 2 marks. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Part (a)** Describe the working principle of an **Autothermal Reformer (ATR)**. Explain why ATR is preferred over SR and POX for compact fuel cell systems. **(7 marks)** **Part (b)** A PEMFC stack operates on **reformate containing 70% H₂, 22% CO₂, 6% N₂, 2% CO** (dry basis, by volume). The stack has **40 cells in series**, each with an active area of **200 cm²**, operating at **0.7 V/cell** and **0.5 A/cm²**. A **PROX unit** upstream reduces CO from 2% to **8 ppm**. Determine: (i) the **stack power output**, (ii) the **CO removal rate in PROX (mol/s)** if feed flow is **10 L/min at STP**. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Question B** **Part (a) — 7 marks** **Working Principle (3 marks):** - ATR combines SR (endothermic) and POX (exothermic) in a single catalytic bed. Steam and a sub-stoichiometric amount of O₂ are co-fed with the desulfurized hydrocarbon. - The catalyst (typically Ni on Al₂O₃ or Rh on CeO₂-ZrO₂) simultaneously promotes both SR and POX pathways. - The O/C and S/C ratios are tuned so that $\Delta H_{SR} + \Delta H_{POX} \approx 0$, making the reactor **thermally neutral** (no external heating or cooling required). **Why ATR is preferred for compact systems (4 marks):** 1. **Compactness:** Single reactor replaces the bulky fired-heated SR furnace; saves space and weight. 2. **Fast startup:** Exothermic POX component ensures rapid light-off; SR provides the H₂-rich output. 3. **High H₂/CO ratio (~2.5–3):** Better than POX (2:1) and adequate for downstream WGS. 4. **Good dynamic response:** Operates robustly under transient loads (essential for vehicular APU). 5. **Lower CO outlet:** Slightly less CO than pure POX, easing the WGS/PROX clean-up burden. 6. **Better carbon management:** Steam suppresses coking, while O₂ ensures complete hydrocarbon conversion. **Disadvantages:** Requires precise air/O₂ dosing; complex controls; risk of hot spots at the inlet where POX dominates. --- **Part (b) — 7 marks** **Given:** $N = 40$ cells, $A = 200$ cm², $V_{cell} = 0.7$ V, $j = 0.5$ A/cm². **Step 1 — Cell current and stack current (series ⇒ same current).** $$ I = j \cdot A = 0.5 \times 200 = 100 \text{ A} $$ **Step 2 — Stack voltage (cells in series).** $$ V_{stack} = N \cdot V_{cell} = 40 \times 0.7 = 28 \text{ V} $$ **Step 3 — Stack power.** $$ P_{stack} = V_{stack} \cdot I = 28 \times 100 = 2800 \text{ W} = 2.8 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 4 — Molar flow rate of dry reformate at STP.** At STP, 1 mol = 22.4 L. $$ \dot{n}_{total} = \frac{10 \text{ L/min}}{22.4 \text{ L/mol}} = 0.4464 \text{ mol/min} = 0.00744 \text{ mol/s} $$ **Step 5 — CO inlet molar flow.** $$ \dot{n}_{CO}^{in} = 0.02 \times 0.00744 = 1.488 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/s} $$ **Step 6 — CO outlet molar flow (8 ppm = 8 × 10⁻⁶ mole fraction).** $$ \dot{n}_{CO}^{out} = 8 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.00744 = 5.95 \times 10^{-8} \text{ mol/s} $$ **Step 7 — CO removal rate in PROX.** $$ \Delta \dot{n}_{CO} = 1.488 \times 10^{-4} - 5.95 \times 10^{-8} \approx 1.487 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/s} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):** Many students confuse **series vs parallel** cell connections when computing stack voltage/current. For a stack with cells in **series**, the current is identical to a single cell's current; the voltage adds. Mark deduction is harsh (–3 marks) if you write $V = V_{cell}/N$ by mistake. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **MUST REMEMBER for KTU Board Exam – Module 4** - ✅ A **fuel processor** is mandatory for any fuel cell system using hydrocarbons (except SOFC and direct-fuel types). - ✅ The four sequential stages: **Desulfurization → Reforming → WGS → PROX/CO clean-up**. - ✅ **Steam Reforming (SR)** = endothermic, max H₂ yield (3:1), needs external heat. - ✅ **Partial Oxidation (POX)** = exothermic, fast startup, lower H₂/CO (2:1). - ✅ **Autothermal Reforming (ATR)** = hybrid of SR + POX, thermally neutral, industry preferred. - ✅ **Methanol SR** runs at only 200–300 °C; ideal for portable fuel cells. - ✅ **WGS** is exothermic, equilibrium-limited → use HTS + LTS cascade. - ✅ **PROX** reduces CO to < 10 ppm (Pt-PEMFC tolerance); alternative is **selective methanation**. - ✅ **S/C ratio** typically 2.5–3.5 to prevent coking. - ✅ **Reformer efficiency** (η_proc) is the *fraction of fuel LHV recovered as H₂ LHV*. - ✅ **Overall system efficiency:** $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{FC} \cdot \eta_{proc} \cdot (1 - f_{BOP})$. - ✅ Heat integration: **anode off-gas** is burned to supply reformer endothermic heat (CHP principle). - ✅ Catalyst poisoning: Sulfur kills **both** reformer and anode — desulfurization is non-negotiable. - ✅ PEMFC CO tolerance: < 10 ppm for pure Pt; < 100 ppm for Pt-Ru anodes. - ✅ LHV of H₂ = **120 MJ/kg**; LHV of CH₄ = **50 MJ/kg**; LHV of CH₃OH = **20 MJ/kg** — commit to memory.

topics.title: System Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT721 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** System Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**System Efficiency** of a fuel cell is defined as the ratio of the net useful electrical (and/or thermal) power output delivered by the complete fuel cell power plant to the total chemical energy input supplied by the fuel (and oxidant). Mathematically, it expresses how effectively the *entire integrated system* — including the electrochemical stack, fuel processing subsystem, thermal management, and balance-of-plant (BOP) auxiliaries — converts the enthalpy of the input fuel into usable work.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Efficiency ($\eta_{sys}$)** is the product of the individual layer efficiencies of a fuel cell power plant:
> $$\eta_{sys} = \eta_{thermo} \cdot \eta_{voltage} \cdot \eta_{faradaic} \cdot \eta_{utilization} \cdot \eta_{parasitic}$$
> where every term on the right-hand side represents a distinct loss mechanism in the *real* engineering plant.

It is critically distinguished from **Stack Efficiency**, which considers only the electrochemical membrane electrode assembly (MEA), and from **Cell Efficiency**, which describes a single repeating unit.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a highly efficient water pump (the **stack**) connected to a network of pipes, valves, and a small electric motor that powers a secondary water-circulator (the **BOP / auxiliaries**). The pump itself may be 90 % efficient at lifting water, but to keep the whole irrigation system alive, you must also spend energy running the circulator, the filter, the pressure gauge electronics, and the leak-detection alarms. The *useful water reaching the field* is less than what the pump alone would suggest — that *gap* is the **system efficiency loss**.

In a fuel cell vehicle, even if the PEMFC stack is 60 % efficient, the air compressor, hydrogen recirculation blower, coolant pump, and DC-DC converter can consume **15–25 %** of the gross stack power, dragging the *system* efficiency down to roughly **40–50 %** at part-load.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** of H₂ = $\mathbf{240 \ MJ/kg}$ ($\mathbf{1.93 \ \times 10^{-4} \ MJ/mol}$ is incorrect — LHV per mole ≈ **241.83 kJ/mol**).
- **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** of H₂ = $\mathbf{285.83 \ MJ/kg}$ (≈ **286 kJ/mol**).
- **Gibbs Free Energy of formation of H₂O(l)** at STP: $\Delta G^0 = -237.13 \ \text{kJ/mol}$.
- **Faraday's Constant** $F = \mathbf{96{,}485 \ C/mol}$.
- **Universal Gas Constant** $R = \mathbf{8.314 \ J/(mol\cdot K)}$.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners commonly test whether students use LHV or HHV in the efficiency expression. For **mobile (vehicle) applications** → use **LHV** because exhaust water leaves as **vapour**. For **stationary CHP** → **HHV** is acceptable since water may condense.
